topics.title: Review of drafting software and plan a single storeyed residential building with flat roof: draw site plan, plan, section and elevation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL408 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Review of drafting software and plan a single storeyed residential building with flat roof: draw site plan, plan, section and elevation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Computer-Aided Drafting (CAD) in Civil Engineering

**Computer-Aided Drafting (CAD)** is the use of computer software to create, modify, analyze, and optimize engineering drawings and designs. In civil engineering, CAD is an indispensable tool for preparing **2D plans**, **3D models**, **structural drawings**, and **Building Information Models (BIM)** with high precision and efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCCEL408):** *Computer-Aided Drafting is the process of using specialized software tools to produce technical drawings that conform to National Building Code of India (NBC) and Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR) standards for residential, commercial, and infrastructure projects.*

## 1.2 Major Drafting Software Used in Civil Engineering Practice

| Software | Developer | Primary Use | File Format |
|----------|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **AutoCAD** | Autodesk | General 2D/3D drafting, plans, sections, elevations | `.dwg`, `.dxf` |
| **Revit** | Autodesk | Building Information Modelling (BIM) | `.rvt`, `.ifc` |
| **SketchUp** | Trimble | Quick 3D conceptual modelling | `.skp` |
| **STAAD.Pro** | Bentley | Structural analysis integrated with drafting | `.std` |
| **ArchiCAD** | Graphisoft | Architectural BIM | `.pln`, `.ifc` |
| **LibreCAD** | Open Source | Free 2D drafting | `.dxf` |

> [!NOTE]
> For **KTU Lab Examinations**, **AutoCAD** is the most commonly expected software. Students are advised to be proficient in AutoCAD 2D drafting commands such as `LINE`, `OFFSET`, `TRIM`, `EXTEND`, `COPY`, `MIRROR`, `ARRAY`, `HATCH`, `DIM`, `MTEXT`, `LAYER`, `BLOCK`, and `INSERT`.

## 1.3 The Four Essential Drawings of a Single-Storeyed Residential Building

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A complete set of architectural drawings for any residential building in India **must** contain the following four primary views as per **NBC 2016** and **KMBR (Kerala Municipal Building Rules)**:

### (a) **Site Plan** (also called *Layout Plan* or *Key Plan*)
A **bird's-eye view** of the entire plot of land showing:
- Plot boundaries and dimensions
- Location of the proposed building within the plot
- Setbacks (front, rear, side) from plot boundaries
- Access road, driveway, and gate
- North direction indicator
- Existing trees, wells, septic tanks, and other site features
- Adjacent plots/roads for context

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Think of the site plan as a **Google Maps satellite view** of your house. It shows *where* the house sits on the land, the shape of the land, the road leading to it, and how much empty space (setback) is left around the house.

### (b) **Plan** (Floor Plan / Ground Floor Plan)
A **horizontal cut** taken at approximately **1.0 m to 1.5 m above the finished floor level (FFL)**, looking downward, showing:
- All rooms with their dimensions and names
- Walls (internal and external) with thickness
- Doors and windows with their openings and swing directions
- Staircase (if any)
- Furniture layout (optional but recommended)
- Plinth level, floor level markings
- Section line indicating where the section is cut

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine you have a **toy house** and you slice off the roof with a giant knife exactly 1 metre above the floor, then look down at the cut. Everything you see is the **plan**.

### (c) **Section** (Cross-Section)
A **vertical cut** through the building (along the section line shown in the plan), showing:
- Foundation details (footing, plinth beam, DPC)
- Floor structure (floor finish, slab)
- Wall construction (with lintel, sun-shade, parapet)
- Roof structure (RCC flat roof with thickness, weathering course)
- Door and window heights and positions
- Floor-to-floor heights, plinth height
- Ground level (GL) and natural ground level (NGL)

**Conceptual Analogy:** If the plan is slicing horizontally and looking down, the **section is slicing vertically and looking sideways** — like cutting a cake in half and seeing the layers of cream and sponge inside.

### (d) **Elevation** (Front/Side/Rear Elevation)
An **orthographic projection** of the external face(s) of the building showing:
- External appearance of the building
- All visible doors, windows, ventilators
- Parapet wall height and design
- Sun-shades, chajjas, balconies
- External finishes, colours (if rendered)
- Plinth height, floor level, roof level markings
- No hidden lines, no internal details

**Conceptual Analogy:** The **elevation is what a person standing on the road would see** when they look at your house from outside. It is essentially a *photograph* drawn with technical accuracy and dimensions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold):**
> - **Plinth height:** Typically **0.45 m to 1.2 m** above ground level (NGL)
> - **Standard room height (floor to ceiling):** **3.0 m** for residential (NBC 2016)
> - **Wall thickness:** **0.20 m** (external), **0.10 m to 0.15 m** (internal partitions)
> - **Standard door size:** **0.9 m × 2.1 m** (main), **0.8 m × 2.1 m** (internal)
> - **Standard window size:** **1.2 m × 1.2 m** or **1.5 m × 1.2 m**
> - **Verandah / corridor width:** Minimum **1.0 m** (residential)
> - **Sun-shade projection:** **0.45 m to 0.6 m**

## 1.4 National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) and KMBR Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Setback Rules (Front, Rear, Side)** are the most-tested KTU concept. For a residential plot in Kerala (as per KMBR), the minimum setbacks depend on plot area, plot width, road width, and number of floors. For a typical **single-storeyed building on a plot of 200–400 m²**:
> - **Front setback:** **3.0 m** (minimum)
> - **Rear setback:** **1.5 m to 2.0 m**
> - **Side setback:** **1.0 m to 1.5 m** (each side)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Drawing Scales as Per Indian Standard (IS 962:1989)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scale** is the ratio of the *drawing size on paper* to the *actual size in reality*. Every architectural drawing MUST have its scale mentioned in the title block.

| Drawing Type | Standard Scale (Metric) | Use |
|--------------|------------------------|-----|
| **Site Plan** | **1:200** (small plots) or **1:500** (large plots) | Layout planning |
| **Floor Plan** | **1:50** or **1:100** | Architectural planning |
| **Elevation** | **1:50** or **1:100** | External view |
| **Section** | **1:50** or **1:100** | Internal vertical details |
| **Detail Drawings** | **1:5, 1:10, 1:20** | Staircase, door, window details |

> [!NOTE]
> A scale of **1:50** means **1 unit on paper = 50 units in reality**. So a wall of actual length **5.0 m** will be drawn as **5.0 m ÷ 50 = 0.10 m = 10 cm** on the A1/A2 sheet.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet for Building Planning Calculations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are the most-tested numerical concepts in KTU lab/model exams for Civil Engineering Modelling.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard Value | Unit |
|---|-----------|--------------------------|------|
| 1 | **Plot Coverage (Plinth Area Ratio)** | $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{A_{\text{plinth}}}{A_{\text{plot}}} \times 100$ | **%** |
| 2 | **Floor Area Ratio (FAR / FSI)** | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{A_{\text{total floor}}}{A_{\text{plot}}}$ | **unitless** |
| 3 | **Built-up Area** | $A_{\text{built-up}} = A_{\text{plinth}} + A_{\text{verandah}} + A_{\text{porch}}$ | **m²** |
| 4 | **Plinth Area** | $A_{\text{plinth}} = L_{\text{external}} \times B_{\text{external}}$ | **m²** |
| 5 | **Carpet Area** | $A_{\text{carpet}} = A_{\text{built-up}} - A_{\text{walls}}$ | **m²** |
| 6 | **Setback Compliance** | $\text{Required setback} \leq \text{Provided setback}$ | **m** |
| 7 | **Maximum Plinth Coverage** (KMBR) | For plots $\leq$ 200 m²: **75%**; 200–500 m²: **65%**; **>500 m²: 50%** | **%** |
| 8 | **FAR (Kerala Residential)** | Typically **1.5 to 2.0** for residential | **unitless** |
| 9 | **Wall Length on Drawing** | $L_{\text{drawing}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{actual}}}{\text{Scale factor}}$ | **cm or mm** |
| 10 | **Height of Building** (KMBR) | $\leq$ **7.5 m** (no lift) for 2 floors | **m** |
| 11 | **Staircase Rise & Tread** | Rise = **0.15 m**, Tread = **0.25 m to 0.30 m** | **m** |
| 12 | **Staircase Width (residential)** | $\geq$ **1.0 m** | **m** |
| 13 | **Head Room (clearance)** | $\geq$ **2.2 m** (NBC) | **m** |
| 14 | **Parapet Height (flat roof)** | $\geq$ **0.45 m** (min) to **1.2 m** (max) | **m** |
| 15 | **Number of Riser Formula** | $n = \dfrac{\text{Floor-to-floor height}}{\text{Riser height}}$ | **integer** |
| 16 | **Sum of Riser and Tread (2R + T)** | $2R + T \approx 0.60$ m to **0.64 m** | **m** |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing these formulas in your answer sheets, **always** use the backslash `\\` for line breaks in the aligned environment, and **never** use the vertical pipe `|` inside table cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

## 2.3 Key Indian Standards (IS Codes) for Building Drawings

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently ask "As per which IS code…?" type questions. Memorize the following:

| IS Code | Title | Relevance |
|---------|-------|-----------|
| **IS 962:1989** | Code of Practice for Architectural and Building Drawings | Scales, line types, symbols |
| **IS 10711:2001** | General Requirements of Doors, Windows, Ventilators | Door/window symbols |
| **IS 795:1982** | Code of Practice for Structural Design of Flat Roofs | Flat roof design |
| **NBC 2016** | National Building Code of India | Overall standards |
| **KMBR / KPBR** | Kerala Municipal Building Rules | Kerala-specific setbacks |

## 2.4 Line Types & Symbols in Architectural Drawings

| Line Type | Lineweight | Purpose |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **Object/Visible Line** | Thick (0.5–0.7 mm) | Visible edges of walls, doors, windows |
| **Hidden/Dashed Line** | Thin (0.25 mm) | Hidden edges (in elevation — rarely used) |
| **Centre Line** | Thin (0.25 mm), chain-dashed | Centre of windows, doors, columns |
| **Dimension Line** | Thin (0.25 mm) | Dimensions with arrowheads |
| **Leader/Extension Line** | Thin (0.25 mm) | Extensions from objects to dimensions |
| **Section Cutting Line** | Thick chain-dashed | Indicates where section is taken |
| **Break Line** | Thin, freehand or zigzag | To shorten long drawings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symbol for door swing:** An **arc of 90°** with a small straight line at the hinge.  
> **Symbol for window:** Two parallel lines (representing frame and shutter) with a centre line.  
> **Symbol for stair direction (Up/Down):** A **triangle/arrow** pointing in the direction of ascent.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The skills developed in this experiment are foundational for:
> - **Site engineers** who must read and interpret drawings at construction sites
> - **Architectural draughtsmen** who prepare municipal approval drawings
> - **Quantity surveyors** who extract measurements from drawings for BoQ (Bill of Quantities)
> - **BIM modellers** who work on advanced 3D coordination (clash detection, MEP integration)
> - **Structural engineers** who overlay structural drawings on architectural plans

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedural Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the **exhaustive, step-by-step workflow** for executing Module Experiment 2 in AutoCAD (or equivalent software). Every step is written out explicitly. **No step is skipped.**

## 3.1 Pre-Drafting Stage: Information Gathering

**Step 1: Obtain the Building Requirements**
- Plot dimensions (e.g., 15 m × 20 m = 300 m²)
- Number of rooms required: 2 Bedrooms, 1 Hall, 1 Kitchen, 1 Dining, 2 Toilets, 1 Verandah
- Orientation: Typically **north-facing** main door, **south-facing** windows for tropical Kerala climate
- Vaastu considerations (optional but expected in Kerala residential design)

**Step 2: Verify KMBR Compliance**
- Calculate the maximum permissible plinth coverage
- Verify proposed setbacks
- Calculate allowable FAR

**Step 3: Sketch a Hand-Drawn Concept Plan**
- On graph paper, roughly place rooms to ensure circulation and functionality
- Finalize wall positions and dimensions

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Single-Storeyed Building on 15 m × 20 m Plot

> [!NOTE]
> **Given Data:**
> - Plot size: **15 m × 20 m** = **300 m²**
> - Building type: Single-storeyed, flat-roof residential
> - Front setback: **3.0 m**, Rear setback: **2.0 m**, Side setbacks: **1.5 m** each
> - Wall thickness: **0.23 m** (external) and **0.115 m** (internal)
> - Scale of drawing: **1:50**

### 3.2.1 Derivation: Maximum Permissible Plinth Coverage

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Plot Area} &= L_{\text{plot}} \times B_{\text{plot}} \\
&= 15\,\text{m} \times 20\,\text{m} \\
&= 300\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Since the plot area is between 200 m² and 500 m², as per KMBR, the **maximum permissible plinth coverage = 65%**.

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{plinth, max}} &= 0.65 \times A_{\text{plot}} \\
&= 0.65 \times 300\,\text{m}^2 \\
&= 195\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.2 Derivation: Available Buildable Footprint After Setbacks

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{buildable}} &= L_{\text{plot}} - (\text{Front setback} + \text{Rear setback}) \\
&= 15\,\text{m} - (3.0\,\text{m} + 2.0\,\text{m}) \\
&= 10\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{\text{buildable}} &= B_{\text{plot}} - (\text{Left setback} + \text{Right setback}) \\
&= 20\,\text{m} - (1.5\,\text{m} + 1.5\,\text{m}) \\
&= 17\,\text{m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{buildable, max}} &= 10\,\text{m} \times 17\,\text{m} \\
&= 170\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Since **170 m² < 195 m²**, the building can occupy the full buildable footprint.

### 3.2.3 Derivation: Drawing Length on Paper at 1:50 Scale

For a wall of actual length **5.0 m**:

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{drawing}} &= \frac{L_{\text{actual}}}{\text{Scale factor}} \\
&= \frac{5.0\,\text{m}}{50} \\
&= 0.10\,\text{m} \\
&= 10\,\text{cm}
\end{aligned}$$

In AutoCAD, since the software uses **1 unit = 1 mm by default**, this wall should be drawn as:

$$L_{\text{AutoCAD}} = 5.0 \times 1000 = 5000 \text{ units (mm)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always set the **insertion units to millimetres** and **drawing units to millimetres** in AutoCAD before starting, to avoid scale confusion.

### 3.2.4 Derivation: Number of Stair Risers (If Provided)

Given:
- Floor-to-floor height = **3.0 m** (assuming ground to roof slab = 3.0 m)
- Riser height = **0.15 m**

$$\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{riser}} &= \frac{\text{Floor-to-floor height}}{\text{Riser height}} \\
&= \frac{3.0\,\text{m}}{0.15\,\text{m}} \\
&= 20 \text{ risers}
\end{aligned}$$

For 20 risers, the **number of treads = 19** (since the last riser is at the landing).

Using the **2R + T rule** (Blondel's formula):

$$\begin{aligned}
2R + T &= 2(0.15) + 0.30 \\
&= 0.30 + 0.30 \\
&= 0.60\,\text{m} \;\; \checkmark \text{(within 0.60 to 0.64 m range)}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2.5 Derivation: Built-up Area and Carpet Area

Assume the building footprint is **10 m × 9 m** (leaving 8 m of buildable width for a future scope or garden):

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{plinth, actual}} &= 10\,\text{m} \times 9\,\text{m} \\
&= 90\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{walls}} &= \text{External wall area} + \text{Internal wall area} \\
&\approx (2 \times (10 + 9) \times 0.23) + (10 \times 0.115) \\
&\approx 8.74 + 1.15 \\
&\approx 9.89\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{carpet}} &= A_{\text{plinth}} - A_{\text{walls}} \\
&= 90 - 9.89 \\
&\approx 80.11\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Drafting Procedure in AutoCAD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **complete workflow** for drafting the four drawings. Follow this sequence exactly in lab examinations.

### **Phase 1: Setup**
1. Open AutoCAD → New Drawing → Choose **acadiso.dwt** (metric template)
2. Set drawing units: Type `UNITS` → Select **Millimetres** → OK
3. Set drawing limits: `LIMITS` → `0,0` → `29700,21000` (for A1 sheet) or `42000,29700` (for A0)
4. Set grid spacing: `DSETTINGS` → Snap **250 mm**, Grid **1000 mm**
5. Create **layers**: `WALL-EXT`, `WALL-INT`, `DOOR`, `WINDOW`, `DIM`, `TEXT`, `HATCH`, `TITLE`, `SITE`
6. Assign **colours and lineweights** to each layer

### **Phase 2: Drafting the Floor Plan (Most Time-Consuming)**
1. Draw the **outer wall rectangle** using `RECTANGLE` command
   - Dimensions: 10000 mm × 9000 mm (for 10 m × 9 m)
2. **Offset** the rectangle inwards by **230 mm** to get the inner wall line
3. Use `MLINE` or draw inner partition walls
4. Place **door openings**: Use `OFFSET` to mark the door jamb position, then `TRIM` to cut the wall
5. Insert **door blocks** (use `INSERT` → Block from Design Center)
6. Place **window openings** similarly
7. Insert **window blocks** with proper sill height (typically 900 mm from FFL)
8. Add **furniture** (optional) using `INSERT` of standard blocks
9. Mark the **section line** (e.g., A-A) on the plan
10. Add **room names** with `MTEXT` and **dimensions** with `DIM`

### **Phase 3: Drafting the Section**
1. Identify the **section line** (e.g., A-A) on the plan
2. Project all elements along the section line vertically
3. Draw **ground level line** (GL)
4. Draw **plinth level** at **+0.45 m to +0.6 m**
5. Draw **floor level** at **+0.0 m** (FFL)
6. Draw **roof slab** at **+3.0 m** (slab thickness **100 mm to 150 mm**)
7. Draw **parapet** at **+3.45 m to +3.6 m** (450 mm to 600 mm above roof)
8. Add **DPC** (Damp Proof Course) line at plinth level (typically 50 mm thick)
9. Show **foundation** with footing (if detail required)
10. Hatch the **cut portions** (walls, ground) with `HATCH` command

### **Phase 4: Drafting the Elevation**
1. Project the **front wall** line vertically from the plan
2. Draw **plinth** to **+0.45 m**
3. Draw **wall** to **+3.0 m**
4. Draw **parapet** to **+3.45 m** (with coping projection)
5. Place **doors and windows** with exact positions from the plan
6. Draw **sun-shades** (chajjas) projecting **450 mm to 600 mm**
7. Add external **finishes** (textures/hatching for plaster)
8. Add **down-pipe** location
9. Show **name board / house number** (optional)

### **Phase 5: Drafting the Site Plan**
1. Draw the **plot boundary** as a thick line
2. Mark all **plot dimensions** (15 m × 20 m) and **north direction**
3. Show the **proposed building** as a simple rectangle (footprint) in the correct position with setbacks
4. Show **driveway / pathway** to the main door
5. Show **gate position**
6. Mark **setback dimensions** explicitly
7. Add **adjacent features** (road on one side, neighbour plot indication)
8. Show **proposed well / borewell / septic tank** if any

### **Phase 6: Annotation and Title Block**
1. Add **title block** in the bottom-right corner with:
   - Project name
   - Drawing title
   - Scale
   - Date
   - Drawn by, Checked by
   - Sheet number
2. Add **north symbol** on site plan
3. Add **scale bar** on each drawing
4. Add **revision table** (optional)

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Errors vs. Best Practices

| # | Common Student Error | Correct Best Practice | Marks Lost |
|---|----------------------|------------------------|------------|
| 1 | Wrong scale (e.g., drawing 10 m wall as 10 cm) | Use scale 1:50 → 10 m = 20 cm | **2 marks** |
| 2 | Missing setback dimensions on site plan | Always dimension setbacks with arrows | **2 marks** |
| 3 | Section without hatching | Hatch all cut portions (walls, ground) | **3 marks** |
| 4 | Door swing not shown | Always draw 90° arc for swing | **2 marks** |
| 5 | Window sill height not marked | Mark "Sill at +0.9 m" or show in section | **1 mark** |
| 6 | No section cutting line on plan | Draw chain-dashed line with arrows | **2 marks** |
| 7 | Title block incomplete | All 6 fields must be filled | **2 marks** |
| 8 | Line weights uniform (all thin) | Walls = thick (0.5 mm), dimensions = thin (0.25 mm) | **2 marks** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

## 4.1 Workflow Diagram: Complete Drafting Process

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram represents the **end-to-end workflow** of Module Experiment 2, from initial data collection to final plot/print.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Plot Data] --> B[Verify KMBR Rules and Setbacks]
    B --> C[Calculate Plinth Coverage and FAR]
    C --> D[Sketch Concept Plan on Graph Paper]
    D --> E[Launch AutoCAD and Set Units to mm]
    E --> F[Create Layers: WALL-EXT, WALL-INT, DOOR, WINDOW, DIM, TEXT, HATCH]
    F --> G[Draft Floor Plan with Outer Walls]
    G --> H[Add Internal Partition Walls]
    H --> I[Insert Doors and Windows]
    I --> J[Add Room Labels and Dimensions]
    J --> K[Mark Section Line A-A on Plan]
    K --> L[Draft Section along A-A Line]
    L --> M[Add Foundation, Plinth, Floor, Roof, Parapet]
    M --> N[Hatch All Cut Portions]
    N --> O[Draft Elevation Projected from Plan]
    O --> P[Add Doors, Windows, Sun-shades, Parapet]
    P --> Q[Draft Site Plan with Plot Boundary]
    Q --> R[Mark Setbacks, North Direction, Road, Gate]
    R --> S[Create Title Block with Scale and Details]
    S --> T[Add Scale Bar and North Symbol]
    T --> U[Plot to PDF or Print on Sheet]
    U --> V[End: Final Drawing Set Ready]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Layers of a Residential Drawing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1["Foundation Layer"]
        F1[Footing and Plinth Beam]
        F2[DPC at Plinth Level]
    end

    subgraph L2["Substructure Layer"]
        S1[Plinth Wall 0.45 m to 1.2 m]
        S2[Backfill and PCC]
    end

    subgraph L3["Superstructure Layer"]
        SU1[External Walls 0.23 m]
        SU2[Internal Walls 0.115 m]
        SU3[Lintel Beam at 2.1 m]
    end

    subgraph L4["Roof Layer"]
        R1[RCC Slab 100 mm to 150 mm]
        R2[Waterproofing Course]
        R3[Parapet Wall 0.45 m]
    end

    subgraph L5["Openings Layer"]
        O1[Doors 0.9 m x 2.1 m]
        O2[Windows 1.2 m x 1.2 m]
        O3[Ventilators 0.6 m x 0.6 m]
    end

    subgraph L6["Finishes Layer"]
        FN1[Plaster 12 mm to 20 mm]
        FN2[Paint or Distemper]
        FN3[Floor Finish 50 mm]
    end

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4
    L3 --> L5
    L1 --> L6
    L2 --> L6
    L3 --> L6
    L4 --> L6
```

## 4.3 Drawing Relationship Diagram: How the Four Views Interconnect

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SP[Site Plan: Top View of Plot] -->|locates the building| FP
    FP[Floor Plan: Horizontal Section at +1.0 m] -->|vertical projection| EL
    FP[Floor Plan] -->|section line A-A| SEC
    SEC[Section A-A: Vertical Cut] -->|shares heights with| EL
    EL[Front Elevation: External View from South]
    EL2[Side Elevation: External View from East]

    FP -.->|used for room layout| DET[Door and Window Schedule]
    SEC -.->|used for material spec| DET
    SP -.->|used for area calc| QT[Quantity Take-off]
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix: Drawing Type vs. Software Command

| Drawing | Primary AutoCAD Commands | Output Type |
|---------|--------------------------|-------------|
| Site Plan | LINE, PLINE, OFFSET, HATCH, MTEXT, DIM, INSERT (north symbol block) | 2D Plan |
| Floor Plan | RECTANGLE, OFFSET, TRIM, EXTEND, COPY, MIRROR, BLOCK, HATCH | 2D Plan |
| Section | LINE, PLINE, OFFSET, HATCH (ANSI31), ARRAY, MTEXT, DIM | 2D Section |
| Elevation | LINE, COPY (mirror from plan), OFFSET, INSERT (door/window blocks) | 2D Elevation |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Q: Define a "site plan" as per IS 962:1989. List any four components that must be shown on it.**

**Model Answer:**
A **site plan** is a drawing that shows the proposed building, the boundaries of the plot, and the surrounding features as viewed from above (bird's-eye view). It is drawn to a scale of **1:200** or **1:500** as per IS 962:1989.

The four essential components are:
1. **Plot boundary** with overall dimensions
2. **Location of the proposed building** within the plot with setback dimensions
3. **North direction** indicator (compass rose)
4. **Access road** and **gate position**

> [!NOTE]
> For full marks (3), students must mention the **scale (1:200 or 1:500)**, the **IS code reference**, and **at least 4 components**.

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between a "plan" and a "section" of a building with respect to (i) cutting plane orientation, (ii) view direction, and (iii) information shown.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Plan | Section |
|---------|------|---------|
| **(i) Cutting plane** | Horizontal (typically 1.0 m above FFL) | Vertical (along section line A-A) |
| **(ii) View direction** | Looking *downward* | Looking *sideways* (perpendicular to cut) |
| **(iii) Information** | Room layout, doors, windows, furniture | Foundation, plinth, floor, roof, parapet, floor-to-floor heights |

(Each correct row carries 1 mark × 3 rows = **3 marks**)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **A residential building is proposed on a rectangular plot of size 18 m × 24 m. The front setback is 3.0 m, rear setback is 2.0 m, and side setbacks are 1.5 m each. The wall thicknesses are 230 mm (external) and 115 mm (internal). The building has a flat RCC roof. Using a drawing scale of 1:50:**
>
> **(a)** Calculate the maximum permissible plinth coverage and the buildable footprint. Verify whether the proposed dimensions comply with KMBR. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** List the step-by-step procedure to draft the **floor plan, section, and front elevation** in AutoCAD. Include the software commands used. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Calculate Plot Area** *(1 mark)*

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{plot}} &= 18\,\text{m} \times 24\,\text{m} \\
&= 432\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Identify Applicable Plinth Coverage Limit** *(1 mark)*
Since 200 m² < 432 m² < 500 m², the KMBR maximum plinth coverage = **65%**.

**Step 3: Calculate Maximum Permissible Plinth Area** *(1 mark)*

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{plinth, max}} &= 0.65 \times 432\,\text{m}^2 \\
&= 280.8\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Buildable Footprint** *(2 marks)*

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{buildable}} &= 18 - (3.0 + 2.0) = 13\,\text{m} \\
B_{\text{buildable}} &= 24 - (1.5 + 1.5) = 21\,\text{m} \\
A_{\text{buildable, max}} &= 13 \times 21 = 273\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Compliance Check** *(2 marks)*

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{buildable, max}} &= 273\,\text{m}^2 < A_{\text{plinth, max}} = 280.8\,\text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

✅ **The proposed building dimensions comply with KMBR.**  
The actual building can be designed up to a footprint of **13 m × 21 m = 273 m²** (≈63.2% coverage).

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: AutoCAD Setup** *(2 marks)*
- Launch AutoCAD → Open `acadiso.dwt` template
- Set units: `UNITS` → Millimetres
- Set limits: `LIMITS` → `0,0` → `59400,42000` (A1 sheet)
- Create layers: `WALL-EXT`, `WALL-INT`, `DOOR`, `WINDOW`, `DIM`, `TEXT`, `HATCH`, `SITE`, `ELEV`

**Step 2: Floor Plan Drafting** *(2 marks)*
- Use `RECTANGLE` to draw outer wall (13000 mm × 21000 mm)
- `OFFSET` inward by **230 mm** to get inner wall line
- `OFFSET` inward by **115 mm** for internal partitions
- `TRIM` wall at door/window openings
- `INSERT` door and window blocks
- Add room labels with `MTEXT` and dimensions with `DIM`

**Step 3: Section Drafting** *(2 marks)*
- Mark section line A-A on plan
- Use `LINE` and `PLINE` to project elements vertically
- Draw levels: GL, FFL, plinth, roof slab, parapet
- `HATCH` cut portions with `ANSI31` pattern
- Add `DIM` for heights and `MTEXT` for labels

**Step 4: Elevation Drafting** *(1 mark)*
- Use `COPY` with base point to transfer wall lines from plan
- Draw doors, windows, sun-shades, parapet
- Add `HATCH` for external plaster texture

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **four standard views** (site plan, plan, section, elevation) of a single-storeyed residential building. Mention the scale, cutting plane, and key features of each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** As per **KMBR (Kerala Municipal Building Rules)** and **NBC 2016**, what are the standard values of: plinth height, room height, wall thickness, door size, window size, sun-shade projection, and parapet height for a single-storeyed residential building? Tabulate your answer. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Site Plan (1.5 marks):**
- **Cutting plane:** None (top view of the plot)
- **Scale:** **1:200** (small plot) or **1:500** (large plot)
- **Key features:** Plot boundary, building footprint, setbacks, north direction, road, gate, adjacent features

**Floor Plan (1.5 marks):**
- **Cutting plane:** Horizontal at **+1.0 m** above FFL
- **Scale:** **1:50** or **1:100**
- **Key features:** Room layout, walls, doors (with swing), windows, furniture, section line

**Section (2 marks):**
- **Cutting plane:** Vertical along section line A-A
- **Scale:** **1:50** or **1:100**
- **Key features:** Foundation, plinth, DPC, walls, floor, lintel, roof slab, parapet, GL, NGL

**Elevation (2 marks):**
- **Cutting plane:** None (orthographic projection)
- **Scale:** **1:50** or **1:100**
- **Key features:** External wall, doors, windows, sun-shades, parapet coping, finishes

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Table of Standard Dimensions:**

| # | Element | Standard Value (m) | Reference |
|---|---------|-------------------|-----------|
| 1 | Plinth height | **0.45 to 1.20** | NBC 2016 |
| 2 | Room height (floor to ceiling) | **3.00** | NBC 2016 |
| 3 | External wall thickness | **0.23** (one brick) | KMBR |
| 4 | Internal wall thickness | **0.115** (half brick) | KMBR |
| 5 | Main door size | **0.90 × 2.10** | IS 10711 |
| 6 | Internal door size | **0.80 × 2.10** | IS 10711 |
| 7 | Window size (typical) | **1.20 × 1.20** | IS 10711 |
| 8 | Ventilator size | **0.60 × 0.60** | IS 10711 |
| 9 | Sun-shade projection | **0.45 to 0.60** | Conventional |
| 10 | Parapet height (min) | **0.45** | NBC 2016 |
| 11 | Window sill height | **0.90** above FFL | Conventional |
| 12 | Lintel height | **2.10** above FFL | Conventional |
| 13 | Head room (clearance) | $\geq$ **2.20** | NBC 2016 |
| 14 | Staircase width | $\geq$ **1.00** | NBC 2016 |

(Each correct row: 0.5 marks × 14 = **7 marks**)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Areas (Examiner's Eye-View):**
> 1. **No scale mentioned** in the title block → **−2 marks** per drawing
> 2. **Setback dimensions missing** on the site plan → **−2 marks**
> 3. **Section cutting line (A-A)** not marked on the floor plan → **−2 marks**
> 4. **Hatching not done** on cut portions of the section → **−3 marks**
> 5. **Door swing** direction not indicated with an arc → **−1 mark per door**
> 6. **Wall thickness** drawn as a single line instead of two parallel lines (outer and inner face) → **−2 marks**
> 7. **North direction** symbol missing or incorrectly oriented on the site plan → **−2 marks**
> 8. **Title block** incomplete (missing project name, scale, or date) → **−1 to −2 marks**
> 9. **Line weights** uniform throughout (walls and dimensions in the same thickness) → **−2 marks**
> 10. **Mislabelled rooms** (e.g., "Bed Room" instead of "Bedroom 1" / "Bedroom 2") → **−1 mark**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets for Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep:**

- ✅ **CAD** = Computer-Aided Drafting; **BIM** = Building Information Modelling
- ✅ **AutoCAD** is the most-expected software for KTU PCCEL408 lab exams
- ✅ Four mandatory drawings: **Site Plan, Plan, Section, Elevation**
- ✅ **Site Plan** = top view of plot → scale **1:200 or 1:500**
- ✅ **Plan** = horizontal cut at **+1.0 m** above FFL → scale **1:50 or 1:100**
- ✅ **Section** = vertical cut along A-A → shows foundation, plinth, roof
- ✅ **Elevation** = external orthographic view → no hidden lines
- ✅ **Plinth height:** 0.45 m to 1.2 m; **Room height:** 3.0 m
- ✅ **External wall:** 0.23 m; **Internal wall:** 0.115 m
- ✅ **Door:** 0.9 m × 2.1 m (main); **Window:** 1.2 m × 1.2 m
- ✅ **Parapet:** minimum 0.45 m; **Sun-shade:** 0.45 to 0.6 m projection
- ✅ **Head room:** $\geq$ 2.2 m; **Stair width:** $\geq$ 1.0 m
- ✅ **Plinth coverage** (KMBR): 75% (≤200 m²), 65% (200–500 m²), 50% (>500 m²)
- ✅ **FAR / FSI** for Kerala residential: typically **1.5 to 2.0**
- ✅ **Scale 1:50** → actual 5 m = drawn 10 cm = AutoCAD **5000 units (mm)**
- ✅ **Setbacks (KMBR, single-storey, 200–400 m² plot):** Front 3.0 m, Rear 2.0 m, Side 1.5 m
- ✅ **Line weights:** Walls 0.5–0.7 mm (thick), dimensions/centre lines 0.25 mm (thin)
- ✅ **Door symbol:** 90° arc + hinge line; **Window symbol:** two parallel lines + centre line
- ✅ **Stair formula:** 2R + T = 0.60 to 0.64 m (Blondel's rule)
- ✅ **Number of treads** = (Number of risers) − 1
- ✅ **Section line A-A** must be marked on the plan as a chain-dashed thick line with arrowheads
- ✅ **Hatching:** Use `ANSI31` pattern for brick walls, `EARTH` or `DOTS` for ground in section
- ✅ **AutoCAD default units:** Set to **millimetres** for civil drawings
- ✅ **Title block** must contain: Project, Drawing Title, Scale, Date, Drawn by, Checked by
- ✅ **North symbol** is mandatory on the site plan (use `INSERT` of standard block)
- ✅ **IS 962:1989** governs architectural drawing standards
- ✅ **NBC 2016** governs building construction standards
- ✅ **KMBR** governs Kerala-specific setbacks and FAR
- ✅ **Verandah minimum width:** 1.0 m (residential)
- ✅ **Coverage formula:** $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{A_{\text{plinth}}}{A_{\text{plot}}} \times 100\,\%$
- ✅ **FAR formula:** $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{A_{\text{total floor}}}{A_{\text{plot}}}$
- ✅ **Plinth area** = External length × External breadth (excluding chajja projections)
- ✅ **Carpet area** = Built-up area − Wall area

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Lab Viva:** *Always be ready to draw a quick sketch of a site plan OR plan OR section on plain paper and explain the scale, line types, dimensions, and KMBR compliance within 5 minutes.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plan a double storeyed residential building with sloped roof for a given plot: draw site plan, plan, section and elevation and prepare a file with hardcopies for approval from authorities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL408 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plan a double storeyed residential building with sloped roof for a given plot: draw site plan, plan, section and elevation and prepare a file with hardcopies for approval from authorities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Building Plan and Approval Process**: The systematic preparation of architectural drawings (site plan, floor plans, sections, and elevations) of a proposed double-storeyed residential building with a sloped (pitched) roof, conforming to the **Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR)**, **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)**, and local development authority regulations, followed by compilation of hardcopy documents (commonly called the *building permit file* or *sanction file*) for submission to and approval from the **Local Self Government Institution (LSGI)** — typically the Grama Panchayat, Municipality, or Municipal Corporation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (PCCEL408)**
> A *double-storeyed residential building* is defined as a residential structure with **G + 1** configuration (Ground Floor + First Floor), having a **sloped roof** with a pitch generally between **15° to 30°** (or rise-to-span ratio between **1:2 and 1:1**), designed in accordance with **KMBR (Kerala)**, **NBC 2016**, and the **Town and Country Planning (TCP) Act** of the state.

## 1.2 Key Drawings Required for Approval

| Drawing Type | Purpose | KTU Standard Scale |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Plan / Location Plan** | Shows the building location within the survey boundary and surrounding roads | 1:5000 or 1:10000 |
| **Site Plan** | Shows the proposed building position on the plot with setbacks, north direction, and existing features | 1:200 or 1:500 |
| **Floor Plans (G.F. & F.F.)** | Horizontal cut view of each floor showing walls, doors, windows, room layout | 1:50 or 1:100 |
| **Section** | Vertical cut through the building showing floor heights, roof slope, foundation details | 1:50 or 1:100 |
| **Elevations (All Sides)** | External orthographic projection of all four faces of the building | 1:50 or 1:100 |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of preparing a building approval file like **filing a passport application**:

- Just as a passport application requires your **photograph, address proof, ID proof, and a filled form** — a building approval requires **drawings, ownership proof, tax receipts, and an application form**.
- The drawings are the "identity" of your building — they tell the authorities *what you want to build, where, and how*.
- The **Local Panchayat/Municipality** acts like the **passport office** — it verifies everything, ensures rules are followed (no encroachment, no violation of setbacks, structural safety), and then *approves* the construction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary**: You are essentially creating a "blueprint dossier" — a complete set of architectural drawings backed by ownership and technical documents — that proves your proposed house is **legal, safe, and compliant** with local building rules.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (KMBR / NBC 2016)

- **Minimum Plot Size for Double-Storeyed Residential** : **2.0 cents (80 m²)** for plots with road access
- **Maximum Ground Coverage** : **60 %** for plots ≤ 5 cents, **50 %** for plots > 5 cents
- **Floor Area Ratio (FAR)** : typically **1.5 to 2.0** (varies by local body)
- **Minimum Plinth Height** : **450 mm to 600 mm** above road level (flood-prone areas: **900 mm**)
- **Minimum Head Room** : **2.4 m** for habitable rooms; **2.2 m** for bathrooms
- **Minimum Staircase Width** : **0.9 m to 1.0 m** (1.0 m preferred for double-storeyed)
- **Standard Sloped Roof Pitch** : **1:2 to 1:1** (span-to-rise)
- **Standard Sheet Sizes** : A0 (1189×841 mm), A1 (841×594 mm), A2 (594×420 mm), A3 (420×297 mm), A4 (297×210 mm)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: Students must mention **KMBR (Kerala Municipal Building Rules)** and **NBC 2016** by name. Vague references like "building rules" or "municipal rules" will result in **partial marking**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of Building Planning — Structured Logic

The process of planning a double-storeyed residential building follows a **hierarchical, top-down workflow**:

### Step 1: Plot Analysis and Feasibility Study
- Identify the **plot dimensions, shape, road access, and orientation**.
- Verify **plot ownership documents** (deed, tax receipt, possession certificate).
- Check for **restrictions** if plot is in coastal regulation zone (CRZ), flood-prone zone, or near airport authority.
- Determine the **north direction** using the site plan and orient the building for optimal ventilation and sunlight (recommended: longer side facing **East-West** for cross-ventilation).

### Step 2: Application of Building Rules (KMBR)
The **Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR)** govern:
- **Setbacks** (front, rear, side margins)
- **Ground coverage limits**
- **Floor Area Ratio (FAR)**
- **Height restrictions**
- **Open space requirements**
- **Staircase and circulation norms**

### Step 3: Functional Design (Bubble Diagram → Block Plan)
- Generate a **bubble diagram** showing room relationships (e.g., kitchen near dining, bedroom near bathroom).
- Convert to a **block plan** with preliminary room sizes.
- Apply **Vastu principles** (optional, common in Indian practice).

### Step 4: Preparation of Working Drawings
- **Site Plan** → **Floor Plans** → **Section** → **Elevations**
- Maintain the **projected view consistency**: Section and elevations must be derived from the plan, not drawn independently.

### Step 5: Sloped Roof Geometry
- Calculate the **ridge height** based on the span of the room and the chosen pitch.
- Ridge height $H_r$ above the wall plate:

$$
H_r = \left(\frac{S}{2}\right) \times \tan(\theta)
$$

where $S$ is the span of the roof (clear distance between supporting walls) and $\theta$ is the pitch angle.

- Common design simplification: use the **rise-to-span ratio** (1:2, 1:1.5, 1:1).

### Step 6: Compilation of the Approval File
- Bundle drawings + documents in a **standard file format** (A4 or legal size) with proper indexing.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formulas for PCCEL408 — Module Experiment 2**

| # | Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Ground Coverage** $\text{GC}$ | $\text{GC} = \dfrac{A_b}{A_p} \times 100\,\%$ | % | $A_b$ = built-up area at ground floor; $A_p$ = total plot area |
| 2 | **Floor Area Ratio (FAR)** | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{\sum A_f}{A_p}$ | dimensionless | $\sum A_f$ = sum of all floor areas |
| 3 | **Total Built-up Area (Double-Storeyed)** | $A_{\text{total}} = A_{gf} + A_{ff} - A_{st}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | $A_{st}$ = area occupied by staircase at each floor |
| 4 | **Ridge Height** | $H_r = \dfrac{S}{2} \times \tan(\theta)$ | m | $S$ = roof span; $\theta$ = pitch angle |
| 5 | **Ridge Length** | $L_r = L_p + 2 \times e$ | m | $L_p$ = plan length; $e$ = eave projection (typ. 0.45 m) |
| 6 | **Roof Slope (Pitch)** | $\text{Pitch} = \dfrac{\text{Rise}}{\text{Span}} = \tan(\theta)$ | ratio | Typical: 1:2 to 1:1 |
| 7 | **Plinth Area** | $A_{\text{plinth}} = L_{\text{ext}} \times B_{\text{ext}}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Measured at plinth level outer dimensions |
| 8 | **Carpet Area** | $A_{\text{carpet}} = A_{\text{plinth}} - A_{\text{walls}} - A_{\text{shaft}}$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Excludes walls, ducts, balcony |
| 9 | **Total Height of Building** | $H = H_{pl} + (n \times H_{\text{floor}}) + H_r$ | m | $H_{pl}$ = plinth height; $n$ = number of floors; $H_r$ = ridge ht. |
| 10 | **Staircase Riser / Tread** | $2R + T = 60$ to $64$ cm | cm | Standard anthropometric formula |
| 11 | **Setback (Front Margin)** | As per KMBR (typ. $\geq 3.0$ m) | m | Check local LSGI schedule |
| 12 | **Plot Area Conversion** | $1 \text{ cent} = 40.47 \text{ m}^2$ | $\text{m}^2$ | Kerala standard |

## 2.3 Why This System Exists — Engineering Utility

- **Legal Compliance**: Prevents encroachment on roads, neighbour's land, and public spaces.
- **Safety**: Ensures structural stability, fire safety, and disaster resilience (especially relevant in Kerala's flood-prone geography).
- **Urban Planning**: Maintains orderly growth, road width, drainage corridors, and aesthetic uniformity.
- **Property Valuation**: Sanctioned buildings have higher resale value and are eligible for bank loans, insurance, and government housing schemes.
- **Tax Assessment**: Approved building plans form the basis for property tax and utility connections (water, electricity).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Room Standards (NBC 2016 / KMBR)

| Room Type | Minimum Area | Minimum Width | Ventilation | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Living Room | $9.0 \text{ m}^2$ | $2.4 \text{ m}$ | 1/7 of floor area | Common to all |
| Master Bedroom | $9.5 \text{ m}^2$ | $2.7 \text{ m}$ | 1/7 of floor area | Attached bath preferred |
| Bedroom | $7.5 \text{ m}^2$ | $2.4 \text{ m}$ | 1/7 of floor area | — |
| Kitchen | $5.0 \text{ m}^2$ | $1.8 \text{ m}$ | 1/10 of floor area (window/vent) | — |
| Bathroom | $1.8 \text{ m}^2$ | $1.2 \text{ m}$ | Mechanical/Window $0.3 \text{ m}^2$ | Waterproofing |
| Water Closet (WC) | $1.1 \text{ m}^2$ | $0.9 \text{ m}$ | $0.15 \text{ m}^2$ | — |
| Staircase (residential) | — | $1.0 \text{ m}$ (clear) | — | Riser $\leq 19$ cm; Tread $\geq 25$ cm |
| Passage/Corridor | — | $1.0 \text{ m}$ | — | — |
| Verandah | $5.0 \text{ m}^2$ | $1.8 \text{ m}$ | Open | Common in Kerala |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Building Geometry from Plot

**Given a sample problem statement (typical KTU problem):**
> *"Plan a double-storeyed residential building with a sloped roof on a rectangular plot of 12.0 m × 10.0 m. The site has road access on the south side. The owner requires 3 bedrooms, 1 living room, 1 kitchen, 1 dining area, 2 attached bathrooms, 1 common bathroom, and a car porch."*

### Step 1: Plot Data and Orientation
- Plot dimensions: $L_p \times B_p = 12.0 \text{ m} \times 10.0 \text{ m}$
- Plot area:
$$
A_p = 12.0 \times 10.0 = 120.0 \text{ m}^2
$$
- Convert to cents:
$$
A_p = \frac{120.0}{40.47} \approx 2.97 \text{ cents}
$$
- Road access: South side (front of the house faces South).

### Step 2: Setback Application (KMBR)
Assume applicable setbacks for a 2.97 cent plot in a Panchayat area:
- Front (South): $3.0 \text{ m}$
- Rear (North): $2.0 \text{ m}$
- Side 1 (East): $1.5 \text{ m}$
- Side 2 (West): $1.5 \text{ m}$

Therefore, the **buildable footprint dimensions** are:

$$
L_{\text{build}} = 12.0 - (1.5 + 1.5) = 9.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
B_{\text{build}} = 10.0 - (3.0 + 2.0) = 5.0 \text{ m}
$$

### Step 3: Ground Coverage Check

$$
\text{Plinth area available} = 9.0 \times 5.0 = 45.0 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\text{Ground Coverage (GC)} = \frac{45.0}{120.0} \times 100\,\% = 37.5\,\%
$$

Since KMBR permits up to **60 %** for plots $\leq 5$ cents, this is **compliant**.

### Step 4: FAR Check
For a double-storeyed building, assuming the **first floor** has the same built-up area as the ground floor (typical for residential):

$$
\sum A_f = 45.0 + 45.0 = 90.0 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{90.0}{120.0} = 0.75
$$

Since KMBR allows FAR up to **1.5 to 2.0**, this is well within limits — there is **scope for a future third floor** if desired.

### Step 5: Sloped Roof Geometry
- Roof span: $S = 9.0 \text{ m}$ (width of building)
- Chosen pitch: $1:2$ (rise : span)
- Rise (ridge height above wall plate):
$$
\text{Rise} = \frac{S}{2} = \frac{9.0}{2} = 4.5 \text{ m}
$$

Alternatively, in terms of angle:
$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta = \tan^{-1}(0.5) \approx 26.57^\circ
$$

### Step 6: Total Building Height
- Plinth height: $H_{pl} = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- Floor-to-floor height: $H_{\text{floor}} = 3.0 \text{ m}$
- Ridge height above wall plate: $4.5 \text{ m}$
- Wall plate height above first floor: $3.0 \text{ m}$

$$
H_{\text{total}} = 0.6 + (2 \times 3.0) + 3.0 + 4.5 = 14.1 \text{ m}
$$

This satisfies the KMBR height limit of **15.0 m** for double-storeyed residential buildings (no lift required).

### Step 7: Staircase Design
- Use the relation $2R + T = 60$ to $64$ cm.
- Choose $R = 15 \text{ cm}$ and $T = 30 \text{ cm}$:
$$
2(15) + 30 = 60 \text{ cm} \quad \checkmark
$$
- Number of risers per floor: $\dfrac{300}{15} = 20$ risers
- Number of treads: $20 - 1 = 19$ treads
- Total going per floor: $19 \times 30 = 570 \text{ cm} = 5.7 \text{ m}$
- Staircase plan length (with landing): typically $5.7 + 1.0 = 6.7 \text{ m}$ — needs to be accommodated via a **U-turn or dog-legged stair**.

### Step 8: Room Layout Sketch (Block Plan)
Distribute rooms in the 9.0 m × 5.0 m footprint:

| Zone | Ground Floor | First Floor |
|---|---|---|
| South side (front) | Car Porch, Living Room | Balcony, Master Bedroom |
| Middle | Staircase, Dining | Staircase, Passage |
| North side (rear) | Kitchen, Bedroom-1 | Bedroom-2, Bedroom-3 |
| Wet areas | Common Bath, WC | Attached Bath (Master), Common Bath |

## 3.3 Common Pitfalls in Numerical Verification

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Numerical)**
> 1. Confusing **plot area** with **buildable area** — always subtract setbacks.
> 2. Forgetting to **deduct staircase area** when summing floor areas for FAR.
> 3. Using **roof angle incorrectly** — the rise is calculated from the *half-span*, not the full span.
> 4. Treating **plinth area** and **carpet area** as identical.
> 5. Not checking **FAR after floor deductions** — students often exceed the limit unknowingly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Complete Workflow of Building Plan Preparation and Approval

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Receive Plot Details] --> B[Plot Survey and Document Verification]
    B --> C[Check Zoning, Road Access, NOC Requirements]
    C --> D[Apply KMBR Setbacks and FAR Rules]
    D --> E[Develop Bubble Diagram for Room Layout]
    E --> F[Create Block Plan with Room Sizes]
    F --> G[Finalize Floor Plans GF and FF at 1 to 50]
    G --> H[Draw Section at 1 to 50 showing Sloped Roof]
    H --> I[Draw All Four Elevations at 1 to 50]
    I --> J[Prepare Site Plan at 1 to 200]
    J --> K[Compile Approval File with Hardcopies]
    K --> L{Application Complete and Compliant?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Submit to Local Panchayat or Municipality]
    L -- No --> N[Revise Drawings and Documents]
    N --> G
    M --> O[Authority Reviews and Site Inspects]
    O --> P[Approval Granted or Queries Raised]
    P --> Q[Construction Begins]
    Q --> R[End]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Documents Required in the Approval File

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FILE[Building Approval File Hardcopy]
    A1[Application Form prescribed by LSGI]
    A2[Building Permit Application Form]
    A3[Site Plan Existing and Proposed]
    A4[Floor Plans GF and FF]
    A5[Building Section with Sloped Roof]
    A6[All Four Elevations]
    A7[Key Plan at 1 to 5000]
    A8[Ownership Document Sale Deed]
    A9[Latest Property Tax Receipt]
    A10[Possession Certificate]
    A11[Layout Plan Approval from Authority]
    A12[Engineer Certificate Structural Safety]
    A13[Architect Certificate]
    A14[NOC from Fire Department if applicable]
    A15[Septic Tank and Sanitary Plan]
    end
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Double-Storeyed Plan

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GF[Ground Floor]
    G1[Car Porch South Front]
    G2[Living Room 4.0 x 4.5 m]
    G3[Dining 3.0 x 3.0 m]
    G4[Staircase Dog Legged]
    G5[Kitchen 3.0 x 3.5 m]

    G8[WC 1.0 x 1.5 m]
    end
    subgraph FF[First Floor]
    F1[Balcony South Front]
    F2[Master Bedroom 4.0 x 4.5 m]

    F4[Passage 1.2 m wide]
    F5[Staircase Landing]
    F6[Bedroom 2 3.5 x 3.5 m]
```
