## 4.4 Drawing Conventions and Symbol Legend (for Approval Drawings)

| Symbol | Meaning | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `─────` | Wall (continuous thick line) | `↕` | Stair direction (up) |
| `─ ─ ─` | Centre line | `─ ─ ─` | Hidden line (dashed) |
| `[DOOR]` | Door opening with arc | `[WIN]` | Window with sill |
| `WC` | Water Closet | `B` | Bath |
| `K` | Kitchen | `LR` | Living Room |
| `BR` | Bedroom | `ST` | Store |
| `W/D` | Washing/Drying | `CP` | Car Porch |
| `↗` | North direction arrow | `R` | Roof ridge line |
| `─ ─ ├` | Beam | `●` | Column |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the documents required to be submitted along with the building plan for obtaining approval from the local authority.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Duly filled application form (Form I / prescribed format) — *1 mark*
2. Site plan, floor plans, section, and elevations of the proposed building — *1 mark*
3. Ownership documents (sale deed / possession certificate) and latest property tax receipt — *1 mark*

*(Additional for full file: key plan, layout approval copy, structural certificate, septic tank plan, NOC from fire/panchayat if applicable.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between Plinth Area, Carpet Area, and Built-up Area?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Plinth Area** — Total covered area measured at the *plinth level* (outer dimensions of the building at floor level). — *1 mark*
- **Carpet Area** — The *net usable floor area* inside the walls, excluding wall thickness, ducts, shafts, balconies, and staircases. — *1 mark*
- **Built-up Area** — Plinth area *plus* the area of external walls, balconies, verandahs, and projections (cupboards/ledges not included). — *1 mark*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure to plan a double-storeyed residential building with a sloped roof on a given plot. List the drawings to be prepared and the documents to be included in the approval file. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A rectangular plot measures **15.0 m × 12.0 m** with road access on the west side. As per KMBR, applicable setbacks are: front = 4.0 m, rear = 2.0 m, sides = 1.5 m each. The building has a uniform built-up area on both floors. The roof is a sloped roof with a pitch of **1:2 (rise:half-span)**. Calculate the **buildable footprint area, ground coverage percentage, FAR, and ridge height** of the building. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Procedure for planning (4 marks):**
1. Receive plot details, survey the site, verify ownership documents.
2. Check zoning restrictions, road width, and obtain layout approval.
3. Apply KMBR/NBC rules: setbacks, FAR, height, ground coverage, parking.
4. Prepare bubble diagram → block plan → detailed floor plans.
5. Draw section through the building showing sloped roof, plinth, and floor heights.
6. Draw all four elevations and a site plan showing north, road, setbacks, and surroundings.
7. Compile drawings, certificates, and ownership documents into a hardcopy file.
8. Submit to the local Panchayat / Municipality for approval.

**Drawings to be prepared (2 marks):**
- Key Plan, Site Plan, Floor Plans (G.F. & F.F.), Section, and all four Elevations.

**Documents in the approval file (1 mark):**
- Application form, ownership proof, tax receipt, architect/engineer certificates, NOCs (if any), and the septic tank plan.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
$$
L_p = 15.0 \text{ m}, \quad B_p = 12.0 \text{ m}, \quad \text{Front (West)} = 4.0 \text{ m}
$$
$$
\text{Rear (East)} = 2.0 \text{ m}, \quad \text{Side 1} = \text{Side 2} = 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 1: Buildable footprint dimensions (2 marks)**

$$
L_{\text{build}} = 15.0 - (1.5 + 1.5) = 12.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
B_{\text{build}} = 12.0 - (4.0 + 2.0) = 6.0 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\therefore A_{\text{footprint}} = 12.0 \times 6.0 = 72.0 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2: Ground Coverage (2 marks)**

$$
A_{\text{plot}} = 15.0 \times 12.0 = 180.0 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\text{GC} = \frac{72.0}{180.0} \times 100\,\% = 40.0\,\%
$$

Since 40 % $\leq$ 60 % (KMBR limit for plots $\leq 5$ cents ≈ **202 m²**), the design is **compliant**.

**Step 3: FAR (1.5 marks)**

$$
\sum A_f = 72.0 + 72.0 = 144.0 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\text{FAR} = \frac{144.0}{180.0} = 0.80
$$

Within the KMBR limit (typ. 1.5–2.0) — **compliant**.

**Step 4: Ridge Height (1.5 marks)**

Roof span $S = 6.0 \text{ m}$ (shorter side, the natural roof span direction).
Pitch ratio 1:2 (rise : half-span):
$$
\text{Rise} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6.0}{2} \times 2 = 1.5 \text{ m}
$$

Wait — reinterpreting "pitch 1:2 (rise : half-span)":
- If rise is $R$ and half-span is $S/2$, then $R : (S/2) = 1 : 2$
- Therefore $R = (S/2) \times (1/2) = (6.0 / 2) \times 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ m}$

$$
\boxed{H_r = 1.5 \text{ m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question A Part b)**
> 1. Students often confuse the roof **span direction** — the ridge runs *parallel* to the longer side of the building, so the span is the *shorter* side ($B_{\text{build}}$).
> 2. Misinterpretation of the pitch ratio: "1:2 (rise:half-span)" means **rise = (half-span) ÷ 2**, not half-span itself.
> 3. Forgetting to **express GC as a percentage** — full marks only if % is given.
> 4. Treating **FAR** as a percentage (it is dimensionless, written as a decimal like 0.80 or ratio like 0.80 : 1).
> 5. Not explicitly stating whether the design is "**compliant**" with KMBR — examiners award a bonus mark for the compliance statement.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is a sloped roof? Explain the different types of sloped roofs used for residential buildings in Kerala. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Draw a neat sketch of a **section through a double-storeyed residential building with a pitched roof**, indicating the plinth, ground floor, first floor, lintel, roof, ridge, eaves, and stair. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **sloped roof** (pitched roof) is a roof that slopes downward in two or more directions from a central ridge, allowing rainwater to drain off by gravity. In Kerala, pitched roofs are preferred over flat roofs due to the high rainfall intensity (annual rainfall > **3000 mm** in many districts).

**Types of sloped roofs used in Kerala (5 marks):**

| Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Gable Roof** | Two-sloped roof with triangular ends | Most common in Kerala houses |
| **Hip Roof** | All four sides slope down from the ridge | Wind-resistant, used in coastal areas |
| **Gambrel Roof** | Two slopes on each side (steeper lower, flatter upper) | Provides attic space |
| **Mansard Roof** | Four-sided with double slope on each side | Less common in Kerala |
| **Shed / Mono-pitch Roof** | Single slope | Verandah, car porch, outbuildings |
| **A-Frame Roof** | Steep pitch extending to ground | Resorts, modern villas |

**Materials used:** Country tiles (traditional), clay tiles, Mangalore tiles, **AC sheets, GI sheets, concrete tiles, slate, and shingles**. Modern Kerala houses often use **reinforced concrete slab with tile cladding** to simulate a sloped look.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Section Drawing — Key Features to Indicate:**

1. **Foundation** — Footing, plinth beam, DPC (Damp Proof Course) — *1 mark*
2. **Plinth** — 450–600 mm above ground level (or 900 mm in flood zones) — *1 mark*
3. **Ground Floor** — 3.0 m clear headroom, with floor finish above plinth — *1 mark*
4. **Lintel and Sunshade** — at door/window locations, 600–750 mm projection — *1 mark*
5. **First Floor Slab and Walls** — 3.0 m clear height — *1 mark*
6. **Sloped Roof** — pitched at 1:2 with ridge, rafters, purlins, tiles, ceiling — *1 mark*
7. **Staircase** — showing flights, landing, and handrail — *1 mark*

**Schematic ASCII Representation (to be drawn neatly in the answer sheet):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Sloped Roof Ridge 4.5m above wall plate] --> B[Slope 1 in 2 pitch]
    B --> C[Eave Projection 0.45 m]
    C --> D[First Floor Wall Plate 3.0 m]
    D --> E[First Floor Slab 3.0 m]
    E --> F[Ground Floor Wall Plate 3.0 m]
    F --> G[Ground Floor Slab 0.3 m thick]
    G --> H[Plinth Level 0.6 m above GL]
    H --> I[Foundation Footing 1.0 m below GL]
    J[Staircase Flight 1 Ground to First] --> E
    K[Staircase Flight 2 Ground to First] --> E
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B)**
> 1. **Part (a)**: Students often forget to mention **Kerala-specific context** (rainfall, traditional tiles). Generic answers lose marks.
> 2. **Part (a)**: Only listing types without describing them = only partial credit.
> 3. **Part (b)**: Section must show **proportions correctly** — examiners check whether the plinth is shorter than the floor height, and whether the roof slope is visible.
> 4. **Part (b)**: Skipping the **DPC line** at plinth level is a common error.
> 5. **Part (b)**: Not labeling any dimension = at least 1–2 marks lost. Always provide **at least 4–5 dimensions**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for PCCEL408 — Module Experiment 2**

### A. Core Concept
- [ ] Plan = "**What** to build"; Approval file = "**Proof that it is legal**" to build.
- [ ] For a **double-storeyed residential building with sloped roof**, the KTU 2024 scheme expects drawings + a hardcopy file.
- [ ] Always cite **KMBR (Kerala Municipal Building Rules)** and **NBC 2016** by name.

### B. Mandatory Drawings (Approval File)
- [ ] Key Plan (1:5000 or 1:10000)
- [ ] Site Plan (1:200 or 1:500) — showing setbacks, north, road, existing features
- [ ] Floor Plans (G.F. & F.F.) at 1:50 or 1:100
- [ ] Section (1:50 or 1:100) — showing foundation, plinth, floors, lintel, roof, staircase
- [ ] All four Elevations (1:50 or 1:100)
- [ ] Septic tank / sanitary layout
- [ ] Structural drawing (if asked)

### C. Mandatory Documents (Approval File)
- [ ] Application form (Form prescribed by LSGI)
- [ ] Sale Deed / Ownership Proof
- [ ] Latest Property Tax Receipt
- [ ] Possession Certificate
- [ ] Layout Approval Copy (from Village Officer / Town Planner)
- [ ] Architect's Certificate & Engineer's Certificate (Structural Stability)
- [ ] NOC (Fire / Pollution / Airport / Coastal / Panchayat, as applicable)
- [ ] Key Plan / Location Plan
- [ ] Site Photographs (recent)

### D. Numerical Essentials
- [ ] **1 cent = 40.47 m²**
- [ ] **Ground Coverage** = (Plinth Area / Plot Area) × 100 %
- [ ] **FAR** = (Total Floor Area) / (Plot Area), dimensionless
- [ ] **Ridge Height** $H_r = (S/2) \times \tan(\theta)$ where $S$ = roof span
- [ ] **Staircase Rule** $2R + T = 60$ to $64$ cm
- [ ] **Minimum Headroom** = 2.4 m habitable, 2.2 m bath/WC
- [ ] **Minimum Plinth Height** = 450–600 mm (900 mm in flood-prone)

### E. Sloped Roof Essentials
- [ ] **Pitch** = Rise / Span (or Rise / Half-span, depending on convention)
- [ ] Common pitches: 1:2 (gentle) to 1:1 (steep)
- [ ] Ridge runs **parallel to the longer side** of the building
- [ ] Eave projection typical: 450–600 mm
- [ ] Materials: Country tiles, Mangalore tiles, AC/GI sheets, concrete tiles

### F. Common Examiner Traps
- [ ] Confusing **plot area** with **buildable area** (setbacks not subtracted).
- [ ] Forgetting to **deduct staircase** from total floor area for FAR.
- [ ] Calling FAR a percentage — it is a **dimensionless ratio**.
- [ ] Drawing section/elevation **inconsistently** with the plan (e.g., door shifted).
- [ ] Skipping the **DPC (Damp Proof Course)** at plinth level in section.
- [ ] Not specifying **north direction** on the site plan.

### G. Mnemonics for Quick Recall
- **"PFFSEK"** = **P**lans, **F**loor plans, **F**ile, **S**ection, **E**levations, **K**ey plan — the 5 components of the approval drawing set.
- **"GC-FAR-H"** = **G**round coverage, **F**AR, **H**eight — the three KMBR numerical checks you must do.
- **"KMBR + NBC + TCP"** = the three regulatory frameworks you must always cite.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plan a public building (office complex, public health centre, commercial, educational, post office, or bank) and draw the plan, section and elevation: any two
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL408 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plan a public building (office complex, public health centre, commercial, educational, post office, or bank) and draw the plan, section and elevation: any two

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Public Building Planning** is the systematic process of designing a building intended for public use — such as an office complex, public health centre, commercial complex, educational institution, post office, or bank — by integrating functional requirements, statutory building byelaws, structural logic, and architectural aesthetics into a coordinated set of working drawings consisting of the **Plan**, **Section**, and **Elevation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Working Definition (PCCEL408):**
> "The arrangement of rooms, circulation spaces, services, and structural elements in a public building, represented through orthographic projections — plan (horizontal cut at sill level, 900 mm above FFL), section (vertical cut through the building), and elevation (orthographic view of external faces) — in accordance with the National Building Code of India (NBC 2016) and Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR 2019)."

The three primary orthographic drawings required for a KTU lab viva and record are:

| Drawing | Definition | Section Line Position |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Horizontal cut through the building showing room layout, walls, doors, windows | **900 mm above Finished Floor Level (FFL)** |
| **Section** | Vertical cut through the building showing floor, roof, wall thickness, heights | Through the staircase, a window, or major openings |
| **Elevation** | Orthographic projection of an external face of the building | No cut — surface view only |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of designing a public building like **dressing a 3D human body for three different photo shoots**:

- **The Plan** is like an **X-ray from above** — you slice the body horizontally at chest level and see all the organs (rooms) arranged in the torso (footprint).
- **The Section** is like a **side-cut MRI scan** — you slice the body vertically and see the spine (structural wall), heart (staircase), head (roof) all in relation.
- **The Elevation** is the **passport photograph** — a clean, flat view of just the outer face, with no internal organs visible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why public buildings are special:** Unlike a private house, a public building must accommodate **hundreds of users per day** with diverse needs — elderly persons, persons with disabilities, children, staff, and visitors. Hence, rules like ramp width, staircase rise, and corridor width are **not optional**; they are governed by **statute**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants for Public Buildings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Constants to Memorize for Viva:**

- **Plinth height** (above ground level): **450 mm to 900 mm** (standard: 600 mm)
- **Sill level** (window base above FFL): **900 mm**
- **Lintel level** (window top above FFL): **2100 mm**
- **Head room** (clear floor-to-ceiling): **3000 mm minimum** (residential); **3600 mm preferred for public**
- **Door height**: **2100 mm** (residential); **2400 mm** (public)
- **Standard brick wall thickness**: **200 mm (1B)**, **300 mm (1½B)**, **400 mm (2B)**
- **Concrete/column wall thickness**: **230 mm to 300 mm**
- **Staircase rise (R)**: **150 mm to 190 mm**
- **Staircase going (G)**: **250 mm to 300 mm**
- **2R + G** rule: **Between 580 mm and 650 mm** (ideally **600 mm**)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Public Building Planning

A KTU-graded public building design must satisfy these **five interlocking pillars**:

### Pillar 1 — Functional Requirements (User-Based Design)

Every public building has a specific user activity profile:

| Building Type | Primary Functional Need |
|---|---|
| **Office Complex** | Individual cabins, conference halls, server rooms, reception, waiting area |
| **Public Health Centre (PHC)** | OPD rooms, consultation cabins, pharmacy, waiting hall, isolation ward, store |
| **Commercial (Shop/Showroom)** | Display area, billing counter, godown, trial rooms, parking |
| **Educational (School)** | Classrooms, staff room, principal's room, library, toilet block, playground |
| **Post Office** | Public counters (PO/SO), sorting room, record room, cash room (vault), staff room |
| **Bank** | Banking hall, cash cabin, strong room/vault, locker room, manager's cabin, ATM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Always design **circulation space (corridors + staircase)** first, then fit functional rooms around it. Circulation typically consumes **25% to 35%** of total built-up area in public buildings.

### Pillar 2 — Building Byelaws (Statutory Compliance)

Kerala follows the **Kerala Municipal Building Rules (KMBR 2019)** along with **NBC 2016** provisions:

| Parameter | KMBR 2019 / NBC 2016 Standard |
|---|---|
| **Plot coverage (F.A.R / F.S.I)** | Varies by zone — typically **1.5 to 2.5** |
| **Setbacks (Front)** | **3 m to 6 m** depending on road width |
| **Setbacks (Side)** | **1.5 m to 3 m minimum** |
| **Setbacks (Rear)** | **2 m to 3 m minimum** |
| **Maximum height** | **15 m** without lift; higher with mandatory lift |
| **Staircase width (public)** | **1500 mm minimum** (NBC) |
| **Corridor width (public)** | **1800 mm to 2400 mm** (single/double loaded) |
| **Toilet ratio** | **1 per 50 occupants** (men); **1 per 25** (women) |
| **Ramp gradient** | **1 in 12 to 1 in 15** for accessibility |
| **Ramp width** | **1200 mm minimum** for wheelchair access |
| **Fire escape** | Required for buildings **> 15 m height** or **> 500 m² floor area** |

### Pillar 3 — Structural Logic (Load Path)

A public building must transfer loads safely:

**Dead Load + Live Load → Slab → Beam → Column → Foundation → Soil**

| Component | Typical Material | Typical Size |
|---|---|---|
| **Foundation** | Isolated RCC footing | 1.5 m × 1.5 m × 0.3 m (for 2-storey) |
| **Column** | RCC | 230 mm × 300 mm minimum |
| **Beam** | RCC | 230 mm × 450 mm |
| **Slab** | RCC | 125 mm to 150 mm thick |
| **Wall** | Brick masonry / AAC block | 200 mm external, 100 mm internal |

### Pillar 4 — Circulation Design (Vertical & Horizontal)

**Horizontal circulation = Corridor / Verandah**
**Vertical circulation = Staircase / Ramp / Lift**

For a public building, the staircase must be designed using:

$$2R + G = 580 \text{ to } 650 \text{ mm (ideally } 600 \text{ mm)}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Rise of step (vertical height), typically **150 mm**
- $G$ = Going of step (horizontal tread), typically **250–300 mm**

**Number of risers per flight** = $\frac{\text{Floor-to-Floor Height}}{R}$

**Number of treads** = (Risers per flight) − 1

### Pillar 5 — Drawing Standards (SP 46:1988 & IS 962)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IS 962:1989 + SP 46:1988 — Drawing Sheet Standards for KTU Lab:**
>
> - **Sheet size:** A1 (594 × 841 mm) or A2 preferred for full submission
> - **Border margin:** 20 mm (left), 10 mm (other sides)
> - **Title block:** Bottom-right corner, contains name, roll no., scale, date
> - **Scales used:**
>   - **Plan** → 1:50 or 1:100
>   - **Section** → 1:50 or 1:100
>   - **Elevation** → 1:50 or 1:100
>   - **Site plan** → 1:200 or 1:500
>   - **Detail drawings** → 1:5, 1:10, 1:20

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Public Building Design)

| # | Quantity | Formula / Standard | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Staircase ergonomics | $2R + G \in [580, 650]$ mm | mm |
| 2 | Floor Area (Room) | $A = L \times B$ | m² |
| 3 | Carpet Area | $\approx 0.7 \times \text{Built-up Area}$ | m² |
| 4 | Built-up Area | $\text{Plinth Area} + \text{External walls}$ | m² |
| 5 | Plinth Area | $\text{Outer dimension} \times \text{Outer dimension}$ | m² |
| 6 | FSI / FAR | $\dfrac{\text{Total Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | ratio |
| 7 | Plot Coverage | $\dfrac{\text{Ground Floor Plinth Area}}{\text{Plot Area}} \times 100$ | % |
| 8 | Stair Width (Public) | $\ge 1500$ | mm |
| 9 | Corridor Width (Public) | $\ge 1800$ (single), $\ge 2400$ (double loaded) | mm |
| 10 | Ramp Gradient | $1:12$ (preferred) to $1:15$ (max) | ratio |
| 11 | Head Room | $\ge 3000$ (resi) / $\ge 3600$ (public) | mm |
| 12 | Window-to-Floor Ratio | $\dfrac{A_w}{A_f} \approx \dfrac{1}{6} \text{ to } \dfrac{1}{8}$ | ratio |
| 13 | Ventilation Ratio | $\dfrac{A_v}{A_f} \ge \dfrac{1}{16}$ | ratio |
| 14 | Toilet Fixture Ratio (M) | 1 WC + 1 UR per 50 users | count |
| 15 | Toilet Fixture Ratio (F) | 1 WC per 25 users | count |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

These planning principles are directly applied by:
- **Architects** in schematic design stage
- **Structural engineers** in design of foundations
- **Municipal authorities** for building plan approval
- **Fire department** for NOC issuance
- **Disability Commission** for barrier-free access certification

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU student who masters these standards can directly read and understand any approved building plan in India — a foundational skill for site engineers, BIM modelers, and quantity surveyors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Planning Process & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Phase 1 — Site Analysis (Before Drawing Anything)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Before drawing, the student must record the design brief. Marks are awarded for the design narrative.

**Step 1.1:** Identify the building type and tabulate functional requirements.

$$\text{Room Schedule} = \{(\text{Room Name},\ L \times B,\ \text{Occupancy},\ \text{Adjacency})\}$$

**Step 1.2:** Identify the plot dimensions (assume for a KTU problem):
- Plot size: typically given (e.g., **30 m × 20 m = 600 m²**)
- Road width: assumed (e.g., **9 m main road, 6 m side road**)
- Orientation: Assume **North direction** marked on plan

**Step 1.3:** Apply KMBR setbacks:

$$\text{Buildable Area} = (\text{Plot Length} - \text{Front S.B.} - \text{Rear S.B.}) \times (\text{Plot Width} - 2 \times \text{Side S.B.})$$

**Worked Example:**
- Plot = 30 m × 20 m
- Front S.B. = 3 m, Rear S.B. = 2 m, Side S.B. = 1.5 m each
- Buildable footprint = $(30 - 3 - 2) \times (20 - 1.5 - 1.5) = 25 \times 17 = 425 \text{ m}^2$

## 3.2 Phase 2 — Bubble Diagram (Functional Zoning)

**Step 2.1:** Group rooms by **function**:
- **Public Zone:** Reception, waiting, banking hall / OPD
- **Private Zone:** Manager's cabin, doctor's room, cash cabin
- **Service Zone:** Toilets, store, electrical room, AC plant
- **Circulation Zone:** Staircase, corridor, lift lobby, ramp

**Step 2.2:** Create a **bubble diagram** showing adjacency (to be drawn in SECTION 4).

## 3.3 Phase 3 — Plan Generation (Block Plan → Detailed Plan)

**Step 3.1:** Translate the bubble diagram into a **block plan** with approximate dimensions.

**Step 3.2:** Check structural grid (column layout).

**Step 3.3:** Mark all openings:
- **Doors:** width as per function (main entrance = **1500 mm to 2000 mm double leaf**; toilet = **750 mm**; office = **1000 mm**)
- **Windows:** as per ventilation ratio

$$\text{Window Area Required} = \dfrac{\text{Floor Area of Room}}{6} \text{ to } \dfrac{\text{Floor Area of Room}}{8}$$

**Step 3.4:** Place staircase and verify the formula:

$$2R + G = 600 \text{ mm (ideal)}$$

If floor-to-floor height = **3300 mm** and R = **150 mm**:

$$\text{Risers per flight} = \frac{3300}{150} = 22 \text{ risers}$$

A 2-storey building = 2 flights of 11 risers each, or split into 2 flights of 11 with a landing between.

$$\text{Going per step} = 250 \text{ mm}$$
$$\text{Staircase horizontal run} = 11 \times 250 = 2750 \text{ mm} = 2.75 \text{ m}$$

$$\text{Landing width} = \text{Staircase width} = 1500 \text{ mm (min)}$$

**Step 3.5:** Mark all dimensions in **running dimensions** (continuous) and **overall dimensions** (extreme-to-extreme).

## 3.4 Phase 4 — Section Drawing

**Step 4.1:** Choose the section line (typically through the staircase and a major window).

**Step 4.2:** Project the heights:
- **Plinth height:** 600 mm above GL
- **Sill height:** 900 mm above FFL (= 1500 mm above GL)
- **Lintel height:** 2100 mm above FFL (= 2700 mm above GL)
- **Floor-to-floor:** 3300 mm (residential) / 3600 mm (public)
- **Parapet:** 900 mm to 1200 mm above roof slab

**Step 4.3:** Show the following in the section:
- Ground line, GL, FFL marks
- Plinth beam (200 mm × 300 mm)
- Floor slab (125 mm)
- Roof slab (125 mm to 150 mm)
- Lintel bands at lintel level
- Staircase with rise/tread labels
- Door and window symbols in section
- Roof treatment (weathering course, insulation)

## 3.5 Phase 5 — Elevation Drawing

**Step 5.1:** Project horizontally from the plan to draw the elevation.

**Step 5.2:** Maintain the **same horizontal scale** as the plan (1:50 or 1:100).

**Step 5.3:** Show:
- Parapet line at top
- Eaves line at bottom of roof
- Window and door positions (matching plan)
- Plinth line
- Steps/ramp at entrance
- Textures (brick, plaster, glass) using standard hatching
- Sunshade projection (typically 600 mm to 750 mm)

## 3.6 Phase 6 — Annotation & Detailing (Final Submission)

- **Title block** with student name, roll no., sheet no., date
- **North arrow** on plan
- **Scale bar** below each drawing
- **Schedule of openings** (Door Schedule, Window Schedule)
- **Materials legend** (if multiple materials shown)
- **Note block** with assumptions

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Public Building Specific Requirements

| Building | Public Counter (mm) | Strong Room | Specific Add-On |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bank** | 1500 wide × 750 high (cash counter) | Vault with **150 mm RCC wall** | Locker room, ATM room |
| **Post Office** | 1200 wide (mail counter) | Record room (fire-resistant) | Sorting hall, PO box wall |
| **PHC** | Reception desk at 900 mm height | Pharmacy store | Isolation ward, OT (if upgraded) |
| **School** | — | — | Assembly hall, playground, separate toilet for boys/girls |
| **Office** | Reception counter 1100 mm | Server room (AC) | Conference hall, pantry |
| **Commercial** | Billing counter 900 mm | — | Display windows, trial rooms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake (Penalty Risk):**
> Students often show the **same room dimensions** in plan and section without applying the **plinth, sill, lintel offsets**. Always remember:
> - Plan section line is at **900 mm above FFL** (sill level).
> - Section line cuts through the entire vertical.
> - Therefore, **walls in the plan are drawn at sill level** — door/window sills align with this cut.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bubble Diagram — Public Building Functional Zoning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["PUBLIC ZONE: Reception / Waiting Hall / Banking Hall / OPD"]:::public
    nodeB["STAFF ZONE: Manager Cabin / Doctor Room / Officer Cabins"]:::staff
    nodeC["SERVICE ZONE: Toilets M and F / Store / Electrical Room / Pantry"]:::service
    nodeD["VERTICAL CORE: Staircase / Lift Lobby / Ramp"]:::core
    nodeE["SECURE ZONE: Cash Room / Strong Room / Record Room / Server"]:::secure

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeA --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeD
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeB --- nodeE
    nodeD --- nodeE

    classDef public fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A07000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef staff fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#004080,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef service fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef core fill:#E0BBE4,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef secure fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Plan-to-Section-to-Elevation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["STEP 1: Site Analysis and Plot Dimensions"]:::step
    node2["STEP 2: Apply KMBR Setbacks and Calculate Buildable Area"]:::step
    node3["STEP 3: Bubble Diagram for Functional Zoning"]:::step
    node4["STEP 4: Block Plan with Approximate Room Sizes"]:::step
    node5["STEP 5: Final PLAN with Walls Doors Windows and Dimensions"]:::plan
    node6["STEP 6: Choose Section Line through Staircase and Window"]:::step
    node7["STEP 7: Mark Plinth Sill Lintel Floor Levels on Section"]:::step
    node8["STEP 8: Final SECTION with Staircase Details and Roof"]:::section
    node9["STEP 9: Project Plan Vertically to Elevation"]:::step
    node10["STEP 10: Add Sunshades Parapet Textures and Annotations"]:::step
    node11["STEP 11: Final ELEVATION with Materials and Openings"]:::elevation
    node12["STEP 12: Add Title Block North Arrow Scale Bar and Notes"]:::final

    node1 --> node2 --> node3 --> node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6 --> node7 --> node8
    node5 --> node9 --> node10 --> node11
    node5 -.projection.-> node8
    node11 --> node12
    node8 --> node12

    classDef step fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plan fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef section fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elevation fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#E0BBE4,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Orthographic Projection Logic (Plan-Section-Elevation Relationship)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP["PLAN VIEW: Looking DOWN at 900 mm above FFL"]:::plan
    nodeS["SECTION VIEW: Vertical cut, looking from side"]:::section
    nodeE["ELEVATION VIEW: External view, no cut"]:::elevation
    nodeA["A-A Section Line marked on Plan"]:::annotation

    nodeP -- "Vertical cut at A-A" --> nodeS
    nodeP -- "Look from front direction" --> nodeE
    nodeS -- "Same heights as elevation" --> nodeE

    classDef plan fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef section fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef elevation fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef annotation fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Staircase Component Diagram (Block Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeFloor1["Ground Floor Level 0.000 m"]:::level
    nodeLanding1["Mid-Landing at 1.65 m height"]:::landing
    nodeFloor2["First Floor Level 3.30 m"]:::level
    nodeFlight1["Flight 1: 11 Risers of 150 mm + 10 Treads of 250 mm"]:::flight
    nodeFlight2["Flight 2: 11 Risers of 150 mm + 10 Treads of 250 mm"]:::flight
    nodeRiser["Rise R equals 150 mm"]:::dim
    nodeTread["Going G equals 250 mm"]:::dim
    nodeCheck["Check 2R plus G equals 600 mm OK"]:::check

    nodeFloor1 --> nodeFlight1 --> nodeLanding1 --> nodeFlight2 --> nodeFloor2
    nodeFlight1 -.- nodeRiser
    nodeFlight1 -.- nodeTread
    nodeFlight1 -.- nodeCheck

    classDef level fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef landing fill:#FFCC80,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef flight fill:#C5E1A5,stroke:#33691E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dim fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.5 Wall Section Detail (Roof-to-Foundation Vertical Stack)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeP["Parapet Wall: 900 mm above roof slab, 100 mm thick"]:::wall
    nodeR["Roof Slab: 125 mm RCC with weathering course"]:::slab
    nodeLB2["Lintel Band at 2.7 m above GL: 230 mm x 150 mm"]:::beam
    nodeW["Window Opening: 1.2 m x 1.2 m at sill 0.9 m, lintel 2.1 m"]:::opening
    nodeFL["Floor Slab: 125 mm RCC at 0.6 m above GL"]:::slab
    nodePB["Plinth Beam: 230 mm x 300 mm at GL level"]:::beam
    nodeFT["Footing: 1.5 m x 1.5 m x 0.3 m at 1.2 m below GL"]:::foundation
    nodeGL["Ground Level GL: 0.000 m reference"]:::ground

    nodeP --> nodeR --> nodeLB2 --> nodeW --> nodeFL --> nodePB --> nodeFT
    nodeGL -.- nodePB

    classDef wall fill:#FFAB91,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slab fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef beam fill:#90CAF9,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef opening fill:#FFF59D,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef foundation fill:#A1887F,stroke:#3E2723,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ground fill:#BDBDBD,stroke:#212121,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **Plan**, **Section**, and **Elevation** of a building with respect to the position of the cutting plane.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Plan | Section | Elevation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting plane** | Horizontal at 900 mm above FFL | Vertical through building | No cut |
| **View direction** | Looking down | Looking from side | External face view |
| **Shows** | Room layout, doors, windows | Floor heights, roof, staircase | External appearance, parapet, sunshades |

*Key point for full marks:* The plan section is taken at **sill level (900 mm above FFL)** to ensure all doors and windows appear in the plan.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State any **three National Building Code (NBC 2016)** requirements that are mandatory for a public building but not required for a private residence.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Minimum staircase width of 1500 mm** for public buildings (versus 1000 mm for residences).
2. **Ramp provision with gradient 1:12 to 1:15** for barrier-free access by differently-abled persons.
3. **Fire escape staircase** mandatory if building height exceeds 15 m or floor area exceeds 500 m².
4. **Separate toilets for men and women** with prescribed fixture ratio (1 WC per 50 men / 1 WC per 25 women).
5. **Public corridor minimum width of 1800 mm** (single-loaded) to 2400 mm (double-loaded).

*(Any three for full marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> Plan a **Public Health Centre (PHC)** for a rural area on a plot of size **40 m × 30 m** facing a 9 m wide road on the shorter (south) side. The PHC must have the following:
> - 1 Reception + Waiting Hall (minimum 30 m²)
> - 2 Doctor Consultation Rooms (each 12 m² minimum)
> - 1 Pharmacy + Store (15 m²)
> - 1 Injection / Dressing Room (10 m²)
> - 2 Toilet blocks (1 for men, 1 for women)
> - 1 Staff Room (15 m²)
>
> **Draw to a suitable scale:**
> **(a)** The **Plan** at sill level with all dimensions, doors, windows, and staircase. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** The **Section** along the central line and the **Front Elevation**, showing all levels, staircase detail, and roof treatment. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Plan Drawing (7 marks)

**Step 1: Plot and Setbacks (1 mark)**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Plot size | 40 m × 30 m = 1200 m² |
| Road | 9 m wide on south |
| Front setback (south) | 3 m |
| Rear setback (north) | 2 m |
| Side setback (east & west) | 1.5 m each |
| Buildable footprint | $(40 - 3 - 2) \times (30 - 1.5 - 1.5) = 35 \times 27 = 945 \text{ m}^2$ |
| Proposed ground floor plinth | ~ 32 m × 24 m = 768 m² (within buildable) |

**Step 2: Functional Zoning (1 mark)**

- **South strip (facing road):** Reception, Waiting Hall — public access.
- **Middle strip:** Doctor rooms, Injection room, Staff room.
- **Rear strip:** Pharmacy, Store, Toilets, Staircase (service core).
- **Main entrance:** South face, central.
- **Staircase:** Northeast corner to provide vertical access to first floor.

**Step 3: Room Schedule (2 marks)**

| Room | Size (L × B) | Area (m²) | Door (mm) | Window |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reception + Waiting | 8 m × 4 m | 32 | 1500 D (main) | 1500 × 1500 × 2 |
| Doctor Room 1 | 4 m × 3 m | 12 | 1000 S | 1200 × 1200 |
| Doctor Room 2 | 4 m × 3 m | 12 | 1000 S | 1200 × 1200 |
| Injection Room | 4 m × 2.5 m | 10 | 900 S | 1000 × 1000 |
| Staff Room | 5 m × 3 m | 15 | 1000 S | 1500 × 1500 |
| Pharmacy + Store | 5 m × 3 m | 15 | 1000 S | 1200 × 1200 |
| Toilet (M) | 2.5 m × 1.8 m | 4.5 | 750 S | 600 × 600 (vent.) |
| Toilet (F) | 2.5 m × 1.8 m | 4.5 | 750 S | 600 × 600 (vent.) |
| Corridor | 1.8 m wide | — | — | — |
| Staircase | 3.0 m × 1.5 m | 4.5 | — | — |

**Step 4: Walls and Openings (2 marks)**
- External walls: 230 mm brick
- Internal walls: 115 mm brick
- All external doors double-leaf 1500 × 2400 mm
- Windows as per ventilation ratio
- Show dimensions: running + overall
- North arrow, scale bar, title block

**Step 5: Staircase Check (1 mark)**

$$2R + G = 2(150) + 250 = 550 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(acceptable, between 580-650 mm is ideal; suggest } R = 160, G = 280 \text{ to get } 600)$$

Risers per flight = $\frac{3300}{160} \approx 20.6 \rightarrow$ use **20 risers** (flight height = 3200 mm + 100 mm landing allowance).

---

#### Part (b) — Section and Elevation (7 marks)

**Section (4 marks):**

The section must be cut along the central east-west line passing through:
- Main entrance door
- Waiting Hall → Doctor rooms → Corridor
- Staircase at rear

Mark the following levels (from bottom to top):

| Level | Height above GL |
|---|---|
| Foundation bottom | −1.2 m |
| Plinth top (FFL) | +0.6 m |
| Sill level (window base) | +1.5 m |
| Lintel level (window top) | +2.7 m |
| Floor-to-floor | +3.3 m |
| Roof slab top | +3.45 m (3.3 + 0.15 slab) |
| Parapet top | +4.35 m (3.45 + 0.9 parapet) |

Show in section:
- Plinth beam 230 × 300 mm at GL
- Lintel band at 2.7 m
- Roof slab 125 mm with weathering course
- Staircase with **risers, treads, landing** clearly visible
- Door at +0.6 m (start) to +2.7 m (top) — 2100 mm high
- Window from +0.9 m (sill) to +2.1 m (lintel)
- Ground line (GL) hatching

**Front (South) Elevation (3 marks):**

- Show parapet line at top: 900 mm above roof
- Eaves line: at roof slab bottom
- Main entrance: central, 1500 × 2400 mm door
- Two windows flanking entrance on ground floor: 1500 × 1500 mm
- Show sunshade projections: 750 mm horizontal projection above windows
- Steps from GL to plinth: 3 risers × 150 mm = 450 mm rise, 300 mm tread → **1.2 m wide steps**
- Textures:
  - Walls: dotted plaster texture
  - Plinth: cement plaster (darker shade)
  - Windows: glass pane (hatched lines)
- Scale: 1:50 (same as plan)

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
> Plan a **Bank Branch** on a plot of size **24 m × 18 m** with a 12 m wide road on the west. The bank must include a **Banking Hall, Manager Cabin, Cash Cabin, Strong Room (Vault), 2 Counters, and Toilets**.
>
> **(a)** Draw the **Plan** at sill level with full dimensioning, door and window schedule. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Draw the **Section** through the Strong Room and the **Front (West) Elevation** with all level marks. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution Hints — Question B

**Part (a):** Apply the same workflow — setbacks, zoning, room schedule, dimensions. The **Strong Room** must be placed on the **rear wall** (away from public access) with **150 mm RCC wall**, **steel door with IBMer lock**, and **no windows** (only a ventilator at high level).

**Part (b):** The section must pass through the strong room to show the **150 mm RCC wall thickness** as a key detail. The elevation should show the **banking hall large glass façade** for visibility, with a discrete back entrance for staff.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Triggers:**
>
> - **❌ Drawing the section line through the elevation area instead of through the staircase** — Examiner deducts 1–2 marks.
> - **❌ Forgetting to show the plinth offset in plan (showing external wall edge-to-edge instead of wall-thickness offset at plinth level)** — Deduct 0.5 mark.
> - **❌ Not marking the sill level (900 mm) and lintel level (2100 mm) on the section** — Deduct 1 mark.
> - **❌ Writing the scale as 1:50 without drawing a scale bar** — Deduct 0.5 mark.
> - **❌ Skipping the title block or north arrow** — Deduct 0.5 mark.
> - **❌ Drawing toilet doors opening into a public area without a lobby** — Deduct 1 mark.
> - **❌ Not verifying 2R + G = 580–650 mm on staircase** — Deduct 0.5 mark.
> - **❌ Elevation not matching plan horizontally (window widths differing)** — Deduct 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Last-Minute Revision for KTU Exam:**

- [ ] Plan section is taken at **900 mm above FFL (sill level)** — NOT at floor level.
- [ ] Section is a **vertical cut** through the entire building; elevation is a **non-cut external view**.
- [ ] **Plinth height = 600 mm** (standard for public buildings).
- [ ] **Sill = 900 mm above FFL**; **Lintel = 2100 mm above FFL**; **Door height = 2400 mm (public)**.
- [ ] **Staircase rule: 2R + G = 600 mm (ideal); range 580–650 mm.**
- [ ] **Public staircase width ≥ 1500 mm**; **corridor ≥ 1800 mm**.
- [ ] **Ramp gradient: 1:12 (preferred) to 1:15 (max)**; **ramp width ≥ 1200 mm**.
- [ ] **Toilet ratio: 1 WC per 50 men / 1 WC per 25 women** (NBC 2016).
- [ ] **External wall = 230 mm; internal wall = 115 mm (brick)**.
- [ ] **Roof slab = 125–150 mm RCC**; **parapet = 900 mm above roof**.
- [ ] **Drawings follow IS 962:1989 + SP 46:1988** — title block, north arrow, scale bar mandatory.
- [ ] **Setbacks per KMBR 2019** depend on plot and road width; always state assumed setbacks.
- [ ] **Strong room (bank): 150 mm RCC wall, no windows, steel door.**
- [ ] **Window area = 1/6 to 1/8 of floor area**; **ventilation = 1/16 minimum**.
- [ ] **Head room ≥ 3000 mm (resi) / ≥ 3600 mm (public).**
- [ ] **Bubble diagram → Block plan → Final plan** is the correct sequencing.
- [ ] **Sunshade projection = 600–750 mm** for windows in Kerala climate.
- [ ] **KMBR setback front = 3–6 m** depending on road width.
- [ ] **Always show foundation in section** with depth marked below GL.
- [ ] **Plinth beam 230×300 mm at GL** is mandatory for load transfer.
- [ ] **Lintel band 230×150 mm at lintel level** is mandatory.
- [ ] **Match elevations horizontally with plan openings** — common 1-mark deduction if mismatched.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plan and prepare plumbing and sanitary drawings of a building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL408 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plan and prepare plumbing and sanitary drawings of a building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plumbing and Sanitary Drawings** are the set of working drawings that graphically represent the complete network of pipelines, fittings, fixtures, and ancillaries required to convey **potable water** into a building (**supply system**) and to remove **waste water**, **soil waste**, and **vent gases** out of the building (**drainage system**), prepared in conformity with **IS 1742**, **IS 1172**, **IS 2065**, and **IS 5329**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme vocabulary, this experiment evaluates the student's ability to **read**, **plan**, and **draft** a building's **above-ground (soil & waste stack) system** and **below-ground (sewer & septic tank) system**, including the corresponding **plan view**, **sectional elevation**, and **isometric schematic**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Building as a Human Body"

Think of a building as a living human being:
- The **arteries** are the **water supply pipes** (carrying clean water to every fixture — like the heart pumping blood).
- The **veins** are the **waste/soil pipes** (carrying used water back to the septic tank — the kidneys).
- The **lungs** are the **vent pipes** (letting foul gases escape and air enter to break the vacuum).
- The **toilet, wash basin, sink, gully trap, manhole, septic tank** are the **organs** performing specific excretion tasks.

If the *arteries* are blocked → no water reaches you. If the *veins* are choked → sewage backs up. If the *lungs* (vents) are sealed → the traps get siphoned and foul gases enter rooms. Therefore, every drawing must balance all three networks.

## 1.3 Classification of Plumbing Systems

> [!NOTE]
> A KTU board examiner expects you to **list and differentiate** these systems — a 3-mark favourite.

| S.No | System | Description | Typical Building Type |
|------|--------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Single Stack System** | One vertical pipe carries soil + waste + vent | Low-rise (< 5 floors) |
| 2 | **One-Pipe System** | One soil stack; one waste stack joined to it via a deep seal trap; separate vent stack optional | Residential buildings |
| 3 | **One-Pipe Partially Ventilated System** | Same as one-pipe, but only WC branch is vented | Multi-storey residential |
| 4 | **Two-Pipe (Fully Ventilated) System** | Separate soil stack + separate waste stack + separate vent stack | Hospitals, Hotels, High-rise |
| 5 | **Single Stack Ventilated (De-Sharman)** | Modified single stack with relief vent at the base — 50% size of main stack | Modern high-rise |

## 1.4 Sanitary Fixtures — The "End Organs"

| Fixture | Symbol | Trap Used | Pipe Connection |
|---------|--------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Water Closet (Indian/Western)** | WC | S-Trap / P-Trap | Soil pipe (100 mm Ø) |
| **Wash Basin** | WB | Bottle / P-Trap | Waste pipe (40 mm Ø) |
| **Kitchen Sink** | KS | P-Trap / Nahani Trap | Waste pipe (50 mm Ø) |
| **Bath Tub** | BT | Built-in trap | Waste pipe (50 mm Ø) |
| **Urinal** | UR | Integral trap | Waste pipe (50 mm Ø) |
| **Floor Drain / Nahani Trap** | FD | Floor trap with grating | Waste pipe (50–75 mm Ø) |
| **Bidet** | BD | Integral | Waste pipe (40 mm Ø) |

## 1.5 KTU-Mandated IS Code List (Highlighted for Viva)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Name the relevant IS Code"** is the single most common viva question in this experiment. Memorise the bolded codes below.

- **IS 1172 : 1993 (Reaffirmed)** — Basic requirements for water supply, drainage and sanitation (gives per-capita water demand = **135 lpcd** for residences).
- **IS 2065 : 1983** — Code of practice for water supply in buildings.
- **IS 5329 : 1983** — Code of practice for sanitary pipe work above ground.
- **IS 1742 : 1983** — Code of practice for building drainage.
- **IS 2470 (Part 1 & 2)** — Design of septic tanks.
- **IS 4111 (Part 1–4)** — Ancillary structures (manholes, inspection chambers).
- **IS 458** — Precast concrete pipes.
- **IS 1536** — Centrifugally cast (spun) iron pipes for water mains.
- **IS 1239 (Part 1 & 2)** — Mild steel tubes and tubulars.
- **IS 2548 / IS 2963** — P-trap and S-trap for water closets.
- **IS 1726** — Cast iron manhole covers.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Design Philosophy — The "Three C's" of Plumbing Design

Every plumbing drawing must satisfy three cardinal rules:

1. **Clean water in** (under pressure, no contamination, no air-locks).
2. **Containment of waste** (no leakage, no backflow, no siphonage of traps).
3. **Continuous ventilation** (equalise pressure, prevent trap seal loss, exhaust gases above roof).

## 2.2 Pipe Gradient — The Slope That Drives Flow by Gravity

> [!NOTE]
> **Gravity is the engine of drainage**. The pipe slope is therefore not optional — it is structural.

| Pipe Type | Minimum Gradient | Reasoning |
|-----------|------------------|-----------|
| **Soil pipe (horizontal)** | **1 in 40** (2.5 %) | Carries solids — needs self-cleansing velocity ≥ 0.6 m/s |
| **Waste pipe (horizontal)** | **1 in 60** (1.67 %) | Carries liquids only — slower flow tolerable |
| **Rainwater pipe (horizontal)** | **1 in 100** (1.0 %) | Carries stormwater — high volume, low solid content |
| **Sewer (outside building)** | **1 in 60 to 1 in 200** | Designed for self-cleansing velocity 0.6–0.9 m/s |

**Derivation of self-cleansing velocity** (using Manning's equation — see §3.1) gives $V \geq 0.6 \text{ m/s}$ for 100 mm Ø pipe at 1:40 gradient.

## 2.3 Trap Seal — The Watery Barrier Against Sewer Gas

A **trap** is a U-shaped or S-shaped pipe section that retains a small quantity of water (**seal = 25 mm to 75 mm**) which physically blocks foul gases from entering occupied rooms.

- **P-Trap** — used with **wall-hung** WCs; horizontal outlet to wall.
- **S-Trap** — used with **floor-mounted** Indian WCs; vertical outlet to floor.
- **Q-Trap / Gully Trap** — used at the junction of bath/WB waste and floor drain.
- **Nahani Trap** — floor-level grating trap (modern, easy to clean).
- **Bottle Trap** — used under wash basins (compact, 32 mm seal).
- **Intercepting Trap** — installed at the last manhole to prevent reverse flow of sewer gases.

> [!WARNING]
> **Trap seal loss** happens when **siphonage** or **back-pressure** occurs in the stack. Prevention: provide a **vent pipe** (anti-siphonage pipe) within 1 m of the trap outlet.

## 2.4 Vent Pipe — The "Lung" of the System

- **Anti-siphonage pipe** (50 mm Ø for 100 mm soil stack) — fitted at the last fixture of the stack.
- **Vent stack / Main vent** — 75 mm Ø for high-rise, terminating **2 m above** the top-most fixture with a **cowlscreen**.
- **Relief vent** — at the base of a deep stack to relieve pressure surges.
- **Yoke vent** — joins the soil stack and vent stack at the lowest branch.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All numerical problems in KTU ESE are solved using the formulas below. **Memorise every row.**

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Unit | IS Code Reference |
|------|-----------|---------|------|-------------------|
| 1 | Per-capita water demand | $Q_d = P \times q$ | litres/day | IS 1172 |
| 2 | Peak demand factor | $Q_p = 1.5 \times Q_d$ (residential); 2.0 (offices) | litres/day | IS 2065 |
| 3 | Pipe discharge (Hazen-Williams) | $V = 0.85 \times C_H \times R^{0.63} \times S^{0.54}$ | m/s | IS 2065 |
| 4 | Manning's velocity (sewer) | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | m/s | IS 1742 |
| 5 | Pipe discharge | $Q = A \times V$ | m³/s | — |
| 6 | Hydraulic radius | $R = \dfrac{A}{P}$ (A = area, P = wetted perimeter) | m | — |
| 7 | Fixture unit to LPS conversion (empirical table) | See Table 2.6 below | — | IS 2065 |
| 8 | Septic tank liquid capacity | $V_{liq} = P \times q \times t_r$ (t_r = 24–48 h retention) | litres | IS 2470 |
| 9 | Septic tank length:width ratio | **2:1 to 3:1** | m | IS 2470 |
| 10 | Minimum manhole size | **0.9 m × 1.2 m** (rectangular, for depth ≤ 0.9 m) | m | IS 4111 |
| 11 | Manhole spacing | 30 m (≤ 1.5 m deep); 45 m (> 1.5 m) | m | IS 1742 |
| 12 | Gradient of sewer | 1 in 60 to 1 in 200 | — | IS 1742 |
| 13 | Pipe gradient (slope) | $S = \dfrac{1}{N}$ where N is the denominator | — | IS 5329 |
| 14 | Self-cleansing velocity | $V_{sc} \geq 0.6$ m/s (sanitary), $\geq 0.9$ m/s (storm) | m/s | IS 1742 |
| 15 | Trap seal depth | $h_{seal} = 25$ mm (min) to $75$ mm (max) | mm | IS 5329 |

> ⚠ **Markdown Safety Note**: All absolute values in the table use parentheses `(...)` or words like "min" instead of `|x|` vertical pipes to preserve table formatting.

## 2.6 Fixture Unit (FU) Loading Table — The KTU Examiner's Favourite

| Fixture | FU (Metric) | Pipe Size (mm) |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| Indian WC | **10** | 100 |
| Western WC | **6** | 100 |
| Wash Basin | **2** | 40 |
| Kitchen Sink | **3** | 50 |
| Bath Tub | **4** | 50 |
| Shower | **2** | 40 |
| Urinal (wall) | **3** | 50 |
| Floor Drain | **2** | 50 |
| Bidet | **3** | 40 |

**Total FU** is summed for the stack; pipe diameter is then read from the **loading table (IS 2065 Table 6)**.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Residences (B+G+1 to G+3)**: Single stack system is most economical.
- **Hotels/Hospitals (G+5 and above)**: Two-pipe fully ventilated system avoids trap siphonage.
- **Smart Cities 2025**: Building Management Systems (BMS) integrate plumbing SCADA — drawings must now show **IoT sensor tapping points**.
- **Green Buildings (GRIHA / LEED)**: Rainwater harvesting, dual-flush WCs, greywater reuse — all drawn in the sanitary plan.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Self-Cleansing Velocity Using Manning's Equation

Manning's equation for uniform flow in a circular pipe flowing **just full** is:

$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

**Step 1** — For a circular pipe of diameter $D$ flowing full:
- Wetted area: $A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- Wetted perimeter: $P = \pi D$
- Hydraulic radius: $R = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{\pi D^2 / 4}{\pi D} = \dfrac{D}{4}$

**Step 2** — Substitute $R = D/4$ into Manning's equation:

$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} \left(\dfrac{D}{4}\right)^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

**Step 3** — For a typical 100 mm Ø soil pipe with $n = 0.013$ (cast iron) and gradient $S = 1/40 = 0.025$:

$$V = \dfrac{1}{0.013} \times \left(\dfrac{0.10}{4}\right)^{2/3} \times (0.025)^{1/2}$$

**Step 4** — Evaluate each term separately (exhaustive, no skipping):
- $\dfrac{D}{4} = \dfrac{0.10}{4} = 0.025$ m
- $(0.025)^{2/3} = (2.5 \times 10^{-2})^{0.6667} \approx 0.0855$
- $(0.025)^{1/2} = 0.1581$
- $\dfrac{1}{n} = \dfrac{1}{0.013} = 76.92$

**Step 5** — Multiply:

$$V = 76.92 \times 0.0855 \times 0.1581 \approx 1.04 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6** — Conclusion: $V = 1.04 \text{ m/s} \geq 0.6 \text{ m/s}$. Hence a 100 mm pipe at 1:40 gradient is **self-cleansing** ✓.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Water Demand for a Hostel Building

**Problem:** A hostel has 120 students. Calculate the daily and peak water demand. Design the storage tank capacity (assuming 50 % of peak demand is to be stored).

**Given:**
- Population, $P = 120$
- Per-capita demand (institutional, IS 1172), $q = 135$ lpcd
- Peak factor, $K_p = 1.8$ (hostel type)

**Step 1 — Daily Demand:**

$$Q_d = P \times q = 120 \times 135 = 16{,}200 \text{ litres/day}$$

**Step 2 — Peak Demand:**

$$Q_p = K_p \times Q_d = 1.8 \times 16{,}200 = 29{,}160 \text{ litres/day}$$

**Step 3 — Storage capacity (50 % of peak):**

$$V_{tank} = 0.5 \times Q_p = 0.5 \times 29{,}160 = 14{,}580 \text{ litres} \approx 15 \text{ kL}$$

**Step 4 — Tank dimensions** (assume depth 1.5 m, square plan):
- Plan area = $15{,}000 / 1.5 / 1000 = 10$ m²
- Side = $\sqrt{10} \approx 3.16$ m

Adopt **3.2 m × 3.2 m × 1.5 m** underground sump with **overhead tank of 50 % volume (7.5 kL)**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Septic Tank Design (IS 2470)

**Problem:** Design a septic tank for 50 users in a rural school.

**Given:**
- Users, $N = 50$
- Per-capita sewage, $q = 135$ lpcd (assume 80 % of water supplied)
- Retention time, $t_r = 24$ h
- Cleaning interval, $t_c = 365$ days
- Sludge accumulation rate, $r = 30$ lpcd (IS 2470)

**Step 1 — Liquid volume (V₁):**

$$V_1 = N \times q \times t_r = 50 \times 135 \times 1.0 \text{ (in m³, since 135/1000)} = 6.75 \text{ m³}$$

Wait — careful: convert litres to m³ first.

$$V_1 = 50 \times 0.135 \times 24 = 162 \text{ m³}$$

That is excessive for 50 people. Re-check IS 2470: actually $q$ is taken as **per capita sewage flow**, and for 50 users retention of 24 h:

**Step 1 (corrected) — Liquid volume:**

$$V_1 = N \times q_s \times t_r = 50 \times 0.135 \text{ m³/day} \times 1 \text{ day} = 6.75 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 2 — Sludge volume (V₂):**

$$V_2 = N \times r \times t_c = 50 \times 0.00003 \text{ m³/d} \times 365 = 0.55 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 3 — Total volume:**

$$V_{total} = V_1 + V_2 = 6.75 + 0.55 = 7.30 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 4 — Adopt dimensions** (L : B = 2 : 1, liquid depth = 1.2 m, freeboard 0.3 m):
- Width, $B = 1.5$ m
- Length, $L = 2 \times B = 3.0$ m
- Total depth = $1.2 + 0.3 = 1.5$ m
- Volume provided = $3.0 \times 1.5 \times 1.2 = 5.40$ m³

Since $5.40 < 7.30$, revise to $B = 1.7$ m, $L = 3.4$ m → volume $= 3.4 \times 1.7 \times 1.2 = 6.94$ m³. Still short. Adopt **B = 1.8 m, L = 3.6 m** → $3.6 \times 1.8 \times 1.2 = 7.78$ m³ ≥ 7.30 m³ ✓.

**Final Septic Tank: 3.6 m × 1.8 m × 1.5 m (total depth)**

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Pipe Sizing Based on Fixture Units

For a soil stack serving 8 WCs, 6 WBs and 4 Kitchens (typical G+2 floor):

| Fixture | Quantity | FU each | Sub-total FU |
|---------|----------|---------|--------------|
| WC | 8 | 10 | 80 |
| WB | 6 | 2 | 12 |
| Kitchen Sink | 4 | 3 | 12 |
| **Total** | | | **104** |

**Per IS 2065 Table 6** (loading for vertical stacks):
- For 100 mm Ø soil stack — maximum loading = **350 FU** ✓
- For 75 mm Ø vent stack — acceptable.

Hence **100 mm Ø soil stack, 75 mm Ø vent stack, 50 mm Ø waste stack** is sufficient.

## 3.5 Gradient Calculation — Worked Out

A horizontal soil pipe runs from the building to the septic tank manhole, distance 12 m. Required gradient = 1 in 40.

**Step 1 — Drop required:**

$$\text{Drop} = \dfrac{L}{N} = \dfrac{12{,}000 \text{ mm}}{40} = 300 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — If the pipe starts at GL – 0.6 m and manhole is 1.2 m deep:**
- Invert level of pipe at building: – 0.80 m
- Invert level of pipe at manhole: – 0.80 m – 0.30 m = – 1.10 m
- This matches the manhole invert (1.2 m depth, + 0.10 m benching allowance) ✓.

- Body depth: 75 mm

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the term "plumbing drawing" and list any four plumbing IS codes.**

**Model Answer:**
A plumbing drawing is a graphical representation of the network of pipes, fittings, fixtures, and ancillaries that convey potable water to and wastewater from a building, prepared per **IS 1172** and **IS 1742**.

Four IS codes:
1. **IS 1172 : 1993** – Basic requirements for water supply, drainage and sanitation.
2. **IS 1742 : 1983** – Building drainage.
3. **IS 2065 : 1983** – Water supply in buildings.
4. **IS 5329 : 1983** – Sanitary pipework above ground.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | P-Trap | S-Trap |
|---------|--------|--------|
| **Shape** | U-shaped, outlet horizontal to wall | S-shaped, outlet vertical to floor |
| **Use** | Wall-hung Western WC | Floor-mounted Indian WC |
