## 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a)** Plan and prepare a **plumbing layout** for a residential single-storey building (2 BHK) with the following fixtures: 1 Indian WC, 1 Western WC, 2 wash basins, 1 kitchen sink, 1 bath tub, 1 floor drain, and 1 gully trap. Show the system in **plan view, isometric view, and vertical section**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **septic tank** for a colony of 200 persons, with per-capita sewage of 130 lpcd and sludge accumulation of 30 lpcd. Assume cleaning interval 1 year and retention period 24 hours. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1 — Fixture list and FU loading:**

| Fixture | Quantity | FU each | Sub-total |
|---------|----------|---------|-----------|
| Indian WC | 1 | 10 | 10 |
| Western WC | 1 | 6 | 6 |
| Wash Basin | 2 | 2 | 4 |
| Kitchen Sink | 1 | 3 | 3 |
| Bath Tub | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| Floor Drain | 1 | 2 | 2 |
| **Total** | | | **29** |

**Step 2 — System selection:**
- Total FU = 29 → low-rise, hence adopt **Single Stack System** (most economical).
- Soil stack diameter = **100 mm** (FU capacity of 100 mm stack is 350 FU, hence 29 FU is well within limit).
- Vent stack = **50 mm** (anti-siphonage pipe at last fixture of each stack).
- Waste stack = **50 mm**.

**Step 3 — Layout rules to be observed:**
- Soil pipe from WC joins soil stack directly.
- Bath, basin, sink and floor drain connect to **gully trap**, which discharges into the **soil stack** via the gully trap (since it is a single stack system).
- Soil stack is **offset at the base** through a **45° bend** (not 90°), and taken underground at 1:40 gradient.
- Vent stack terminates **2 m above** the roof with a **cowlscreen**.

**Step 4 — Isometric view:**
- Show all branches as **double-line symbols** with slopes.
- Indicate the gradient direction by an **arrow**.
- Mark the **direction of flow** at every junction.

**Step 5 — Vertical section:**
- Show the **stack passing through the roof slab** with a sleeve.
- Indicate the **cowlscreen** at top.
- Mark the **ground floor slab** level and the **GL** at the base.
- Show the **inspection chamber** with **0.9 m × 1.2 m** size.

> Drawing must be on **A1 sheet** with title block (KTU practical exam convention).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** $N = 200$, $q = 130$ lpcd, $r = 30$ lpcd, $t_r = 24$ h, $t_c = 1$ year.

**Step 1 — Liquid volume (V₁):**
$$V_1 = N \times q \times t_r = 200 \times 0.130 \text{ m³/d} \times 1 \text{ d} = 26.0 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 2 — Sludge volume (V₂):**
$$V_2 = N \times r \times t_c = 200 \times 0.00003 \text{ m³/d} \times 365 = 2.19 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 3 — Total volume:**
$$V_{total} = 26.0 + 2.19 = 28.19 \text{ m³}$$

**Step 4 — Adopt dimensions** (L:B = 2:1, liquid depth 1.5 m):
- Width, $B = 2.5$ m
- Length, $L = 5.0$ m
- Liquid volume = $5.0 \times 2.5 \times 1.5 = 18.75$ m³ → insufficient.
- Revise: $B = 3.0$ m, $L = 6.0$ m → $6.0 \times 3.0 \times 1.5 = 27.0$ m³ → still short.
- Revise: $B = 3.1$ m, $L = 6.1$ m → $6.1 \times 3.1 \times 1.5 = 28.36$ m³ ✓
- Add freeboard 0.3 m → **total depth = 1.8 m**.

**Step 5 — Final septic tank dimensions:**
$$\boxed{L \times B \times D = 6.10 \text{ m} \times 3.10 \text{ m} \times 1.80 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 6 — Inlet/Outlet:**
- Inlet T-pipe: 100 mm Ø, submerged 300 mm below scum.
- Outlet T-pipe: 100 mm Ø, submerged 200 mm below scum.
- Baffle wall: in middle, with submerged slot 100 mm × 200 mm at mid-depth.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Explain the **Single Stack, One-Pipe, and Two-Pipe systems** with neat sketches. State the conditions under which each is adopted. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-storey hostel has 60 students. Calculate the **daily water requirement, peak demand, and storage tank capacity** as per IS 1172. Design the **overhead tank size** for half of the peak demand. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Single Stack System** — One vertical pipe (100 mm Ø) carries soil + waste water and is also vented. The WC, bath, basin, sink all connect to this single stack. Traps at each fixture prevent gas entry. **Used in low-rise (≤ 3 floors) buildings where traps are unlikely to siphon.**

**One-Pipe System** — One soil stack + one waste stack. The waste stack joins the soil stack at the bottom through a **gully trap** (deep seal, 75 mm). A separate vent stack is provided. **Used in multi-storey residential buildings (G+3 to G+5).**

**Two-Pipe (Fully Ventilated) System** — Two completely independent stacks: a **soil stack** (100 mm) and a **waste stack** (50 mm). Each is provided with a separate **vent pipe** (75 mm). All WCs and gully traps discharge into the soil stack. All basins, sinks, baths discharge into the waste stack. **Used in hospitals, hotels, high-rise buildings where trap siphonage is a risk.**

**Conditions:**
- **Single Stack** → cost priority, low-rise, short stacks.
- **One-Pipe** → balance of cost and performance for residential.
- **Two-Pipe** → absolute safety, high-rise, heavy usage.

> Sketches must show stack diameters, vent positions, and trap locations.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:** $P = 60$, $q = 135$ lpcd (institutional), peak factor = 1.8.

**Step 1 — Daily demand:**
$$Q_d = 60 \times 135 = 8{,}100 \text{ litres/day}$$

**Step 2 — Peak demand:**
$$Q_p = 1.8 \times 8{,}100 = 14{,}580 \text{ litres/day}$$

**Step 3 — Storage tank** (50 % of peak as per IS 1172 recommendations):
$$V_{tank} = 0.5 \times 14{,}580 = 7{,}290 \text{ litres} \approx 7.5 \text{ kL}$$

**Step 4 — OHT dimensions** (depth 1.2 m, square plan):
- Plan area = $7{,}500 / (1.2 \times 1000) = 6.25$ m²
- Side = $\sqrt{6.25} = 2.5$ m

$$\boxed{\text{OHT: } 2.5 \text{ m} \times 2.5 \text{ m} \times 1.2 \text{ m} = 7.5 \text{ kL}}$$

**Step 5 — UGT (underground sump, holds full 14,580 L at 2.0 m depth):**
- Area = $14{,}580 / (2.0 \times 1000) = 7.29$ m²
- Side ≈ 2.7 m

$$\boxed{\text{UGT: } 2.7 \text{ m} \times 2.7 \text{ m} \times 2.0 \text{ m} \approx 14.5 \text{ kL}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons for losing marks in plumbing & sanitary drawing questions:**
> 1. **Missing cowl on vent stack** — examiner deducts **1 mark** if the vent does not extend 2 m above the roof.
> 2. **Wrong gradient** — using 1:100 for soil pipe (must be 1:40) costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **No inspection chamber at junction** — must be shown at every change of direction or branch.
> 4. **Omitting trap at fixture** — every fixture MUST show a trap; forgetting it loses **1 mark each**.
> 5. **No title block / scale / north arrow** — KTU mandates these on every drawing; missing them costs **1–2 marks**.
> 6. **Confusing soil pipe (100 mm) with waste pipe (50 mm)** — diameter labelling is critical.
> 7. **Septic tank wrong L:B ratio** — must be 2:1 to 3:1; a square tank is wrong.
> 8. **Forgetting freeboard** in septic tank — minimum 0.3 m must be shown.
> 9. **Manhole size not as per IS 4111** — use 0.9 m × 1.2 m, not 0.6 m × 0.6 m for deep manholes.
> 10. **No legend/symbol table** on the sheet — examiners consider this a presentation flaw.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision before entering the lab / exam hall:**

- ✅ **Plumbing drawing = water supply (OW/HW) + drainage (Soil/Waste) + venting + fixture layout.**
- ✅ **Five systems to remember**: Single stack, One-pipe, One-pipe partially ventilated, Two-pipe fully ventilated, Single stack ventilated (De-Sharman).
- ✅ **Key IS codes**: IS 1172, IS 1742, IS 2065, IS 5329, IS 2470, IS 4111, IS 2548, IS 2963.
- ✅ **Pipe gradients**: Soil 1:40, Waste 1:60, Rainwater 1:100, Sewer 1:60–1:200.
- ✅ **Standard diameters**: Soil 100 mm, Waste 50 mm, Vent 50–75 mm, Rainwater 100 mm, WC 100 mm, Wash basin 40 mm.
- ✅ **Trap types**: P, S, Q, Bottle, Nahani, Intercepting — each has a specific use.
- ✅ **Trap seal**: minimum 25 mm, maximum 75 mm, ideal 50 mm.
- ✅ **Vent stack** must extend **2 m above roof** with **cowlscreen**.
- ✅ **Manhole size**: 0.9 × 1.2 m (rectangular) for depth ≤ 0.9 m; 1.2 × 1.5 m (circular 1.5 m Ø) for depth > 0.9 m.
- ✅ **Septic tank L:B ratio = 2:1 to 3:1**; L ≥ 2 × B; freeboard 0.3 m.
- ✅ **Septic tank** typically two-compartment (60:40) for ≥ 100 users.
- ✅ **Soak pit**: 1.5 m Ø, 3 m deep, filled with brickbats; one soak pit per 10 users (approx).
- ✅ **Per-capita water demand** (IS 1172): Residential 135 lpcd; Schools 45 lpcd; Hospitals 340 lpcd; Hotels 180 lpcd.
- ✅ **Peak factor**: 1.5 to 2.0 (residential 1.5; institutional 1.8; hospitals 1.2).
- ✅ **Storage capacity**: 50 % of peak demand (1/3 UGT + 1/6 OHT) — or as per NBC.
- ✅ **Pipe offsets** at stack base: use 45° or 88° long-radius bend (NEVER 90° sharp bend).
- ✅ **No drain pipe** should pass through a **living room or kitchen**.
- ✅ **Anti-siphonage pipe** required if stack height > 4 floors or fixtures > 5 per floor.
- ✅ **Water closet trap** must be **within 1 m** of vent pipe connection.
- ✅ **Inspection chamber** at every 30 m on straight run and at every bend.
- ✅ **Rainwater pipe** must not be connected to the **soil stack** (separate system).
- ✅ **Kitchen sink** should not be connected to a **floor trap**; it has its own P-trap.
- ✅ **Symbols, legend, scale, north arrow, title block** are **mandatory** on every KTU drawing sheet.
- ✅ **Use A1 sheet** for KTU ESE practical; linework must be uniform; arrows on flow direction.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plan and prepare electrical drawings of a building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL408 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Plan and prepare electrical drawings of a building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electrical Drawings of a Building** are a set of precise graphical representations prepared on standardized architectural plans that depict the complete electrical installation layout of a structure, including the position, type, and interconnection of all electrical fixtures, wiring routes, switchboards, distribution points, earthing systems, and service connections, in strict accordance with the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** — primarily **IS 732:2019** (Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations) and **IS 2302:2007** (Graphical Symbols for Electrical Engineering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (PCCEL408):**
> *Electrical drawings of a building constitute the engineering documentation that translates the architect's floor plan into an electrically functional layout, ensuring safety, load distribution, energy efficiency, and conformity with the National Electrical Code (NEC) of India.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Nervous System of a Building"

Imagine a human body:
- The **brain** = Main Distribution Board (MDB) / Main Switch Board
- The **spinal cord** = Sub-Distribution Boards (SDBs) and rising mains
- The **nerves** = Branch circuits and wiring through conduits
- The **sensory organs** = Switches, sockets, light points, fans
- The **skin (protective layer)** = Insulation, earthing, and conduit sheathing

Just as a neurologist maps the nervous system before surgery, a **Civil/Electrical engineer plans the electrical "nervous system"** of a building **before construction begins** so that every wall, slab, and switch is pre-planned. The drawing is the **X-ray and surgical map combined**.

---

## 1.3 Why Civil Engineers Must Learn This

> [!NOTE]
> Although the execution is done by electrical engineers, **Civil Engineers** in KTU's PCCEL408 lab are trained to:
> - **Coordinate** electrical conduits with **structural elements** (beams, slabs, columns) to avoid clashes.
> - **Pre-embed** conduit pipes in slabs/walls during RCC work (since post-construction cutting weakens concrete).
> - **Verify** the architect's plan is electrically feasible and code-compliant.
> - **Read and interpret** electrical drawings submitted by consultants for site execution.

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants, Standards & Metrics

The following standards govern building electrical drawings in India:

| Standard Code | Title | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **IS 732:2019** | Code of Practice for Electrical Wiring Installations | Wiring design rules |
| **IS 2302:2007** | Graphical Symbols for Electrical Engineering | Symbol library |
| **IS 4648:1968** | Guide for Electrical Layout in Residential Buildings | Domestic planning |
| **IS 8884:1978** | Code of Practice for Installation of Electric Bells | Bell circuits |
| **IE Rules 1956** | Indian Electricity Rules | Legal compliance |
| **NEC 2011** | National Electrical Code of India | Comprehensive design |

Mandatory values to memorize:
- **Ceiling fan height** above floor: **2.75 m (minimum)**
- **Switch board height** above floor: **1.2 m to 1.5 m**
- **Socket outlet height**: **0.45 m to 1.2 m** (general), **1.2 m** (kitchen/counter)
- **Minimum clear space** between gas pipeline and electrical conduit: **300 mm (horizontal)**, **150 mm (vertical)**
- **Standard ceiling fan sweep sizes**: **900 mm, 1200 mm, 1400 mm**

---

## 1.5 Types of Electrical Drawings Prepared for a Building

A complete electrical drawing package typically contains **six distinct drawings**:

1. **Electrical Layout Plan (Floor-wise)** — Shows switchboards, fan points, light points, socket outlets on the architectural plan.
2. **Single Line Diagram (SLD)** — Represents the entire power flow from utility supply to each load using single lines and standard symbols.
3. **Wiring Diagram** — Shows the actual conductor connections between switches, holders, and loads.
4. **Conduit Layout / Cable Routing Plan** — Indicates the path of concealed/surface conduits in slabs and walls.
5. **Earthing Layout** — Depicts the position of earth pits, earth strips, and earth continuity conductors.
6. **Load Schedule & Distribution Chart** — A tabular sheet showing connected load per circuit, phase balancing, and protective device ratings.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Electrical Symbols (As Per IS 2302)

Electrical symbols are the **alphabet of electrical drawings**. Every symbol is a small pictogram that, once understood, allows the engineer to read an entire installation from a single sheet.

### 2.1.1 Symbols for Wiring Accessories

| Symbol (Description) | Pictorial Representation |
|---|---|
| **SPST Switch (1-way, 6A)** | A line cutting through a small circle with a single terminal |
| **2-Way Switch (SPDT)** | Line with two branching terminals |
| **Bell Push** | Small circle with "B" inside |
| **Ceiling Rose** | Solid filled circle with a stem line |
| **Socket Outlet (3-pin, 6A)** | Hollow circle with two short parallel lines inside |
| **Socket Outlet (3-pin, 16A)** | Hollow circle with two short parallel lines (heavy) |
| **Switched Socket** | Socket with a small line (switch) over it |
| **Earth Point** | Three decreasing horizontal lines (ground symbol) |
| **Neutral Point** | Solid filled triangle or labelled "N" |

### 2.1.2 Symbols for Luminaires (Light Fittings)

| Luminaire Type | Symbol |
|---|---|
| **Single Fluorescent Tube** | Rectangle with diagonal hatching |
| **Bulkhead Fitting** | Circle with internal cross |
| **Ceiling Fan (Regulator)** | Circle with three curved blades and a square (regulator) |
| **Exhaust Fan** | Circle with two arrows showing airflow |
| **Wall Bracket Lamp** | Small filled circle with an angled line (bracket) |
| **LED Panel / Downlighter** | Inverted triangle inside a circle |
| **Emergency Light** | Lamp symbol with "EM" annotation |

### 2.1.3 Symbols for Distribution Equipment

- **Main Switch (MS / Isolator)** = A square with a single line entering and exiting, with a "T" handle symbol.
- **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** = A square with a small "X" or angled line inside.
- **MCCB (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker)** = Larger square with the same but heavier representation.
- **ELCB / RCCB (Earth Leakage / Residual Current Circuit Breaker)** = Square with a "delta" test button symbol.
- **Distribution Board (DB)** = A larger rectangle, sub-divided into smaller cells for each MCB.

---

## 2.2 Standardized Wiring Colour Code (As Per IS 732:2019)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory colour coding (must appear on every drawing's legend block):**

- **Phase (Line / Live) — Red, Yellow, or Blue** (one per phase in 3-phase systems)
- **Neutral — Black**
- **Earth / Protective Conductor (PE) — Green or Green-with-Yellow-stripe**
- **DC Positive — Red**
- **DC Negative — Blue/Black**

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.3.1 Load Calculation Formulas

| Purpose | Formula | Variables / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Connected Load** | $L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (N_i \times W_i)$ | $N_i$ = number of points, $W_i$ = wattage of each |
| **Demand Factor** | $DF = \dfrac{L_{max\_demand}}{L_{total\_connected}}$ | Always < 1.0; typical = **0.6 to 0.8** for residential |
| **Maximum Demand** | $MD = L_{total} \times DF$ | Used to size transformer / service cable |
| **Diversity Factor** | $DvF = \dfrac{\sum L_{individual}}{L_{max\_demand}}$ | Always > 1.0 |
| **Current in a Single-Phase Circuit** | $I = \dfrac{P}{V \times \cos\phi}$ | $P$ in W, $V = 230V$ (1-ph), $\cos\phi$ = power factor |
| **Current in a 3-Phase Circuit** | $I = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} \times V_L \times \cos\phi}$ | $V_L = 415V$ line voltage |
| **Voltage Drop in Cable** | $V_{drop} = \dfrac{I \times L \times 2 \times \rho}{A}$ | $L$ = one-way length (m), $\rho$ = resistivity, $A$ = area mm², factor 2 for go-return |
| **Permissible Voltage Drop (Lighting)** | $\leq 3\%$ of declared voltage | As per IS 732 |
| **Permissible Voltage Drop (Power)** | $\leq 5\%$ of declared voltage | As per IS 732 |
| **Number of Points per Circuit** | Maximum **8 to 10 points** | Code: IS 4648 |
| **Maximum Load per Circuit** | **800 W for lighting, 1000 W for power (sockets)** | Avoid overloading |

---

### 2.3.2 Conduit & Conductor Sizing

| Quantity | Rule of Thumb / Formula |
|---|---|
| **Maximum conductors in a conduit** | 40% of internal cross-sectional area filled |
| **Bending radius of conduit** | $\geq 6 \times$ outer diameter of conduit |
| **Standard conduit sizes (PVC)** | **20 mm, 25 mm, 32 mm, 40 mm, 50 mm** |
| **Standard copper conductor sizes** | **1.5 mm² (light), 2.5 mm² (socket), 4 mm² (AC), 6 mm² (main)**, 10 mm² (sub-main) |
| **Standard aluminum conductor sizes** | 6 mm², 10 mm², 16 mm², 25 mm² |
| **Earthing conductor (copper)** | **1.5 mm² for socket, 4 mm² for main** |
| **Earth pit depth** | **Minimum 2.5 m below ground** |
| **Earth electrode (GI pipe)** | **40 mm dia, 2.5 m long** (for domestic) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Coordination with Structural Drawings**: Electrical conduits of 20–25 mm diameter are embedded in slabs. The civil engineer must coordinate so that no two conduits cross in the same slab layer (otherwise the slab thickness must increase, raising cost).
- **Coordination with Plumbing Drawings**: Electrical conduits must NEVER run parallel within 300 mm of water/gas pipes.
- **Coordination with HVAC**: AC outdoor unit points, fresh-air fan points, and duct clearances must be planned together.
- **Safety Audit & Insurance**: Building electrical drawings are required by fire departments, insurance assessors, and electrical inspectors before energizing the building.
- **Energy Audit (ECBC compliance)**: For commercial buildings, lighting load calculations directly feed into Energy Conservation Building Code compliance.

---

## 2.5 Standard Room-wise Electrical Provision (As Per IS 4648)

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum recommended points per residential room (KTU exam-friendly):**

| Room | Light Points | Fan Points | 6A Sockets | 16A Sockets | Other |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bedroom (12 m²)** | 2 (1 ceiling + 1 wall) | 1 ceiling fan | 2 | 1 (AC point) | TV point, telephone |
| **Living Room** | 2–3 | 1 ceiling fan | 3 | 1 (AC) | Bell, decorative |
| **Kitchen** | 2 (1 over counter) | 1 exhaust fan | 2 | 2 (mixer, microwave, fridge) | Aquaguard point |
| **Bathroom** | 1 (above mirror) | 1 exhaust fan | 1 (shaver, IP-rated) | 0 | Geyser point (16A) |
| **Balcony** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | — |
| **Staircase** | 1 per flight | 0 | 0 | 0 | — |
| **Passage** | 1 per 3 m length | 0 | 0 | 0 | — |
| **Utility/Store** | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | — |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Planning Procedure & Analytical Workflow

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Methodology to Plan and Prepare Electrical Drawings of a Building

This is the **core experimental procedure** for KTU's PCCEL408 Module 2. Follow these ten steps in strict order.

---

### **Step 1 — Collection of Base Drawings**

Gather the **architectural floor plans** of all floors in **.DWG/.RVT** (or hand-drawn) format. Ensure the plans show:
- Wall thicknesses, door and window positions
- Room names and dimensions
- North direction
- Scale (typically **1:50 or 1:100** for residential)

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU lab, the studio provides a standard residential plan (e.g., 2BHK, 80–120 m² built-up).

---

### **Step 2 — Load Survey and Appliance Inventory**

List every electrical appliance/point the owner requires in each room. A typical 2BHK load inventory:

| S.No | Item | Quantity | Wattage (W) | Sub-total (W) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LED Light 9W | 12 | 9 | 108 |
| 2 | Ceiling Fan 75W | 4 | 75 | 300 |
| 3 | Exhaust Fan 40W | 2 | 40 | 80 |
| 4 | 6A Socket (mobile, TV) | 8 | 100 (assumed) | 800 |
| 5 | 16A Socket (AC) | 2 | 1500 | 3000 |
| 6 | Geyser 2000W | 1 | 2000 | 2000 |
| 7 | Kitchen mixer/iron 750W | 1 | 750 | 750 |
| 8 | Fridge 300W | 1 | 300 | 300 |
| 9 | Washing Machine 500W | 1 | 500 | 500 |
| 10 | TV + Set-top 150W | 1 | 150 | 150 |
| | | | **Total Connected Load** | **7988 W ≈ 8 kW** |

---

### **Step 3 — Apply Demand Factor and Calculate Maximum Demand**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **demand factor** accounts for the fact that **not all appliances run simultaneously**. For a residential building, $DF = 0.7$ is commonly used.

$$
MD = L_{total} \times DF
$$

$$
MD = 7988 \times 0.7 = 5591.6 \text{ W} \approx 5.6 \text{ kW}
$$

Therefore, the building requires a **single-phase 230 V, ≥ 25 A** service connection (rounded up to next standard size: **30 A or 40 A**).

---

### **Step 4 — Design the Distribution Hierarchy**

A typical 2BHK uses a **3-tier distribution**:

1. **Utility Service Pole → Main Distribution Board (MDB) at entrance**
2. **MDB → Sub-Distribution Boards (one per floor)**
3. **SDB → Final branch circuits (lighting circuit, power circuit, AC circuit)**

```
Utility (EB) ──► MDB (Main Switch 40A + ELCB) ──► SDB1 (Floor 1) ──► C1: Lighting
                                                                  └──► C2: 6A sockets
                                                                  └──► C3: 16A (AC/Geyser)
                                                  └──► SDB2 (Floor 2) ──► similar
```

---

### **Step 5 — Design Lighting Circuit**

- Group all **lighting points** (max 8–10 points) into one circuit.
- Use **1.5 mm² copper (PVC insulated)** conductors.
- Protect with **6 A MCB** in the SDB.

Example: Bedrooms (2 lights + 1 fan) + Hall (2 lights + 1 fan) + Kitchen (2 lights) + Bath (1 light) = **10 points** → One **6A lighting circuit**.

---

### **Step 6 — Design Power (Socket) Circuit**

- Group **6 A sockets** in another circuit. Max **6 sockets per circuit**.
- Use **2.5 mm² copper** conductors and **16 A MCB**.
- 6 A sockets in living + bedrooms = 5 sockets → One 16A circuit.
- Provide a **separate 16 A circuit for each AC** (split AC requires a dedicated socket).

---

### **Step 7 — Mark Switchboard Locations and Heights**

- **Main switchboard** (with bell push, master switch): near the **entrance door**, height **1.2 m**.
- **Fan regulators**: alongside the **door of each room**, height **1.2 m**.
- **Light switches**: 1.2 m height, on the **latch side** of the door.
- **Sockets**: 0.45 m height (general), 1.2 m (kitchen counter), 1.8 m (AC indoor unit).

---

### **Step 8 — Draw Conduit Routing on Slab Plan**

- Conduits run **through the slab** (concealed) at a depth of **20–25 mm** from the top.
- Conduits should run **perpendicular or parallel to walls**, never diagonally (except for unavoidable crossings).
- Use **junction boxes** at every change of direction and at every switchboard.
- For **fan points**, a **fan hook box** (round) is embedded in the slab at the ceiling centre.

---

### **Step 9 — Earthing Layout**

Draw the **earthing diagram**:
- **Two earth pits** for a residential building: one for the system neutral/body earthing, one for the lightning protection (if required).
- GI pipe electrode 40 mm dia × 2.5 m length, surrounded by alternating layers of **charcoal and salt**.
- Earth resistance must be **< 1 Ω (preferred) to 5 Ω (max permissible)**.

---

### **Step 10 — Prepare Final Drawing Sheet with Legend**

The final submission sheet must contain:
1. **Title block** (project name, drawing title, scale, date, student name, roll no).
2. **North arrow**.
3. **Plan view** with all electrical symbols.
4. **Conduit routing** (dotted/dashed lines).
5. **Switchboard details** (1-Gang, 2-Gang, etc.).
6. **Single Line Diagram**.
7. **Load schedule table**.
8. **Legend / Symbol table** mapping every symbol used.
9. **Notes**: "All wiring to be in PVC insulated copper conductors in PVC conduits as per IS 732:2019."

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Load Calculation for a Small Office)

**Given**: A small office has the following loads:
- 8 tube lights × 40 W
- 4 ceiling fans × 75 W
- 6 computer workstations × 200 W
- 1 printer × 500 W
- 2 AC units × 1500 W

**Find**: (a) Total connected load, (b) Maximum demand at DF = 0.75, (c) Full-load current on the main incomer.

**Solution**:

**Step (a) — Total Connected Load:**

$$
L_{total} = (8 \times 40) + (4 \times 75) + (6 \times 200) + (1 \times 500) + (2 \times 1500)
$$

$$
L_{total} = 320 + 300 + 1200 + 500 + 3000
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{total} = 5320 \text{ W} = 5.32 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step (b) — Maximum Demand:**

$$
MD = 5320 \times 0.75 = 3990 \text{ W} = 3.99 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (c) — Full-Load Current (Single-Phase, 230 V, $\cos\phi = 0.9$):**

$$
I = \frac{P}{V \times \cos\phi} = \frac{3990}{230 \times 0.9}
$$

$$
I = \frac{3990}{207} = 19.28 \text{ A}
$$

**Conclusion**: The main isolator and busbar must be rated for at least **25 A** (next standard size above 19.28 A), and a **25 A ELCB** should be installed at the MDB.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below use **Mermaid** to render block-level functional architecture of the electrical drawing workflow. Mermaid syntax rules have been followed strictly: alphanumeric node IDs prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords as node names, and clean uppercase alphanumeric labels inside double quotes.

---

## 4.1 Workflow Block Diagram — From Architectural Plan to Final Electrical Drawing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["START PCCEL408 MODULE 2"] --> A1["RECEIVE ARCHITECTURAL FLOOR PLAN"]
    A1 --> A2["IDENTIFY ROOMS AND FIXTURES"]
    A2 --> A3["PREPARE APPLIANCE INVENTORY"]
    A3 --> A4["CALCULATE TOTAL CONNECTED LOAD"]
    A4 --> A5["APPLY DEMAND FACTOR FOR MAXIMUM DEMAND"]
    A5 --> A6["DESIGN DISTRIBUTION HIERARCHY MDB SDB"]
    A6 --> A7["DESIGN LIGHTING POWER AC CIRCUITS"]
    A7 --> A8["LOCATE SWITCHBOARDS SOCKETS FAN POINTS"]
    A8 --> A9["DRAW CONDUIT ROUTING ON SLAB PLAN"]
    A9 --> A10["DESIGN EARTHING LAYOUT"]
    A10 --> A11["PREPARE SINGLE LINE DIAGRAM"]
    A11 --> A12["COMPILE LOAD SCHEDULE AND LEGEND"]
    A12 --> A13["REVIEW AGAINST IS 732 2019 AND IS 4648"]
    A13 --> A14["FINAL DWG FILE OR HAND PLOT READY FOR SUBMISSION"]
    A14 --> A15["END MODULE 2 EXPERIMENT"]

    style A0 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f4e79
    style A15 fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f4e79
    style A5 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#c00000
    style A11 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#c00000
    style A13 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2e7d32
```

---

## 4.2 Single Line Diagram (SLD) — Functional Block View of a 2BHK Residence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    UTL["UTILITY SUPPLY 230V SINGLE PHASE"] --> MET["ENERGY METER KWH"]
    MET --> MAIN["MAIN SWITCH 40A DP"]
    MAIN --> ELCB["ELCB 30MA 40A"]
    ELCB --> MDB["MAIN DISTRIBUTION BOARD MDB"]

    MDB --> C1["CIRCUIT 1 LIGHTING 6A MCB"]
    MDB --> C2["CIRCUIT 2 6A SOCKETS 16A MCB"]
    MDB --> C3["CIRCUIT 3 AC BEDROOM 20A MCB"]
    MDB --> C4["CIRCUIT 4 AC LIVING 20A MCB"]
    MDB --> C5["CIRCUIT 5 GEYSER KITCHEN 20A MCB"]
    MDB --> ERTH["EARTH BUS TO EARTH PIT"]

    C1 --> L1["LIGHTS BEDROOM 1 2 AND FANS"]
    C1 --> L2["LIGHTS HALL KITCHEN BATH"]
    C2 --> S1["6A SOCKETS 6 NOS"]
    C3 --> AC1["AC 1.5 TON SPLIT UNIT"]
    C4 --> AC2["AC 1.5 TON SPLIT UNIT"]
    C5 --> G1["GEYSER 2 KW"]
    C5 --> K1["MIXER AND MICROWAVE 16A SOCKETS"]

    style UTL fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000
    style MAIN fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#c00000
    style ELCB fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#c00000
    style ERTH fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2e7d32
```

---

## 4.3 Hierarchical Distribution Architecture (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    GRID["EB GRID 11 KV OR 415 V THREE PHASE"] --> XFMR["STEP DOWN TRANSFORMER IF NEEDED"]
    XFMR --> SVC["SERVICE CABLE TO BUILDING METER ROOM"]
    SVC --> LV1["MAIN LT PANEL AT GROUND FLOOR"]
    LV1 --> FR1["RISING MAIN TO FIRST FLOOR DB"]
    LV1 --> FR2["RISING MAIN TO SECOND FLOOR DB"]
    FR1 --> FC1["FLAT 1 DB 6 CIRCUITS"]
    FR1 --> FC2["FLAT 2 DB 6 CIRCUITS"]
    FR2 --> FC3["FLAT 3 DB 6 CIRCUITS"]
    FR2 --> FC4["FLAT 4 DB 6 CIRCUITS"]

    FC1 --> FC1L["FLAT 1 LIGHTING LOADS"]
    FC1 --> FC1P["FLAT 1 POWER LOADS"]
    FC1 --> FC1A["FLAT 1 AC LOADS"]
    FC1 --> FC1E["FLAT 1 EARTHING BUS"]

    style GRID fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke:#1f4e79
    style LV1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#c00000
    style FC1E fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2e7d32
```

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Floor Plan — Fan/Light/Switch Layout (Top-View Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph FL["FIRST FLOOR TOP VIEW"]
        direction LR
        subgraph BD["BEDROOM 1"]
            B1F["FAN"]
            B1L["LIGHT"]
            B1S["SWITCHBOARD AT DOOR"]
            B1SK["6A SOCKET 2 NOS"]
        end
        subgraph BD2["BEDROOM 2"]
            B2F["FAN"]
            B2L["LIGHT"]
            B2S["SWITCHBOARD AT DOOR"]
            B2SK["6A SOCKET 2 NOS"]
        end
        subgraph LR1["LIVING ROOM"]
            LRF["FAN"]
            LRL1["LIGHT 1"]
            LRL2["LIGHT 2"]
            LRS["MAIN SWITCHBOARD ENTRANCE"]
            LRSK["16A AC SOCKET"]
        end
        subgraph KT["KITCHEN"]
            KTL["LIGHT"]
            KTF["EXHAUST FAN"]
            KTS["SWITCHBOARD"]
            KTSK["16A SOCKETS 2 NOS"]
        end
        subgraph BT["BATHROOM"]
            BTL["MIRROR LIGHT"]
            BTF["EXHAUST FAN"]
            BTS["IP44 SWITCH"]
            BTSK["GEYSER 16A SOCKET"]
        end
    end
```

---

## 4.5 Earthing System Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EARTH["EARTH PIT 1 BODY AND NEUTRAL"] --> ESTRP["EARTH STRIP 25X3 MM GI"]
    PIT2["EARTH PIT 2 LIGHTNING ARRESTER"] --> ESTRP2["EARTH STRIP 25X3 MM GI"]
    ESTRP --> EC["EARTH COLLECTOR BUS IN MDB"]
    ESTRP2 --> EC
    EC --> NEUT["NEUTRAL LINK"]
    EC --> BODY1["ALL SOCKET EARTH TERMINALS"]
    EC --> BODY2["ALL METAL SWITCH PLATES"]
    EC --> BODY3["AC OUTDOOR UNIT METAL BODY"]
    EC --> BODY4["WATER TANK METAL BODY"]
    EC --> BODY5["LIFT MOTOR BODY IF ANY"]

    style EARTH fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2e7d32
    style PIT2 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff,stroke:#2e7d32
    style EC fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff,stroke:#c00000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`
**List any six standardized electrical symbols used in building electrical drawings as per IS 2302.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 per symbol):**
1. **SPST Switch**: Single line cutting through a small circle.
2. **Ceiling Rose**: A solid filled circle with a vertical stem.
3. **6A Socket Outlet**: Hollow circle with two short parallel lines inside.
4. **Ceiling Fan**: A circle with three curved blades and a regulator symbol (square).
5. **Earth Point**: Three decreasing horizontal lines stacked vertically.
6. **MCB**: A square with a small angled line inside.
7. **Fluorescent Tube Light**: A rectangle with internal diagonal hatching.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the standard heights above floor level for the following electrical accessories in a residential building.**
(i) Ceiling fan, (ii) Light switch, (iii) 6A socket outlet.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 per item):**
- (i) **Ceiling fan**: The fan hook must be embedded in the slab such that the fan blade sweep is at a **minimum height of 2.75 m** above the finished floor level. Standard fan sweep sizes are **900 mm, 1200 mm, and 1400 mm**.
- (ii) **Light switch**: All wall-mounted switches are installed at a standard height of **1.2 m to 1.5 m** above the finished floor level, on the **latch side** of the door, and at least **100 mm** away from the door frame.
- (iii) **6A socket outlet**: For general-purpose use, mounted at **0.45 m** above the floor. In **kitchens and work-counters**, raised to **1.2 m** to be above counter level. **Bathroom sockets** must be IP44-rated and located away from water sources.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice) (1 × 14 = 14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule**: Each module question carries 14 marks with internal choice. Students answer **one of two alternatives**. We provide both choices below with full sub-parts and step-by-step model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Single Line Diagram (SLD)** of a residential building having the following loads: 4 ceiling fans, 8 tube lights, 6 general-purpose sockets, 2 AC points, and 1 geyser point. Show clearly the main switch, ELCB, MCBs, and the earth bus. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential building has the following connected loads: 6 tube lights (40 W each), 4 ceiling fans (60 W each), 2 TVs (100 W each), 1 refrigerator (300 W), 1 geyser (2 kW), and 1 AC (1.5 kW). Calculate:
   (i) Total connected load,
   (ii) Maximum demand (take demand factor = 0.75),
   (iii) Full-load current on the main switch (single-phase, 230 V, pf = 0.9). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Single Line Diagram (7 marks):**

The SLD shows the **power flow from utility → meter → main switch → ELCB → MDB → branch circuits → loads** using single lines.

**Components and ratings:**
- **Utility supply**: 230 V, single phase, 50 Hz.
- **Energy meter**: 5–30 A, kWh.
- **Main switch (DP)**: 40 A, double-pole.
- **ELCB/RCCB**: 40 A, 30 mA sensitivity.
- **MDB** with the following MCB-fed circuits:
  - **C1 (Lighting)**: 6 A MCB → 8 tube lights in parallel.
  - **C2 (Fans)**: 6 A MCB → 4 fans with regulators.
  - **C3 (Sockets)**: 16 A MCB → 6 × 6 A sockets.
  - **C4 (AC-1)**: 20 A MCB → 1 × 16 A socket for AC.
  - **C5 (Geyser)**: 20 A MCB → 1 × 16 A socket for geyser.
- **Earth bus bar**: Connected from MDB to two earth pits; from earth bus, the green-yellow protective conductor (PE) goes to every socket's earth terminal and metal bodies of appliances.

A neat hand-drawn SLD should be shown, with each component labelled with its rating, the wire gauge (1.5 mm² for lighting, 2.5 mm² for sockets, 4 mm² for AC/geyser) clearly noted along the line, and the colour coding (R-phase = Red, N = Black, E = Green) shown in a legend box.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step (i) — Total Connected Load:**

$$
L_{total} = (6 \times 40) + (4 \times 60) + (2 \times 100) + (1 \times 300) + (1 \times 2000) + (1 \times 1500)
$$

$$
L_{total} = 240 + 240 + 200 + 300 + 2000 + 1500 = 4480 \text{ W}
$$

**Step (ii) — Maximum Demand:**

$$
MD = 4480 \times 0.75 = 3360 \text{ W} = 3.36 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step (iii) — Full-Load Current:**

$$
I = \frac{P}{V \times \cos\phi} = \frac{3360}{230 \times 0.9} = \frac{3360}{207}
$$

$$
I = 16.23 \text{ A}
$$

**Conclusion**: The main switch should be selected at the **next higher standard rating = 20 A or 25 A** with a **30 mA ELCB** for safety.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** List and draw the **standard BIS (IS 2302) symbols** for any ten electrical accessories used in residential wiring. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Plan the **electrical layout of a 2BHK residential flat (approx. 90 m²)** with the following room-wise provisions: 1 living-cum-dining, 2 bedrooms, 1 kitchen, 2 bathrooms, 1 balcony. State the number of light points, fan points, 6A sockets, 16A sockets, and special points in each room. Justify your choices as per **IS 4648**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) BIS Symbols (7 marks — 0.7 each for 10 symbols):**

| S.No | Symbol Name | Description | Mark |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | SPST Switch (6A) | Single line cuts through a small circle | 0.7 |
| 2 | 2-Way Switch | Line with two branching terminals | 0.7 |
| 3 | Bell Push | Small circle with letter "B" | 0.7 |
| 4 | Ceiling Rose | Solid black circle with vertical stem | 0.7 |
| 5 | 6A Socket (3-pin) | Hollow circle with two parallel lines | 0.7 |
| 6 | 16A Socket (3-pin) | Hollow circle with two thick parallel lines | 0.7 |
| 7 | Ceiling Fan + Regulator | Circle with three curved blades + small square | 0.7 |
| 8 | Exhaust Fan | Circle with two airflow arrows | 0.7 |
| 9 | Fluorescent Tube | Rectangle with diagonal hatching | 0.7 |
| 10 | Earth Point | Three decreasing horizontal lines | 0.7 |
| 11 | MCB | Square with angled line inside | 0.7 |

**Note**: For full marks, students must draw (not just describe) each symbol neatly within a small grid.

**(b) Room-wise Electrical Layout (7 marks — distribute marks as: 3 for table + 2 for justification + 2 for neat sketch explanation):**

| Room | Lights | Fans | 6A Sockets | 16A Sockets | Special |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Living/Dining** | 3 (2 ceiling + 1 wall bracket) | 1 ceiling fan | 3 | 1 (AC point) | TV point, telephone, bell |
| **Bedroom 1 (Master)** | 2 (1 ceiling + 1 wall) | 1 ceiling fan | 2 | 1 (AC) | TV point |
| **Bedroom 2 (Kids)** | 2 (1 ceiling + 1 wall) | 1 ceiling fan | 2 | 0 | Study lamp socket |
| **Kitchen** | 2 (1 over counter + 1 ceiling) | 1 exhaust fan | 2 | 2 (mixer, microwave) | Aquaguard, fridge point |
| **Bathroom 1 (attached)** | 1 (above mirror) | 1 exhaust fan | 1 (IP44, shaver) | 1 (geyser) | — |
| **Bathroom 2 (common)** | 1 (above mirror) | 1 exhaust fan | 1 (IP44) | 1 (geyser) | — |
| **Balcony** | 1 (wall light) | 0 | 1 | 0 | — |
| **Passage** | 1 (wall bracket) | 0 | 0 | 0 | — |
| **Entrance** | 1 (porch light) | 0 | 0 | 0 | Doorbell push at door |
| **TOTAL** | **14** | **5 fans + 2 exhausts** | **13** | **6** | As above |

**Justification (as per IS 4648)**:
- **Lighting points**: Each room must have at least one ceiling-mounted light. Larger rooms (living > 12 m²) need 2–3 lights for uniform illumination (lux level 100–150 for living areas).
- **Fan points**: Bedrooms and living MUST have ceiling fans as per **climatic necessity of Kerala**; exhaust fans are mandatory in kitchens and bathrooms for ventilation.
- **6A sockets**: Minimum **2 per room** per IS 4648; living requires more for TV, set-top, mobile chargers, lamps.
- **16A sockets**: Required for **high-power appliances** (AC, geyser, microwave, mixer, washing machine) — must be on **dedicated circuits** with 20 A MCB.
- **Earth continuity**: Every socket MUST have a separate earth wire (green) running back to the MDB earth bus.
- **Switchboard height**: 1.2 m, on the latch side of the door, at least 100 mm from the door frame.

A **neat sketch** of the floor plan with all symbols marked must accompany the answer for full credit.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Demand Factor**: Students often quote the **connected load** as the basis for main switch rating. This is wrong. Always use **Maximum Demand** (Connected Load × Demand Factor) to size the main switch, ELCB, and service cable. **[-2 marks penalty]**
> 2. **Confusing Power Factor (cos φ) with Demand Factor**: $DF$ is a load diversity multiplier; $\cos\phi$ is a phase-shift between V and I. They are different quantities. Using one in place of the other will yield wrong current. **[-2 marks penalty]**
> 3. **Drawing Symbols Without Legend**: Examiners in PCCEL408 require a **legend box** in every submitted drawing. Submitting a plan with symbols but no legend is incomplete. **[-1.5 marks penalty]**
> 4. **Mixing Conduit and Conductor Sizes**: Conduit size (e.g., 20 mm) is **NOT** the same as conductor size (e.g., 2.5 mm²). They are different specifications. **[-1 mark penalty]**
> 5. **Ignoring Earth Wire in Sockets**: In any SLD, the **green earth wire** must be shown going to every socket's earth terminal. Skipping this is a violation of IS 732 and IS 3043. **[-2 marks penalty]**
> 6. **No Title Block**: Every drawing sheet must have a **title block** (project name, drawing title, scale, date, drawn by, checked by). A drawing without it loses marks. **[-1 mark penalty]**

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🎯 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam:**

- **IS 732:2019** is the master code for **electrical wiring**; **IS 2302** is for **symbols**; **IS 4648** is for **residential layout**.
- **Standard heights**: Fan = **2.75 m** (sweep bottom), Switch = **1.2 m**, Socket = **0.45 m** (general) or **1.2 m** (kitchen).
- **Colour code**: Phase = **Red/Yellow/Blue** (3-phase), Neutral = **Black**, Earth = **Green** (or green-yellow).
- **Demand factor** for residential = **0.6 to 0.8**; use it BEFORE sizing the main switch.
- **Diversity factor** = sum of individual max demands / max demand of whole; always **> 1**.
- **Maximum points per circuit** = **8–10 for lighting**, **6 for sockets**.
- **Lighting MCB** = **6 A**; **Socket MCB** = **16 A**; **AC/Geyser MCB** = **20 A**; **Main** = **30–40 A**.
- **Conductor sizes**: 1.5 mm² (light), 2.5 mm² (socket), 4 mm² (AC), 6 mm² (main).
- **Voltage drop limits**: ≤ **3%** for lighting, ≤ **5%** for power.
- **Earth pit** = **2.5 m deep, 40 mm GI pipe**, charcoal-salt filling; resistance **< 5 Ω**.
- **ELCB/RCCB sensitivity** = **30 mA** for human protection, **100–300 mA** for fire protection.
- **Final drawing MUST include**: title block, north arrow, scale, legend, switchboard details, conduit routing, SLD, load schedule.
- **Formulae to memorize**:
  - $L_{total} = \sum N_i W_i$
  - $MD = L_{total} \times DF$
  - $I_{1\phi} = \dfrac{P}{V \cos\phi}$
  - $I_{3\phi} = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3} V_L \cos\phi}$
  - $V_{drop} = \dfrac{I \cdot L \cdot 2 \rho}{A}$
- **Coordination rules**:
  - Conduits must NOT cross plumbing pipes within **300 mm**.
  - Conduits in slabs must run **perpendicular/parallel** to walls, not diagonally.
  - AC outdoor points, geyser points, and washing-machine points are **dedicated 16A circuits**.
- **Kerala-specific note**: Because of the **tropical humid climate**, exhaust fans in every kitchen and bathroom, **IP44 sockets in wet areas**, and **anti-condensation conduit sealing** are **mandatory** considerations.

---

**END OF MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 NOTES — PCCEL408 CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB**
