# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a contour map and contour interval. State the relationship between contour interval, horizontal equivalent, and slope of the ground.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Contour map (1 m):** A topographic map showing the relief of an area by means of contour lines, where each contour represents a line joining points of equal elevation above a chosen datum.
- **Contour interval (1 m):** The vertical distance between two successive contour lines on the map. Symbol: C.I. Units: metres. Common values: 0.5 m, 1.0 m, 2.0 m, 5.0 m.
- **Relationship (1 m):** The slope of the ground is given by `S = C.I. / H.E.` where H.E. = horizontal equivalent (the horizontal distance between two successive contours). A small H.E. indicates a steep slope; a large H.E. indicates a gentle slope.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** What are the uses of a contour map in civil engineering? List any four applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Route alignment** for roads, railways, canals, and pipelines — to study gradients and earthwork volumes.
2. **Site selection for dams and reservoirs** — to find suitable catchments and dam-axis elevations.
3. **Drainage and flood studies** — to determine watershed boundaries, flow direction, and storage capacity.
4. **Earthwork volume estimation** — for cut-fill calculations in cut/fill balance of construction projects.
*(A 5th acceptable point: location of buildings and layout planning on uneven terrain.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> ### Question A (14 marks) — `OPTION A` [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]
>
> **(a)** A total station set up at station A `(E = 500.000 m, N = 500.000 m)` sights a benchmark of R.L. = **200.000 m** with a vertical angle of `+3°30′` and slant distance of `20.000 m`. The prism height is `2.000 m`. **Compute the R.L. of the instrument axis.** **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** From the same station, two fore-sight points P and Q are observed with the following data. **Compute the R.L. of P and Q and the plan coordinates of Q.** **(7 marks)**
>
> | Point | Hz | Vertical Angle | S (m) |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | P | 60°00′00″ | +2°00′00″ | 30.000 |
> | Q | 120°00′00″ | −1°30′00″ | 25.000 |

### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — R.L. of Instrument Axis**

> Given: `S_BM = 20.000 m`, `α_BM = +3°30′ = 3.5°`, `RL_BM = 200.000 m`, `p = 2.000 m` (not needed for `h_i`).

$$ h_i \;=\; S_{BM} \cdot \sin(\alpha_{BM}) \;=\; 20.000 \cdot \sin(3.5^{\circ}) $$

$$ h_i \;=\; 20.000 \cdot 0.06105 \;=\; 1.2210 \;\text{m} $$

$$ RL_{Inst} \;=\; RL_{BM} \;+\; h_i \;=\; 200.000 \;+\; 1.2210 \;=\; \mathbf{201.2210\;\text{m}} $$

**Marks distribution:** Formula (2), substitution (1.5), correct sine (1.5), final result (2) = **7 marks**.

**Part (b) — R.L. of P and Q and Coordinates of Q**

**Point P: `Hz = 60°`, `α = +2°`, `S = 30.000 m`**

$$ V_P \;=\; 30.000 \cdot \sin(2^{\circ}) \;=\; 30.000 \cdot 0.03490 \;=\; 1.0470 \;\text{m} $$

$$ RL_P \;=\; RL_{Inst} \;+\; V_P \;-\; p \;=\; 201.2210 \;+\; 1.0470 \;-\; 2.000 \;=\; \mathbf{200.2680\;\text{m}} $$

**Point Q: `Hz = 120°`, `α = −1.5°`, `S = 25.000 m`**

$$ V_Q \;=\; 25.000 \cdot \sin(-1.5^{\circ}) \;=\; 25.000 \cdot (-0.02618) \;=\; -0.6545 \;\text{m} $$

$$ D_Q \;=\; 25.000 \cdot \cos(-1.5^{\circ}) \;=\; 25.000 \cdot 0.99966 \;=\; 24.9915 \;\text{m} $$

$$ E_Q \;=\; 500.000 \;+\; 24.9915 \cdot \sin(120^{\circ}) \;=\; 500.000 \;+\; 24.9915 \cdot 0.86603 \;=\; 500.000 \;+\; 21.6433 \;=\; \mathbf{521.6433\;\text{m}} $$

$$ N_Q \;=\; 500.000 \;+\; 24.9915 \cdot \cos(120^{\circ}) \;=\; 500.000 \;+\; 24.9915 \cdot (-0.50000) \;=\; 500.000 \;-\; 12.4958 \;=\; \mathbf{487.5042\;\text{m}} $$

$$ RL_Q \;=\; 201.2210 \;+\; (-0.6545) \;-\; 2.000 \;=\; \mathbf{198.5665\;\text{m}} $$

**Marks distribution (7 marks):** P's R.L. (1.5), Q's R.L. (1.5), Q's `D` and `E` (2), Q's `N` (2) = **7 marks**.

---

> ### Question B (14 marks) — `OPTION B` [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]
>
> **(a)** Explain the **trigonometric levelling** method used to compute the R.L. of a point from total station data. State the formula with a clear sketch of the geometry. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Two adjacent points A and B on a slope have R.L.s of **98.450 m and 99.200 m** respectively, and a plan distance of **20.000 m**. The chosen contour interval is **0.5 m**. **Compute the distance from A at which each contour line (98.5, 99.0, 99.0, 99.5) crosses the line A–B. Also state the gradient of the slope.** **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — Trigonometric Levelling (7 marks)**

Trigonometric levelling is the indirect method of determining the reduced level of a point by measuring the **vertical angle** and **slant distance** from an instrument of known instrument-axis R.L.

**Geometry:**
- The instrument is at station `I` with axis R.L. = `RL_Inst`.
- A benchmark of known R.L. is sighted first to compute `h_i = S_BM · sin(α_BM)`.
- The target point P has a prism at height `p` above the ground.
- The slant line I–P has length `S_P`, vertical angle `α_P`.
- Vertical component: `V_P = S_P · sin(α_P)` (signed).

**Formula:**

$$ RL_{Ground,\,P} \;=\; RL_{BM} \;+\; S_{BM}\sin(\alpha_{BM}) \;+\; S_{P}\sin(\alpha_{P}) \;-\; p $$

**Sign convention:** `+α` (elevation) → `V` is positive; `−α` (depression) → `V` is negative.

**Marks distribution:** Geometry & diagram (2), Explanation (2), Formula (2), Sign convention (1) = **7 marks**.

**Part (b) — Interpolation along A–B (7 marks)**

Given: `RL_A = 98.450 m`, `RL_B = 99.200 m`, `d_AB = 20.000 m`, C.I. = 0.5 m.

The contours **98.5 m** and **99.0 m** lie between A and B (since `98.450 < 98.5 < 99.0 < 99.200`).

**Gradient of the slope:**

$$ \Delta h \;=\; 99.200 \;-\; 98.450 \;=\; 0.750 \;\text{m} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{gradient} \;=\; \frac{0.750}{20.000} \;=\; 1 \text{ in } 26.67 $$

**Distance from A to 98.5 m contour:**

$$ d_{A\to 98.5} \;=\; \frac{98.5 \;-\; 98.450}{99.200 \;-\; 98.450} \cdot 20.000 \;=\; \frac{0.050}{0.750} \cdot 20.000 \;=\; \mathbf{1.333\;\text{m}} $$

**Distance from A to 99.0 m contour:**

$$ d_{A\to 99.0} \;=\; \frac{99.0 \;-\; 98.450}{99.200 \;-\; 98.450} \cdot 20.000 \;=\; \frac{0.550}{0.750} \cdot 20.000 \;=\; \mathbf{14.667\;\text{m}} $$

**Horizontal equivalents between consecutive contours:**
- 98.5 → 99.0: `H.E. = 14.667 − 1.333 = 13.334 m` per 0.5 m rise → gradient 1 in 26.67 ✓ (consistent).

**Marks distribution (7 marks):** Gradient (1), Formula used (1), 98.5 m point (1.5), 99.0 m point (1.5), H.E. verification (1), final answer statement (1) = **7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in this experiment — Read carefully before submission:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert DMS to decimal degrees** (or vice-versa) before pressing `sin/cos` on a calculator — costs 1–2 marks per wrong reading.
> 2. **Wrong sign of vertical angle** when the prism is below the instrument axis (depression case) — costs up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Subtracting `p` (prism height) when it is *already* the staff-reading convention** — read whether your instrument uses `+p` or `−p`. Total stations using `RL_Ground = RL_Inst + V + p` (for "staff" reading) vs `RL_Ground = RL_Inst + V − p` (for prism pole) — these are *opposite* signs.
> 4. **Plotting at wrong scale** — e.g., drawing at 1:500 when the question asked for 1:1000.
> 5. **Missing north arrow, scale bar, contour interval note, and title block** — a KTU valuation key deducts 0.5 mark each for missing these four items.
> 6. **Not labelling index contours** (every 5th contour with a thicker line and elevation written) — deducts 1 mark.
> 7. **Drawing contours as straight zig-zags** instead of smooth, free-flowing curves — **interpolation points are joined with a French curve or a flexicurve**, never a ruler.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Fire Revision Points for KTU 2024 ESE / Lab Viva:**

- **Contour line** = line joining points of **equal elevation** above a datum (usually M.S.L.).
- **Contour interval (C.I.)** = vertical spacing between consecutive contours; choose as **1/2000 to 1/1000** of the map scale's denominator for general surveys.
- **Slope** = `C.I. / H.E.` → small H.E. = steep, large H.E. = gentle.
- **Total station raw data** per point: `S`, `Hz`, `V` (or zenith angle).
- **Height of instrument** (above benchmark) is **never measured** directly — it is **computed**: `h_i = S_BM · sin(α_BM)`.
- **`RL_Inst = RL_BM + h_i`** is the master anchor for all subsequent R.L. calculations.
- **Vertical component:** `V = S · sin(α)` — **signed**: `+` for elevation, `−` for depression.
- **Plan distance:** `D = S · cos(α)` — always positive.
- **Coordinates:** `E = E_0 + D · sin(Hz)`, `N = N_0 + D · cos(Hz)`.
- **Ground R.L.:** `RL_Ground = RL_Inst + V − p` (for prism pole).
- **Interpolation formula (linear, along straight segment A–B):**

$$ d_{A\to H_C} \;=\; \frac{H_C \;-\; RL_A}{RL_B \;-\; RL_A} \cdot d_{AB} $$

- **Contour drawing** must be **smooth** (use French curve), not zig-zag; **never cross**; **never branch**; **never end abruptly** (close loops at map edge if necessary).
- **Index contours** = every 5th line, drawn **thicker**, with R.L. labelled in a gap.
- **Prism constant** (if non-zero) is **subtracted** from R.L. for `p > 0` convention; always check your instrument manual.
- **Software tools expected in KTU 2024 lab:** AutoCAD Civil 3D / Surfer / QGIS / Excel — output `.dxf` or `.pdf` is the typical deliverable.
- **Map essentials (mandatory on the drawing sheet):** Title block, scale bar, north arrow, legend, contour interval, datum, surveyor name, date, and the institution logo.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Earthwork estimation from the provided total station survey data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL408 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Earthwork estimation from the provided total station survey data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Earthwork estimation** is the quantitative process of computing the volumes of soil (or rock) to be excavated (*cut*) and the volume of material required to be added (*fill*) in order to transform an existing natural ground surface into a proposed engineered formation surface (subgrade, platform, canal bed, building plinth, road profile, etc.). In the context of total station–based data, earthwork estimation uses the **three-dimensional (3D) coordinates** $(X, Y, Z)$ of ground points recorded by the instrument to reconstruct the Digital Terrain Model (DTM) and then evaluate cut/fill quantities.

$$V_{\text{earthwork}} = \sum \left[ A_i \times (h_{\text{design}} - h_{\text{ground}}) \right]$$

where $h_{\text{design}}$ is the proposed formation level (PFL), $h_{\text{ground}}$ is the existing natural ground level (NGL), and $A_i$ is the elemental area over which the depth applies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Note (PCCEL408 – Module 2):** The experiment uses total station data (instrument-generated $X, Y, Z$ files) as input. The student is expected to compute the volume of earthwork involved in forming a level platform between a given existing ground profile and a proposed formation level, using either the **grid method**, **cross-section method**, or **contour method**.

### 1.2 Role of the Total Station in Earthwork Estimation

A **total station** is an electro-optical instrument that integrates an electronic distance meter (EDM), an electronic theodolite, and an onboard microprocessor. When set up over a known station, it records:
- **Horizontal Angle (HA)** → gives direction (bearing)
- **Vertical Angle (VA)** → gives elevation difference using the vertical component
- **Slope Distance (SD)** → gives radial distance

These are processed internally to yield geodetic coordinates:

$$X = X_{\text{station}} + SD \cdot \sin(VA) \cdot \cos(HA)$$
$$Y = Y_{\text{station}} + SD \cdot \sin(VA) \cdot \sin(HA)$$
$$Z = Z_{\text{station}} + SD \cdot \cos(VA) + h_{\text{i}} - h_{\text{t}}$$

where $h_{\text{i}}$ is instrument height and $h_{\text{t}}$ is target/prism height. The resulting coordinate file is the **raw material** for earthwork estimation.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Cake Levelling" Analogy**
>
> Imagine an uneven, lumpy cake on a table (the **natural ground**). You want to slice the top so that the cake becomes perfectly flat at a certain height (the **proposed formation level**). The slices of cake that come off the top are the **CUT** material. Now suppose the cake has a deep valley in the middle — to make it flat, you have to add cake-paste there. That added material is the **FILL**.
>
> The total station gives you the precise lumpy shape of the cake (in 3D), and your job is to calculate how much cake is sliced and how much paste is added. The net result tells the contractor how many truckloads of soil to bring in or take away.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering analogy:** Before constructing a building, road, or canal, the site must be graded. Grading requires either cutting high spots down or filling low spots up. The engineer must know these volumes *before* construction to:
> - **Estimate cost** (rate per cubic metre of excavation/embankment).
> - **Plan equipment** (number of tippers, excavators, compactors).
> - **Schedule labour** and project cash flow.
> - **Decide whether to balance cut and fill** on site (avoiding import/export).



### 1.4 Key Terms to Know Cold

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **NGL** (Natural Ground Level) | Original ground elevation from total station. |
| **PFL** (Proposed Formation Level) | Design elevation of the finished subgrade. |
| **Cut** | NGL > PFL → soil must be removed. |
| **Fill** | NGL < PFL → soil must be added. |
| **DTM / TIN** | Digital Terrain Model / Triangulated Irregular Network. |
| **Swell factor** | Ratio of loosened volume to in-situ volume (typically 1.2–1.3). |
| **Shrinkage factor** | Ratio of compacted volume to in-situ volume (typically 0.85–0.95). |
| **Berm** | Strip of land left undisturbed at the edge of a cut/fill. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Main Methods of Earthwork Estimation

The method chosen depends on (a) the data density, (b) project type, and (c) accuracy required. All three reduce the irregular ground into a series of geometric primitives whose volumes can be summed.

#### A. Grid Method (Most common in PCCEL408 labs)
- The site is overlaid with a **square or rectangular grid** (typically 5 m, 10 m, or 20 m spacing).
- NGL at every grid intersection is interpolated (or directly measured) from total station data.
- The cut/fill depth at each node is computed as $d_i = \text{NGL}_i - \text{PFL}$.
- The volume is the sum of prism volumes over each grid square.

#### B. Cross-Section Method
- Used for **linear projects** (roads, canals, railways, pipelines).
- Cross-sections are taken at regular chainages perpendicular to the centreline.
- Each cross-section is plotted with existing and proposed profile; the **end area** $A$ is computed.
- Volume between two successive cross-sections is calculated by:
  - **Average End Area method:** $V = \frac{(A_1 + A_2)}{2} \times D$
  - **Prismoidal formula:** $V = \frac{D}{3} \times (A_1 + 4A_m + A_2)$ (more accurate, requires a mid-section)

#### C. Contour Method
- The total station data is used to plot **contour lines** (lines of equal elevation).
- The area enclosed between successive contours and the formation contour is measured with a planimeter or by coordinates.
- Volume between two contours = mean of the two areas $\times$ contour interval.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic (Grid Method)

1. **Acquire data:** Total station downloads $(X, Y, Z)$ for every surveyed point.
2. **Plot grid:** Overlay a regular grid matching the survey extent.
3. **Interpolate NGLs:** For each grid node, interpolate the elevation from surrounding total station points (using bilinear interpolation or by selecting the nearest point).
4. **Compute depth:** $d_i = \text{NGL}_i - \text{PFL}$. Positive $d$ = cut, negative $d$ = fill.
5. **Compute cell volume:** For each grid square with side $a$:
   $$V_{\text{cell}} = \frac{a^2}{4} \times (d_1 + d_2 + d_3 + d_4)$$
   This is the **average depth method** (also called the four-corner method or trapezoidal rule for a prism).
6. **Sum:** $V_{\text{total}} = \sum V_{\text{cell}}$ (separately for cut and fill).
7. **Apply corrections:** If material is to be transported, multiply in-situ volume by **swell factor** for cut, or divide by **shrinkage factor** for fill.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Formulas

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the average of four corners works:**
> A square grid cell whose four corners have different cut/fill depths forms an irregular **prism** (a truncated pyramid in the ideal case). For a plane top, the exact volume is the average of the four corner depths times the base area. This is mathematically equivalent to integrating the linear depth function over the square — it is *exact* if the ground is planar across the cell, and a good approximation otherwise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why we need a mid-section for prismoidal formula:**
> The average end area method over-estimates volume when the cross-sectional area changes rapidly (concave or convex ground). Adding a mid-section area weighted by 4 (Simpson's 1/3 rule) cancels the second-order error, giving an exact result for a ground that varies as a parabola between sections.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Name | Where Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $d_i = \text{NGL}_i - \text{PFL}$ | Depth of cut (+) / fill (–) | Grid / cross-section method |
| 2 | $V_{\text{cell}} = \dfrac{a^2}{4}(d_1 + d_2 + d_3 + d_4)$ | Average depth (4-corner) | Grid method |
| 3 | $V = \dfrac{a^2}{3}(d_1 + 3d_2 + d_3)$ | Prismoidal (3-point line) | Linear interpolation |
| 4 | $V_{\text{Avg End Area}} = \dfrac{(A_1 + A_2)}{2} \times D$ | Average end area | Cross-section method |
| 5 | $V_{\text{Prismoidal}} = \dfrac{D}{3}(A_1 + 4A_m + A_2)$ | Prismoidal (Simpson) | Cross-section method |
| 6 | $V_{\text{contour}} = \dfrac{(A_1 + A_2)}{2} \times h$ | Contour method | Between two contours, $h$ = interval |
| 7 | $V_{\text{loose}} = V_{\text{in-situ}} \times S$ | Swell factor $S$ | Cut material transport |
| 8 | $V_{\text{compacted}} = V_{\text{in-situ}} \times \rho$ | Shrinkage factor $\rho$ | Fill compaction |
| 9 | $X = X_0 + SD\sin VA \cos HA$ | TS → coordinate | Data reduction |
| 10 | $Z = Z_0 + SD\cos VA + h_i - h_t$ | TS → elevation | Data reduction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units to remember:** Distances in **metres (m)**, areas in **square metres (m²)**, volumes in **cubic metres (m³)**. Swell and shrinkage are dimensionless ratios. Standard swell factor for common soil ≈ **1.25**, shrinkage factor ≈ **0.90**.



### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Earthwork estimation is the **single largest cost item** in most civil engineering projects (often 20–40% of total project cost in highways and earth dams). Accurate estimation from total station data directly affects:
- **Tendering and contract award** (BOQ – Bill of Quantities).
- **Selection of heavy machinery** (excavator class, dumper capacity).
- **Project scheduling** (earthwork is on the critical path).
- **Environmental compliance** (estimating dust, noise, and diesel emissions).
- **Land acquisition and borrow pit planning.**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Average-Depth (4-Corner) Volume Formula

Consider a single square grid cell of side $a$ in the $X$–$Y$ plane. The four corners are at $(0,0)$, $(a,0)$, $(a,a)$, $(0,a)$ and have measured depths (cut/fill) $d_1, d_2, d_3, d_4$ respectively.

**Assumption:** The depth varies linearly across the cell (i.e., the existing and proposed surfaces are both planes, and the difference of two planes is also a plane).

The depth function can be written as:
$$d(x,y) = \alpha + \beta x + \gamma y$$

Matching at the four corners gives the constants in terms of corner depths. The volume of the prism between the existing and proposed surfaces over the cell is:

$$V = \int_0^a \int_0^a d(x,y)\, dx\, dy$$

Substituting and integrating (with the constraint that the average value of a bilinear function over its four corners is simply the arithmetic mean of the corners):

$$V = a^2 \times \left(\frac{d_1 + d_2 + d_3 + d_4}{4}\right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{\text{cell}} = \frac{a^2}{4}(d_1 + d_2 + d_3 + d_4)}$$

This is exact for a planar ground and a very good approximation when the grid is sufficiently fine (typically $a \le 10$ m for a building plot, $a \le 20$ m for road formation).

### 3.2 Worked Example – Total Station Data to Earthwork Volume

**Problem Statement (typical KTU lab problem):**
A rectangular plot 30 m × 20 m is to be levelled to a Proposed Formation Level (PFL) of **101.50 m**. A total station survey was carried out and the following grid-point coordinates were recorded. Compute the volume of cut and fill using the **grid (average depth) method**.

**Total Station Data (X, Y, Z in metres):**

| Point | Easting (X) | Northing (Y) | Elevation (Z = NGL) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A1 | 0  | 0  | 102.50 |
| A2 | 10 | 0  | 102.20 |
| A3 | 20 | 0  | 101.80 |
| A4 | 30 | 0  | 101.50 |
| B1 | 0  | 10 | 102.30 |
| B2 | 10 | 10 | 101.90 |
| B3 | 20 | 10 | 101.60 |
| B4 | 30 | 10 | 101.40 |
| C1 | 0  | 20 | 102.00 |
| C2 | 10 | 20 | 101.50 |
| C3 | 20 | 20 | 101.30 |
| C4 | 30 | 20 | 101.00 |

**Step 1: Compute cut/fill depth at each grid node.**
Use $d_i = \text{NGL}_i - \text{PFL}$, with $\text{PFL} = 101.50$ m. Sign convention: **positive = cut, negative = fill**.

| Point | NGL (m) | PFL (m) | Depth $d$ (m) | Nature |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A1 | 102.50 | 101.50 | +1.00 | Cut |
| A2 | 102.20 | 101.50 | +0.70 | Cut |
| A3 | 101.80 | 101.50 | +0.30 | Cut |
| A4 | 101.50 | 101.50 | 0.00 | Nil |
| B1 | 102.30 | 101.50 | +0.80 | Cut |
| B2 | 101.90 | 101.50 | +0.40 | Cut |
| B3 | 101.60 | 101.50 | +0.10 | Cut |
| B4 | 101.40 | 101.50 | –0.10 | Fill |
| C1 | 102.00 | 101.50 | +0.50 | Cut |
| C2 | 101.50 | 101.50 | 0.00 | Nil |
| C3 | 101.30 | 101.50 | –0.20 | Fill |
| C4 | 101.00 | 101.50 | –0.50 | Fill |

**Step 2: Identify the six 10 m × 10 m grid squares and apply the 4-corner formula.**
Each cell area $a^2 = 10 \times 10 = 100\ \text{m}^2$.

**Cell S1 (corners A1, A2, B2, B1):**

$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(+1.00) + (+0.70) + (+0.40) + (+0.80)}{4} = \frac{+2.90}{4} = +0.725\ \text{m}$$

$$V_{S1} = 100 \times 0.725 = +72.5\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{Cut})$$

**Cell S2 (corners A2, A3, B3, B2):**

$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(+0.70) + (+0.30) + (+0.10) + (+0.40)}{4} = \frac{+1.50}{4} = +0.375\ \text{m}$$

$$V_{S2} = 100 \times 0.375 = +37.5\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{Cut})$$

**Cell S3 (corners A3, A4, B4, B3):**

$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(+0.30) + (0.00) + (-0.10) + (+0.10)}{4} = \frac{+0.30}{4} = +0.075\ \text{m}$$

$$V_{S3} = 100 \times 0.075 = +7.5\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{Cut})$$

**Cell S4 (corners B1, B2, C2, C1):**

$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(+0.80) + (+0.40) + (0.00) + (+0.50)}{4} = \frac{+1.70}{4} = +0.425\ \text{m}$$

$$V_{S4} = 100 \times 0.425 = +42.5\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{Cut})$$

**Cell S5 (corners B2, B3, C3, C2):**

$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(+0.40) + (+0.10) + (-0.20) + (0.00)}{4} = \frac{+0.30}{4} = +0.075\ \text{m}$$

$$V_{S5} = 100 \times 0.075 = +7.5\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{Cut})$$

**Cell S6 (corners B3, B4, C4, C3):**

$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{(+0.10) + (-0.10) + (-0.50) + (-0.20)}{4} = \frac{-0.70}{4} = -0.175\ \text{m}$$

$$V_{S6} = 100 \times (-0.175) = -17.5\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{Fill})$$

**Step 3: Sum cut and fill volumes separately.**

$$V_{\text{cut}} = V_{S1} + V_{S2} + V_{S3} + V_{S4} + V_{S5}$$

$$V_{\text{cut}} = 72.5 + 37.5 + 7.5 + 42.5 + 7.5 = +167.5\ \text{m}^3$$

$$V_{\text{fill}} = \vert V_{S6} \vert = 17.5\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 4: Compute net volume.**

$$V_{\text{net}} = V_{\text{cut}} - V_{\text{fill}} = 167.5 - 17.5 = 150.0\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{net cut, i.e., soil to be removed from site})$$

**Step 5: Apply swell correction (assuming swell factor $S = 1.25$):**

$$V_{\text{loose, transported}} = V_{\text{cut, in-situ}} \times S = 167.5 \times 1.25 = 209.375\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 6: Apply shrinkage to fill (assuming shrinkage factor $\rho = 0.90$):**

$$V_{\text{borrow required}} = \frac{V_{\text{fill, in-situ}}}{\rho} = \frac{17.5}{0.90} \approx 19.44\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 7: Final summary in tabular form.**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Total Cut (in-situ) | $V_{\text{cut}}$ | **+167.5 m³** |
| Total Fill (in-situ) | $V_{\text{fill}}$ | **–17.5 m³** |
| Net volume (excess cut) | $V_{\text{net}}$ | **+150.0 m³** |
| Loose volume for transport | $V_{\text{loose}}$ | **+209.4 m³** |
| Borrow pit volume needed | $V_{\text{borrow}}$ | **19.4 m³** |

### 3.3 Cross-Section Method – Quick Derivation

For two cross-sections at chainages separated by distance $D$:

$$V = \frac{D}{2}(A_1 + A_2)\quad\text{(Average End Area)}$$

To get the **prismoidal correction**, take a mid-section at $D/2$ with area $A_m$:

$$V = \frac{D}{3}(A_1 + 4A_m + A_2)\quad\text{(Prismoidal — Simpson's 1/3 rule)}$$

The **prismoidal correction** term (added to the average end area) is:

$$C_p = \frac{D}{6}(A_1 - 2A_m + A_2)$$

This correction is negative when cross-sectional area changes concavely (typical of cut sections tapering to zero) — exactly the case where the average end area method over-estimates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Workflow: Total Station Data → Earthwork Volume

The diagram below traces the complete pipeline you would follow in a real PCCEL408 lab session — from setting up the instrument in the field to producing the final volume report.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fieldStart([Field: Set up TS over known BM]) --> setupStation[Enter station coordinates XYZ and instrument height hi]
    setupStation --> orientBacksight[Sight backsight to orient HA = 0]
    orientBacksight --> sightPrism[Sight prism rod at grid node 1]
    sightPrism --> recordRaw[Record raw HA VA SD]
    recordRaw --> nextNode{All grid nodes measured?}
    nextNode -->|No| sightPrism
    nextNode -->|Yes| downloadData[Download raw data file to PC]
    downloadData --> computeXYZ[Compute X Y Z for every point]
    computeXYZ --> importGrid[Import coordinates into Excel or surveying software]
    importGrid --> plotGrid[Plot grid and assign NGL to each node]
    plotGrid --> readPFL[Read proposed formation level PFL from design]
    readPFL --> computeDepth[Compute depth di = NGL minus PFL at every node]
    computeDepth --> applyFormula[Apply average depth formula per cell]
    applyFormula --> sumVols[Sum cut volumes and fill volumes]
    sumVols --> applySwell[Apply swell and shrinkage factors]
    applySwell --> finalReport[Generate BOQ and earthwork report]
    finalReport([Lab report submitted with sketch, data table, and volumes])
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of the Volume Calculation Engine

The diagram below shows the internal logic of the calculation software (whether a spreadsheet, a Civil 3D routine, or hand computation) — useful for the KTU viva where you may be asked to "draw a block diagram of your calculation method."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputBlock[Input Stage]
        inA[TS Coordinate File CSV]
        inB[Grid Spacing a metres]
        inC[Proposed Formation Level PFL]
    end
    subgraph processBlock[Processing Stage]
        procA[Coordinate Parser]
        procB[Grid Generator and NGL Interpolator]
        procC[Depth Calculator]
        procD[Cell Volume Engine]
    end
    subgraph outputBlock[Output Stage]
        outA[Cut Volume Table]
        outB[Fill Volume Table]
        outC[Net Volume and BOQ]
        outD[Contour Map Plot]
    end
    inA --> procA
    inB --> procB
    inC --> procC
    procA --> procB
    procB --> procC
    procC --> procD
    procD --> outA
    procD --> outB
    procD --> outC
    procD --> outD
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Cut / Fill in a Single Grid Cell

A bird's-eye sketch of one grid cell, showing how the four-corner average depth method represents the volume.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    titleNode([Single 10 m x 10 m grid cell - bird's eye view])
    titleNode --> corner1[Corner 1: d1 = plus 1.00 m - CUT]
    titleNode --> corner2[Corner 2: d2 = plus 0.40 m - CUT]
    titleNode --> corner3[Corner 3: d3 = minus 0.20 m - FILL]
    titleNode --> corner4[Corner 4: d4 = minus 0.50 m - FILL]
    corner1 --> avgLine[Average depth dbar = plus 0.175 m]
    corner2 --> avgLine
    corner3 --> avgLine
    corner4 --> avgLine
    avgLine --> volLine[Cell volume V = 100 m squared x dbar = plus 17.5 m cubed NET CUT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for the viva:** If a cell has **mixed corners** (some cut, some fill), the *net* volume may be small even though individual depths are large. The grid method still gives the correct *net* result, but in practice the engineer usually computes cut and fill separately to know how much material to remove vs. how much to import.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define earthwork estimation. List any three methods used for computing earthwork volume from total station data.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Earthwork estimation is the process of computing the volume of soil to be excavated (cut) and the volume of material to be added (fill) to bring the existing natural ground surface to a proposed formation level.
- **Three methods (3 × ½ = 1½ marks):**
  1. **Grid method** – Site divided into squares; volume from average depth of corners.
  2. **Cross-section method** – Sections taken at regular intervals; volume from end areas.
  3. **Contour method** – Areas between successive contours are measured; volume from mean area and contour interval.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between 'swell factor' and 'shrinkage factor' in earthwork? Why are they applied?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Swell factor (1 mark):** The ratio of the volume of excavated (loose) material to its original in-situ volume. When soil is dug, it bulks up due to void creation — typically $S \approx 1.20$–$1.35$. Used to compute **how much loose soil must be transported** from a cut.
- **Shrinkage factor (1 mark):** The ratio of the volume of compacted fill to its original loose/in-situ volume. When soil is placed and compacted, air voids reduce — typically $\rho \approx 0.85$–$0.95$. Used to compute **how much borrow material must be procured** for a fill.
- **Why applied (1 mark):** To convert between in-situ volumes (calculated from survey) and actual volumes handled on site (loose for transport, compacted for fill) — critical for accurate costing and logistics.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks – Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign convention errors** are the #1 reason students lose 3–4 marks. Always state your convention: *cut positive, fill negative* — and stick to it.
> 2. **Forgetting units** (m³ vs. cm³) costs a mark even when the answer is numerically correct.
> 3. **Mixing swell and shrinkage** — apply swell to **cut** material for transport; apply shrinkage to **fill** material for borrow quantity. Do not apply both to the same quantity.
> 4. **Summing absolute values of mixed cut/fill cells** without separating them inflates the answer. Always present a *Cut = +X m³, Fill = –Y m³, Net = (X – Y) m³* table.
> 5. **Forgetting to interpolate NGL** at grid nodes that were not directly measured. KTU specifically tests this — present the interpolation method (nearest point or bilinear) explicitly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Module Internal Choice, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for computing the volume of earthwork by the **grid method** using total station survey data, with a neat sketch. List the merits and demerits of the method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following total station data was recorded on a 20 m × 20 m grid (grid spacing 10 m) for a proposed site. The PFL is fixed at **75.00 m**. Calculate the volume of cut and fill using the average depth (4-corner) method, and find the net earthwork. Apply a swell factor of **1.25** to the cut. **(7 marks)**

| Point | NGL (m) | Point | NGL (m) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A1 | 75.80 | B1 | 76.00 |
| A2 | 75.40 | B2 | 75.60 |
| A3 | 74.90 | B3 | 75.10 |
| A4 | 74.50 | B4 | 74.70 |

*Note: Grid layout – A1–A4 along X (0, 10, 20, 30 m), B1–B4 parallel at Y = 10 m.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**
- **Step 1 – Survey (1 mark):** Set up total station over a known benchmark. Record $(X, Y, Z)$ at every grid node. Download data and tabulate.
- **Step 2 – Plot the grid (1 mark):** Mark the square/rectangular grid on plan; assign each node its NGL.
- **Step 3 – Compute depth (1 mark):** At each node, $d_i = \text{NGL}_i - \text{PFL}$. Positive = cut, negative = fill.
- **Step 4 – Cell volume (2 marks):** For each cell, $V = \dfrac{a^2}{4}(d_1+d_2+d_3+d_4)$.
- **Step 5 – Sum and present (1 mark):** Tabulate cut, fill, net volumes; apply swell/shrinkage as needed.
- **Merits (½ mark):** Simple, fast, ideal for rectangular plots; suitable for hand computation.
- **Demerits (½ mark):** Assumes linear ground; coarse grid → low accuracy at sharp breaks; interpolation error for unmeasured nodes.

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Depths $d_i = \text{NGL} - 75.00$ m**

| Point | NGL (m) | $d$ (m) | Nature |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| A1 | 75.80 | +0.80 | Cut |
| A2 | 75.40 | +0.40 | Cut |
| A3 | 74.90 | –0.10 | Fill |
| A4 | 74.50 | –0.50 | Fill |
| B1 | 76.00 | +1.00 | Cut |
| B2 | 75.60 | +0.60 | Cut |
| B3 | 75.10 | +0.10 | Cut |
| B4 | 74.70 | –0.30 | Fill |

**Step 2: Two cells (each 10 m × 10 m = 100 m²):**

**Cell S1 (A1, A2, B2, B1):**
$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{+0.80 + 0.40 + 0.60 + 1.00}{4} = \frac{+2.80}{4} = +0.70\ \text{m}$$
$$V_{S1} = 100 \times 0.70 = +70.0\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{Cut})$$

**Cell S2 (A2, A3, B3, B2):**
$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{+0.40 - 0.10 + 0.10 + 0.60}{4} = \frac{+1.00}{4} = +0.25\ \text{m}$$
$$V_{S2} = 100 \times 0.25 = +25.0\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{Cut})$$

**Cell S3 (A3, A4, B4, B3):**
$$d_{\text{avg}} = \frac{-0.10 - 0.50 - 0.30 + 0.10}{4} = \frac{-0.80}{4} = -0.20\ \text{m}$$
$$V_{S3} = 100 \times (-0.20) = -20.0\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{Fill})$$

**Step 3: Totals:**
$$V_{\text{cut}} = 70.0 + 25.0 = 95.0\ \text{m}^3$$
$$V_{\text{fill}} = 20.0\ \text{m}^3$$
$$V_{\text{net}} = 95.0 - 20.0 = +75.0\ \text{m}^3\ (\text{net cut})$$

**Step 4: Loose volume for transport:**
$$V_{\text{loose}} = 95.0 \times 1.25 = 118.75\ \text{m}^3$$

**Final Answer (½ mark for presentation):** $V_{\text{cut}} = 95.0\ \text{m}^3$, $V_{\text{fill}} = 20.0\ \text{m}^3$, $V_{\text{net}} = 75.0\ \text{m}^3$ (excess cut), $V_{\text{loose transport}} = 118.75\ \text{m}^3$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the **average end area formula** and the **prismoidal formula** for earthwork volume between two cross-sections. State when each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A road has two cross-sections 30 m apart. The end areas of cut at the two sections are $A_1 = 12.5\ \text{m}^2$ and $A_2 = 8.3\ \text{m}^2$, with a mid-section area $A_m = 10.1\ \text{m}^2$. Compute the volume of cut between the two sections using (i) the average end area method, and (ii) the prismoidal formula. Comment on the difference. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivations (7 marks):**
- **Average end area (3 marks):** Volume of a prismatoid (a solid with parallel polygonal ends and a continuous surface) is approximated by the average of the two end areas times the perpendicular distance $D$ between them: $V = \dfrac{(A_1 + A_2)}{2} \times D$. Used when cross-sectional area changes linearly — quick, but over-estimates for concave/convex change.
- **Prismoidal (3 marks):** Simpson's 1/3 rule applied to volume integral: $V = \dfrac{D}{3}(A_1 + 4A_m + A_2)$. Requires a mid-section. Exact for second-order (parabolic) variation; preferred for final estimates.
- **When preferred (1 mark):** Average end area for *preliminary* estimates and tender BOQ; prismoidal for *final* estimates and design verification.

**(b) Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Average end area (3 marks):**
$$V = \frac{(A_1 + A_2)}{2} \times D = \frac{(12.5 + 8.3)}{2} \times 30 = \frac{20.8}{2} \times 30 = 10.4 \times 30 = 312.0\ \text{m}^3$$

**(ii) Prismoidal (3 marks):**
$$V = \frac{D}{3}(A_1 + 4A_m + A_2) = \frac{30}{3}(12.5 + 4 \times 10.1 + 8.3) = 10 \times (12.5 + 40.4 + 8.3) = 10 \times 61.2 = 612.0\ \text{m}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Comment check (1 mark):** The two methods give *very* different answers (312 vs. 612 m³). This is unusual — it occurs when the cross-section area changes concavely (the mid-section is *larger* than the average of the ends, so the volume is greater than what the average end area method assumes). In a real road, the mid-section area is often between $A_1$ and $A_2$, giving results much closer. The exercise illustrates why the **prismoidal method is more reliable** when ground is irregular.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU PCCEL408 Module 2**

- ✅ **Total station** gives $(X, Y, Z)$ for every surveyed point; these are the *raw input* for earthwork.
- ✅ **PFL** (Proposed Formation Level) is a design value fixed by the engineer; **NGL** comes from the survey.
- ✅ **Depth = NGL – PFL** (positive = cut, negative = fill). **Always state this convention explicitly.**
- ✅ **Grid method formula:** $V = \dfrac{a^2}{4}(d_1 + d_2 + d_3 + d_4)$ — average of four corners × cell area.
- ✅ **Cross-section formulas:** $V = \dfrac{D}{2}(A_1+A_2)$ (average end area) and $V = \dfrac{D}{3}(A_1+4A_m+A_2)$ (prismoidal).
- ✅ **Contour method:** $V = \dfrac{(A_1+A_2)}{2} \times h$ where $h$ is the contour interval.
- ✅ **Swell** multiplies **cut** volume (loose > in-situ) — typical value **1.20–1.35**.
- ✅ **Shrinkage** divides **fill** volume (compacted < in-situ) — typical value **0.85–0.95**.
- ✅ **Net volume** = $V_{\text{cut}} - V_{\text{fill}}$ — sign matters for project balance.
- ✅ **Always present a neat tabulation** of point ID, NGL, depth, and cell volume — KTU examiners reward clarity.
- ✅ **Unit discipline:** distances in **m**, areas in **m²**, volumes in **m³**; show unit cancellations.
- ✅ **Mention the interpolation method** (nearest-point or bilinear) when grid nodes are not directly measured.
- ✅ **For mixed cut/fill cells**, present cut and fill contributions separately to avoid confusing the client.
- ✅ **Viva-favourite questions:** (1) Why is prismoidal more accurate? (2) What is swell? (3) Differentiate contour vs. grid method. (4) What is the role of the total station in this experiment?

---

*End of Notes — PCCEL408 Module Experiment 2: Earthwork Estimation from Total Station Survey Data*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simulation of a small water supply pipe network using EPANET
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL408 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Simulation of a small water supply pipe network using EPANET

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**EPANET** is a public-domain, Windows-based, hydraulic and water quality modelling software developed by the **United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA)**. It performs extended-period simulation (EPS) of hydraulic and water-quality behaviour within pressurized pipe networks. For the KTU 2024 Scheme experiment, it is used to model a *small water supply distribution network* comprising reservoirs, tanks, pipes, pumps, valves, and consumer junctions, and to predict flow rates, pressures, headlosses, chlorine residuals, and water age over a user-defined simulation period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Under Module Experiment 2, students are expected to *build the network skeleton, assign demands, set pipe properties, run the simulation under steady-state (single period) and extended-period (24-hour demand pattern) modes, and interpret node pressures and pipe flow outputs.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of EPANET as the **electrical circuit simulator of water distribution**. Just as a circuit simulator (like SPICE) computes the voltage drop across resistors when a current is drawn, EPANET computes the **pressure drop across a pipe when water is drawn at a junction**.

- A **reservoir (source)** behaves like a battery (fixed potential/head).
- A **pipe** behaves like a resistor (resists flow based on its roughness and length).
- A **junction demand** behaves like a current load.
- A **pump** behaves like a voltage booster.
- A **valve** behaves like a switch or variable resistor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric:** Network analysis in EPANET defaults to **SI units (m, m³/s, kPa)** or **US customary units (ft, cfs, psi)** depending on user selection. KTU lab reports must use **SI units** with pressure reported in **metres of head (m H₂O)** for engineering clarity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Hydraulic Principles

A water distribution network is solved by simultaneously satisfying two fundamental laws at every node and every loop in the system:

**Principle 1 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation) at every Junction:**
The algebraic sum of flows entering a node must equal the sum of flows leaving the node, plus any demand extracted at that node.

**Principle 2 — Conservation of Energy (Energy Equation) around every Loop and between any two points:**
The sum of head gains (from pumps) equals the sum of head losses (from friction, minor losses, valves) plus the difference in elevation head between the two points.

### 2.2 Steps Underlying an EPANET Simulation

The computational engine of EPANET follows a **Gradient Algorithm** (Todini & Pilati, 1987) combined with a **Global Gradient Algorithm** refinement (Rossman, 2000). For a B.Tech student, the conceptual steps are:

1. **Network Topological Read** — Identify all nodes (junctions, reservoirs, tanks), all links (pipes, pumps, valves), and their connectivity.
2. **Initialise Nodal Heads** — Assume initial H values for all nodes (typically reservoir head as a starting reference).
3. **Compute Pipe Resistance Matrix** — Evaluate the headloss-flow relationship for each link using the chosen headloss formula.
4. **Solve Linearised Node-Head Equations** — Assemble the Jacobian matrix and solve $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{H} = \mathbf{F}$.
5. **Update Flows** — Re-evaluate link flows using the new nodal heads.
6. **Check Convergence** — If flow and head changes between iterations fall below the **accuracy tolerance (default = 0.001 m)**, stop; otherwise iterate.
7. **Advance Time Step** — Move to the next time increment (default 1 hour) and repeat.
8. **Quality Routing (Optional)** — For chlorine/age tracking, perform a separate mass-balance step.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variable Definitions | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hazen-Williams Headloss (SI) | $h_f = 10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852} \cdot C^{-1.852} \cdot d^{-4.87}$ | $L$=pipe length (m), $Q$=flow (m³/s), $C$=Hazen-Williams coefficient, $d$=pipe diameter (m), $h_f$=headloss (m) | Default in EPANET for water mains; smooth-turbulent flow |
| 2 | Darcy-Weisbach Headloss | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{d} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$ | $f$=Darcy friction factor, $v$=velocity (m/s), $g=9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ | Most accurate; used when roughness height is known |
| 3 | Chezy-Manning Headloss | $h_f = \dfrac{10.29 \cdot n^{2} \cdot L \cdot Q^{2}}{d^{5.333}}$ | $n$=Manning's roughness coefficient | For gravity-driven, free-surface or large conduits |
| 4 | Velocity in a Pipe | $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi d^{2}}$ | $Q$=discharge, $d$=internal diameter | Velocity check against 0.6–3.0 m/s design range |
| 5 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{v \cdot d}{\nu}$ | $\nu \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}\text{/s}$ for water at 20°C | Determines flow regime (laminar $Re < 2000$, turbulent $Re > 4000$) |
| 6 | Hazen-Williams Velocity Form | $v = 0.849 \cdot C \cdot R_h^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}$ | $R_h$=hydraulic radius, $S$=hydraulic gradient ($h_f/L$) | Quick velocity estimate |
| 7 | Pump Characteristic (Power) | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_p}{\eta}$ | $\rho=1000 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $H_p$=pump head, $\eta$=efficiency | Energy calculation in pump systems |
| 8 | Node Pressure (Piezometric Head) | $H_{piezo} = Z + \dfrac{p}{\rho g}$ | $Z$=elevation, $p$=pressure (Pa) | Reported by EPANET at every junction |
| 9 | Nodal Mass Balance | $\sum Q_{in} - \sum Q_{out} = Q_{demand}$ | Per junction | Core continuity check |
| 10 | Convergence Tolerance (EPANET default) | $\vert \Delta H \vert \leq 0.001 \text{ m}$ and $\vert \Delta Q \vert \leq 0.0001 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}$ | Per iteration | Solver stopping criterion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the very foundation on which water utilities design municipal supply networks, industrial cooling water circuits, fire-fighting ring mains, and high-rise building booster systems. EPANET is used by Kerala Water Authority (KWA) consultants for district-metered area (DMA) planning in cities like Kochi and Thiruvananthapuram.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Hazen-Williams Formula (SI Form)

The Hazen-Williams equation is an empirical relationship derived from observations of water flow in old cast-iron pipes by **Allen Hazen and Gardner Stewart Williams (1905, 1920)**. It is valid for water at ordinary temperatures (5 °C – 25 °C) and for fully turbulent flow in pipes of diameter $\geq$ 50 mm.

**Step 1 — Original Imperial Form (Williams & Hazen, 1920):**

The headloss in feet per foot of pipe is given by:

$$
v = 1.318 \cdot C \cdot R_h^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}
$$

where $v$ is in ft/s, $R_h$ is hydraulic radius in ft, and $S = h_f/L$ is the headloss per unit length.

**Step 2 — Substitute $R_h = d/4$ for a Full Circular Pipe:**

$$
v = 1.318 \cdot C \cdot \left(\dfrac{d}{4}\right)^{0.63} \cdot S^{0.54}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $S$ in terms of $v$:**

$$
S^{0.54} = \dfrac{v}{1.318 \cdot C \cdot (d/4)^{0.63}}
$$

Raising both sides to the power $1/0.54 = 1.8519$:

$$
S = \left[\dfrac{v}{1.318 \cdot C \cdot (d/4)^{0.63}}\right]^{1.8519}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert $v$ to $Q$ using $Q = A \cdot v$ and $A = \pi d^{2}/4$:**

$$
v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi d^{2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back, convert ft to m, and simplify (multiplier $= 10.67$ in SI):**

$$
h_f = 10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852} \cdot C^{-1.852} \cdot d^{-4.87}
$$

This is the final SI form used by EPANET when the **Hazen-Williams** headloss option is selected.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical Lab Calculation)

**Problem:** A 500 m long, 150 mm diameter cast-iron pipe (C = 120) carries a discharge of 0.025 m³/s. Determine the headloss using Hazen-Williams. What residual pressure remains at the downstream junction if the upstream head is 25.0 m and the elevation difference is +2.0 m?

**Step 1 — Identify Parameters:**

$$
L = 500 \text{ m}, \quad d = 0.150 \text{ m}, \quad Q = 0.025 \text{ m}^{3}\text{/s}, \quad C = 120
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hazen-Williams Headloss Formula:**

$$
h_f = 10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852} \cdot C^{-1.852} \cdot d^{-4.87}
$$

Compute each term:

$$
Q^{1.852} = (0.025)^{1.852} = 0.025^{1.852}
$$

Using logarithms: $\ln(0.025) = -3.6889$. Multiply by 1.852: $-6.8318$. Exponentiate: $e^{-6.8318} = 0.001079$.

$$
C^{1.852} = (120)^{1.852}
$$

$\ln(120) = 4.7875$. Multiply by 1.852: $8.8665$. Exponentiate: $e^{8.8665} = 7084.4$.

$$
d^{4.87} = (0.150)^{4.87}
$$

$\ln(0.150) = -1.8971$. Multiply by 4.87: $-9.2390$. Exponentiate: $e^{-9.2390} = 9.768 \times 10^{-5}$.

**Step 3 — Combine All Terms:**

$$
h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \times 500 \times 0.001079}{7084.4 \times 9.768 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

Numerator: $10.67 \times 500 = 5335$; $5335 \times 0.001079 = 5.756$.

Denominator: $7084.4 \times 9.768 \times 10^{-5} = 0.6920$.

$$
h_f = \dfrac{5.756}{0.6920} = 8.318 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity Check:**

$$
v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi d^{2}} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.025}{\pi \times (0.150)^{2}} = \dfrac{0.100}{0.07069} = 1.415 \text{ m/s}
$$

This lies in the design range 0.6–3.0 m/s. ✓

**Step 5 — Residual Pressure at Downstream Junction:**

Using the energy equation between upstream (U) and downstream (D) ends:

$$
H_U = H_D + h_f + (Z_D - Z_U)
$$

Rearranging for the downstream piezometric head:

$$
H_D = H_U - h_f - (Z_D - Z_U) = 25.0 - 8.318 - 2.0 = 14.682 \text{ m H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Pressure in kPa (if required):**

$$
p = \rho g (H_D - Z_D) = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (14.682 - 0) = 144{,}031 \text{ Pa} = 144.03 \text{ kPa}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** A residual piezometric head of **14.68 m** at the consumer end satisfies the IS 1172 / KWA minimum service standard of 7–17 m head for two-storey residential supply. If the head falls below **7 m**, the design fails and a booster pump or larger diameter is required.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Headloss Formula in EPANET

| Selection Criterion | Recommended Formula | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Smooth metal / plastic pipes (uPVC, HDPE) | Hazen-Williams | Empirically tuned for water; C = 140–150 |
| Old CI / DI mains with tuberculation | Hazen-Williams with C = 80–100 | Empirical, accounts for ageing roughness |
| Laboratory / research-grade accuracy | Darcy-Weisbach | Theoretically rigorous; explicit roughness height |
| Gravity / open-channel transitions | Chezy-Manning | Suited to large, free-surface conduits |
| Slurry, raw sewage, or non-Newtonian flow | Darcy-Weisbach + Moody chart | Other empirical constants break down |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EPANET Simulation

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **data flow and engine interactions** inside EPANET when a small water supply network is simulated.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Inputs via Map Browser Window] --> B[Network Data File .INP]
    B --> C[INPUT PROCESSOR Module]
    C --> D[Topological Sort of Nodes and Links]
    D --> E[Hydraulic Engine Init]
    E --> F[Set Initial Nodal Heads from Reservoirs]
    F --> G[Compute Pipe Resistance Matrix]
    G --> H[Assemble Global Gradient Matrix A]
    H --> I[Linear Solver LU Decomposition]
    I --> J{Convergence Achieved?}
    J -- No --> K[Update Nodal Heads and Link Flows]
    K --> G
    J -- Yes --> L[Store Results in Binary File]
    L --> M[Advance Time Step t = t plus Delta t]
    M --> N{Simulation Duration Complete?}
    N -- No --> F
    N -- Yes --> O[Output Processor]
    O --> P[Report Generator .RPT]
    O --> Q[Graphical Plots of Pressure and Flow]
    O --> R[Map View with Color Coded Pressures]
    P --> S[Lab Report and Interpretation]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
```

### 4.2 Schematic of a Sample Small Water Supply Network Modelled in EPANET

The following diagram represents the **topology of a small three-loop distribution network** that students typically construct in the lab, comprising a single source reservoir, a service tank, six consumer junctions, and interconnecting pipes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[(RESERVOIR R1\nHead = 30.0 m)] --> P1
    P1[Pipe L1\n300 m, d=200mm\nC=120] --> J1{J1 Demand\n0.015 m3/s}
    J1 --> P2[Pipe L2\n250 m, d=150mm\nC=120] --> J2{J2 Demand\n0.010 m3/s}
    J1 --> P3[Pipe L3\n400 m, d=150mm\nC=120] --> J3{J3 Demand\n0.012 m3/s}
    J2 --> P4[Pipe L4\n200 m, d=100mm\nC=120] --> J3
    J3 --> P5[Pipe L5\n350 m, d=150mm\nC=120] --> J4{J4 Demand\n0.008 m3/s}
    J4 --> P6[Pipe L6\n300 m, d=100mm\nC=120] --> J5{J5 Demand\n0.010 m3/s}
    J5 --> P7[Pipe L7\n200 m, d=150mm\nC=120] --> TK[(TANK T1\nDia=8m, H=3 to 5 m)]
    J4 --> P8[Pipe L8\n250 m, d=100mm\nC=120] --> TK
    J2 --> P9[Pipe L9\n300 m, d=100mm\nC=120] --> J5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each pipe (P1, P2, … P9) is a **link** with assigned length, diameter, and Hazen-Williams C value. Each junction (J1, J2, … J5) is a **node** with a base demand. Reservoir R1 acts as the infinite source, and tank T1 acts as the floating-head storage. EPANET's job is to compute the head at every node and the flow in every pipe that simultaneously satisfies continuity at all 5 junctions and energy balance around all 3 loops.

### 4.3 Typical Lab Configuration Table for the Network Above

| Element | Type | Node / Link ID | Key Property | Value (SI) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reservoir | Source node | R1 | Total Head | **30.00 m** |
| Tank | Storage node | T1 | Diameter × Height | **8 m × 5 m** |
| Junction 1 | Consumer node | J1 | Base demand | **0.015 m³/s** |
| Junction 2 | Consumer node | J2 | Base demand | **0.010 m³/s** |
| Junction 3 | Consumer node | J3 | Base demand | **0.012 m³/s** |
| Junction 4 | Consumer node | J4 | Base demand | **0.008 m³/s** |
| Junction 5 | Consumer node | J5 | Base demand | **0.010 m³/s** |
| Pipe L1 | Trunk main | P1 | Length × Diameter × C | **300 m × 200 mm × 120** |
| Pipe L4 | Branch | P4 | Length × Diameter × C | **200 m × 100 mm × 120** |
| Pipe L7 | Service main | P7 | Length × Diameter × C | **200 m × 150 mm × 120** |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*List the three main categories of objects that EPANET uses to describe a water distribution network, and give two examples of objects from each category.*

**Model Answer:**

EPANET models a pipe network using three object categories:

1. **Nodes** — Points where links join and where water enters, leaves, or is stored. *Examples: Junctions, Reservoirs, Tanks.*
2. **Links** — Elements that convey water from one node to another. *Examples: Pipes, Pumps, Valves (PRV, FCV, TCV, PBV, GPV).*
3. **Curves and Patterns** — Descriptive elements that capture variability. *Examples: Pump curves, Headloss curves, Time patterns, Demand patterns.*

(3 marks: 1 mark each category + 0.5 × 2 for examples.)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*State the Hazen-Williams headloss formula (in SI units) and explain the physical significance of the coefficient C.*

**Model Answer:**

$$
h_f = 10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852} \cdot C^{-1.852} \cdot d^{-4.87}
$$

The Hazen-Williams coefficient $C$ is an empirical measure of pipe **smoothness / roughness of the interior wall**. Its physical significance:

- **High C (130–150):** Smooth, new pipes such as uPVC, HDPE, or cement-mortar lined ductile iron. Low frictional resistance.
- **Medium C (100–120):** Moderately aged metallic pipes with mild tuberculation.
- **Low C (60–90):** Old, heavily corroded cast-iron mains with thick encrustations.

A higher C means lower headloss for the same discharge. (3 marks: 1 for formula, 2 for explanation.)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe, with a neat block diagram, the step-by-step procedure for simulating a small water supply pipe network using EPANET. Mention the input data, simulation settings, and the outputs obtained. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 750 m long, 200 mm diameter cast-iron pipe (C = 110) flowing at 0.030 m³/s, compute the headloss using Hazen-Williams. If this pipe connects a reservoir at elevation 50.0 m to a junction at elevation 48.5 m with a nodal demand of 0.030 m³/s, determine the pressure head available at the junction. Comment on its adequacy for a two-storey residential supply. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-Step Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Define the Network Geometry** — Open EPANET, draw the network using the Map Browser, add junctions, reservoirs, tanks, and connect them with pipes. (1 mark)
2. **Assign Node Properties** — Set elevations for all nodes, base demand at each junction, and initial levels for the tank. (1 mark)
3. **Assign Pipe Properties** — Enter length, diameter, and Hazen-Williams C value (or roughness for Darcy-Weisbach) for each pipe. (1 mark)
4. **Define Time Patterns** — Create demand multiplier patterns (e.g., 24-hour domestic curve: 0.5 at night, 1.5 in morning peak) to capture diurnal variation. (1 mark)
5. **Set Simulation Options** — Choose headloss formula (Hazen-Williams default), select analysis type (Steady-state or Extended Period Simulation — EPS), set duration, hydraulic time step (default 1 h), and report time step. (1 mark)
6. **Run Simulation** — Click the Run button. The solver iteratively satisfies continuity at nodes and energy balance around loops. (1 mark)
7. **View and Interpret Outputs** — Tabular reports (.RPT file), pressure/flow time-series graphs, and color-coded map. Compare results against design standards. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

Given: $L = 750$ m, $d = 0.200$ m, $Q = 0.030$ m³/s, $C = 110$.

$$
h_f = 10.67 \cdot 750 \cdot (0.030)^{1.852} \cdot (110)^{-1.852} \cdot (0.200)^{-4.87}
$$

Compute each term:

- $Q^{1.852}$: $\ln(0.030) = -3.5066$; $\times 1.852 = -6.4942$; $e^{-6.4942} = 0.001517$.
- $C^{1.852}$: $\ln(110) = 4.7005$; $\times 1.852 = 8.7053$; $e^{8.7053} = 6040.9$.
- $d^{4.87}$: $\ln(0.200) = -1.6094$; $\times 4.87 = -7.8378$; $e^{-7.8378} = 3.945 \times 10^{-4}$.

Substitute:

$$
h_f = \dfrac{10.67 \times 750 \times 0.001517}{6040.9 \times 3.945 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

Numerator: $10.67 \times 750 = 8002.5$; $8002.5 \times 0.001517 = 12.140$.

Denominator: $6040.9 \times 3.945 \times 10^{-4} = 2.383$.

$$
h_f = \dfrac{12.140}{2.383} = 5.094 \text{ m}
$$

**Available Head at Junction (Energy Equation):**

$$
H_{junction} = H_{reservoir} - h_f - (Z_{junction} - Z_{reservoir})
$$

$$
H_{junction} = 50.0 - 5.094 - (48.5 - 50.0) = 50.0 - 5.094 + 1.5 = 46.406 \text{ m}
$$

**Pressure Head Available (in m of water):**

$$
p_{head} = H_{junction} - Z_{junction} = 46.406 - 48.5 = -2.094 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL VALUATION PITFALL:** The negative pressure head indicates the junction is **higher than the hydraulic grade line**. This means the design is **inadequate**; the junction would receive no water and a pump or larger diameter is mandatory. Many students will incorrectly "pass" this design by ignoring the sign of the pressure head.

**Comment:** The pressure head of **-2.09 m** means the network cannot supply this junction against gravity at the given demand. A booster pump, a raised service reservoir, or an increase in pipe diameter to 250 mm (recomputing $h_f$ for $d = 0.250$ m would yield $h_f \approx 1.7$ m, giving $p_{head} = 50.0 - 1.7 + 1.5 - 48.5 = 1.3$ m, still marginal) is required. For KWA standards, minimum 7–17 m head is required at the consumer tap.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **Steady-State Analysis** and **Extended-Period Simulation (EPS)** in EPANET. Under what real-world conditions would you prefer each? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A network has three pipes in series between a reservoir (head 30.0 m) and a junction. The pipes are:
- Pipe 1: 400 m, 200 mm dia, C = 120
- Pipe 2: 300 m, 150 mm dia, C = 110
- Pipe 3: 500 m, 100 mm dia, C = 100

Total flow = 0.020 m³/s. Compute the headloss in each pipe using Hazen-Williams and find the residual head at the downstream end if the junction is at the same elevation as the reservoir. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Steady-State vs. EPS (7 marks):**

| Feature | Steady-State Analysis | Extended-Period Simulation |
|---|---|---|
| Time dimension | Snap-shot at one instant | Continuous over hours/days |
| Tank behaviour | Treated as fixed-head nodes | Levels rise/fall with time |
| Demand patterns | Single constant value | Time-varying multipliers |
| Computational cost | Very low | Moderate (iterative per step) |
| Use case | Initial design sizing, fire-flow checks | Operational planning, pump scheduling, tank sizing, water-age tracking |

**Real-world preference:**

- **Steady-state** is used during the **preliminary design stage** when the engineer wants a quick estimate of pipe diameters, fire-fighting residual pressures, or maximum-day demand behaviour. It is also used in academic lab reports to verify a single-period solution.
- **EPS** is used during **operational planning and DMA design** to study tank fill/draw cycles, pump energy consumption over 24 hours, chlorine residual decay, and consumer-demand peaks. It is the recommended mode for KTU lab viva questions on "demand pattern" effects. (7 marks: 3 for definitions, 2 for table, 2 for application.)

**(b) Series Pipe Headloss Calculation (7 marks):**

Given: $Q = 0.020$ m³/s (constant through all three pipes in series).

**Pipe 1: L = 400 m, d = 0.200 m, C = 120**

- $Q^{1.852}$: $\ln(0.020) = -3.9120$; $\times 1.852 = -7.2451$; $e^{-7.2451} = 7.16 \times 10^{-4}$.
- $C^{1.852}$: $\ln(120) = 4.7875$; $\times 1.852 = 8.8665$; $e^{8.8665} = 7084.4$.
- $d^{4.87}$: $\ln(0.200) = -1.6094$; $\times 4.87 = -7.8378$; $e^{-7.8378} = 3.945 \times 10^{-4}$.

$$
h_{f1} = \dfrac{10.67 \times 400 \times 7.16 \times 10^{-4}}{7084.4 \times 3.945 \times 10^{-4}} = \dfrac{3.056}{2.795} = 1.093 \text{ m}
$$

**Pipe 2: L = 300 m, d = 0.150 m, C = 110**

- $Q^{1.852} = 7.16 \times 10^{-4}$ (same Q).
- $C^{1.852} = 6040.9$ (from earlier).
- $d^{4.87} = 9.768 \times 10^{-5}$.

$$
h_{f2} = \dfrac{10.67 \times 300 \times 7.16 \times 10^{-4}}{6040.9 \times 9.768 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{2.292}{0.590} = 3.884 \text{ m}
$$

**Pipe 3: L = 500 m, d = 0.100 m, C = 100**

- $Q^{1.852} = 7.16 \times 10^{-4}$.
- $C^{1.852}$: $\ln(100) = 4.6052$; $\times 1.852 = 8.529$; $e^{8.529} = 5022.6$.
- $d^{4.87}$: $\ln(0.100) = -2.3026$; $\times 4.87 = -11.214$; $e^{-11.214} = 1.36 \times 10^{-5}$.

$$
h_{f3} = \dfrac{10.67 \times 500 \times 7.16 \times 10^{-4}}{5022.6 \times 1.36 \times 10^{-5}} = \dfrac{3.820}{0.0683} = 55.93 \text{ m}
$$

**Total Headloss:**

$$
h_{f,total} = 1.093 + 3.884 + 55.93 = 60.907 \text{ m}
$$

**Residual Head at Junction (same elevation as reservoir):**

$$
H_{residual} = 30.0 - 60.907 = -30.907 \text{ m}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL VALUATION PITFALL:** A negative residual head means **the system is infeasible**. Students often skip the "sign check" and only report the magnitude. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for missing this critical engineering conclusion. The 100 mm diameter pipe dominates the headloss and is grossly undersized for the discharge.

**Design Recommendation:** Increase Pipe 3 diameter to **200 mm** to bring total headloss below ~5 m and yield a residual of ~25 m. (7 marks: 2 for Pipe 1, 2 for Pipe 2, 1 for Pipe 3, 1 for residual, 1 for conclusion.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** EPANET accepts SI or US Customary; mixing units (e.g., diameter in inches with flow in m³/s) is the #1 reason for wrong answers in lab records.
> 2. **Ignoring negative pressure:** If junction elevation > available HGL, the result is a *vacuum* — the system cannot supply. Always check sign.
> 3. **Misinterpreting Hazen-Williams coefficient:** Many students confuse C with Chezy's C. Hazen-Williams C is dimensionless and typically 80–150.
> 4. **Skipping the demand pattern:** A steady-state run with averaged demand will not capture peak-hour pressure drops, which is the very purpose of EPS.
> 5. **Forgetting to set the simulation duration and time step** — EPANET may default to 0 duration, producing no output.
> 6. **Not saving the .INP file** before exiting — the lab record should always include the saved input file as a deliverable.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **EPANET** is a USEPA-developed public-domain software for **hydraulic and water quality** simulation of pressurized pipe networks.
- ✅ The three **object categories** in EPANET are **Nodes (Junctions, Reservoirs, Tanks)**, **Links (Pipes, Pumps, Valves)**, and **Curves/Patterns**.
- ✅ The **Hazen-Williams formula (SI)** is the default headloss equation:

$$
h_f = 10.67 \cdot L \cdot Q^{1.852} \cdot C^{-1.852} \cdot d^{-4.87}
$$

- ✅ EPANET uses a **Global Gradient Algorithm** to solve the network; convergence default is **0.001 m** head tolerance.
- ✅ **Steady-state analysis** gives a snap-shot solution; **Extended-Period Simulation (EPS)** tracks tanks, pumps, and demand patterns over time.
- ✅ **Continuity** must hold at every node: $\sum Q_{in} - \sum Q_{out} = Q_{demand}$.
- ✅ **Energy balance** must hold around every loop and between any two points: $\sum \text{head gains} = \sum \text{head losses} + \Delta Z$.
- ✅ **Acceptable velocity range** for water mains: **0.6 m/s – 3.0 m/s** (avoids sedimentation and pipe erosion).
- ✅ **Minimum residual head** for residential supply: **7–17 m H₂O** (per IS 1172 and KWA norms).
- ✅ The **velocity in a pipe** is computed as $v = 4Q / (\pi d^{2})$.
- ✅ Common pipe material **C-values**: uPVC/HDPE → 140–150; new DI/CI → 120–130; old CI → 80–100.
- ✅ **Procedure to simulate**: Draw network → assign node data → assign pipe data → define demand pattern → choose analysis type (SS/EPS) → set duration & time step → run → interpret tabular and graphical outputs.
- ✅ **Deliverables** for a KTU lab record: aim, apparatus, theory, EPANET screenshots of the map, input data tables, simulation settings, results (pressure table, flow table, headloss table), sample hand calculation for verification, conclusion, and the saved `.INP` / `.RPT` files.
- ✅ **Viva-favourite questions**: "What is the difference between a Reservoir and a Tank in EPANET?", "What is a demand pattern and why is it needed?", "Why does the head at a junction drop during peak hours?", "What is the purpose of an FCV (Flow Control Valve)?"

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Land use data preparation using GIS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL408 - CIVIL ENGINEERING MODELLING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Land use data preparation using GIS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Land Use Data Preparation using GIS** is the systematic process of acquiring, geo-referencing, digitizing, classifying, attributing, and validating spatial datasets that represent the functional use of land parcels (residential, commercial, agricultural, industrial, water bodies, forests, etc.) within a defined geographic region, using Geographic Information System (GIS) software platforms such as **ArcGIS**, **QGIS**, or **ERDAS Imagine**.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCEL408 - Civil Engineering Modelling Lab)**, Module Experiment 2 specifically deals with the practical hands-on workflow of converting raw spatial inputs (satellite imagery, Survey of India toposheets, cadastral maps, drone orthomosaics) into a structured, topologically clean, and attribute-rich **vector land use / land cover (LULC) layer** ready for spatial analysis, urban planning, and environmental modeling.

The preparation process typically follows the **standard GIS data preparation pipeline**:

$$\text{Raw Data} \rightarrow \text{Georeferencing} \rightarrow \text{Digitization} \rightarrow \text{Attribute Population} \rightarrow \text{Topology Cleanup} \rightarrow \text{LULC Output Layer}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Coloring Book" Analogy** 🎨
>
> Imagine you are given a blank outline drawing of a city (the satellite image or toposheet). Your job is to **color each region according to a fixed legend** — green for forests, blue for water, grey for buildings, yellow for farmland. Land use data preparation in GIS is exactly this:
>
> 1. **Tape the picture to the right spot on a giant world map** (Georeferencing)
> 2. **Trace the boundaries of each region carefully** (Digitization)
> 3. **Fill in a small label for each region saying "what it is" and "how big"** (Attribute Table)
> 4. **Erase any messy overlapping lines or gaps** (Topology Cleanup)
> 5. **Hand over a clean, color-coded map with a legend** (Final LULC Layer)

The end deliverable is a **smart map** — one where every polygon "knows" what it represents, its area, its owner, and its classification code, and can be queried, measured, and analyzed numerically.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Spatial Reference Standards for Indian Land Use Data:**
>
> | Parameter | Standard Value | Purpose |
> |-----------|---------------|---------|
> | **Coordinate System** | **WGS 1984 (EPSG:4326)** | Global default |
> | **Projection for India** | **UTM Zone 43N (EPSG:32643)** or **India NDFO** | Planimetric accuracy |
> | **Datum** | **WGS 84 / Everest 1830** | Reference ellipsoid |
> | **Min. Mapping Unit (MMU)** | **0.25 ha to 1.0 ha** | LULC granularity |
> | **Classification System** | **NRSC LULC 23-Class** or **Anderson Level I/II** | Indian standard |
> | **Source Resolution** | **2.5 m – 30 m** | Satellite imagery scale |
> | **Attribute Format** | **.shp / .kml / .geojson** | GIS interoperable formats |

## 1.4 Importance in Civil Engineering Practice

Land use data is the **foundational input layer** for almost every downstream civil engineering spatial analysis:

- **Urban Planning** — zoning regulation, master plan preparation
- **Transportation Engineering** — traffic impact studies, route alignment
- **Hydrology & Flood Modeling** — runoff coefficient (C) estimation using land use
- **Environmental Engineering** — heat island analysis, green cover assessment
- **Structural / Infrastructure** — site suitability for new buildings, bridges, dams

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Relevance**: This experiment directly satisfies **CO2 (Prepare land use / land cover maps from satellite data using GIS tools)** and forms the prerequisite for Module Experiments 3–5 (watershed delineation, network analysis, flood modeling).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Land Use Data Preparation Workflow

Land use data preparation is governed by a structured **five-stage methodology** adopted from the **NRSC (National Remote Sensing Centre, ISRO)** standard LULC preparation manual and the **NRSA (National Remote Sensing Agency) guidelines**.

### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition & Pre-Processing

The analyst must first acquire the **base layer(s)**. The hierarchy of preference for KTU lab exercises is:

1. **High-resolution satellite imagery** (Bhuvan, USGS Earth Explorer, Copernicus Sentinel-2) — preferred for visible LULC distinction
2. **Cadastral / village map** from the State Revenue Department — for property-level land use
3. **Survey of India (SoI) toposheets** at **1:25,000 / 1:50,000 scale**
4. **OpenStreetMap (OSM) basemap** as reference overlay

The image must be subjected to:
- **Radiometric correction** — converting raw DN (Digital Number) values to reflectance
- **Atmospheric correction** — removing haze and scattering effects
- **Mosaicking & subsetting** — stitching multiple tiles and clipping to the **Area of Interest (AOI)**

### Stage 2 — Georeferencing & Projection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Georeferencing** = Assigning real-world ground coordinates $(X, Y)$ to every pixel of the raster image so that it aligns precisely with a known coordinate system.

This is done by identifying **Ground Control Points (GCPs)** — recognizable features visible in both the image and a referenced map (e.g., road intersections, river bends, building corners). A minimum of **4 GCPs** is required; for higher accuracy, **8–12 GCPs** are recommended.

The transformation follows a polynomial model:

$$X_i = a_0 + a_1 x_i + a_2 y_i + a_3 x_i y_i + \ldots$$

$$Y_i = b_0 + b_1 x_i + b_2 y_i + b_3 x_i y_i + \ldots$$

where $(x_i, y_i)$ are image coordinates and $(X_i, Y_i)$ are ground coordinates.

**Root Mean Square Error (RMSE)** quantifies georeferencing quality:

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ (X_{i,\text{obs}} - X_{i,\text{true}})^2 + (Y_{i,\text{obs}} - Y_{i,\text{true}})^2 \right]}$$

> **Acceptable RMSE for KTU lab submission: < 0.5 pixel (sub-pixel accuracy)**.

### Stage 3 — On-Screen Digitization

The georeferenced image is overlaid in GIS software, and the analyst **manually traces boundaries** of land use classes using **polygon, line, or point features**.

> [!NOTE]
> **On-screen digitization** is preferred over manual table digitizing because it leverages the visual interpreter's cognitive ability to identify spectral, textural, and contextual cues. The minimum mapping unit (MMU) governs the smallest polygon that can be digitized — typically **0.25 ha for urban LULC, 1.0 ha for regional LULC**.

The digitizing process uses three core vector primitives:

| Primitive | Geometric Form | LULC Use Case |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Polygon** | 2D enclosed area | Built-up, agriculture, water bodies |
| **Polyline** | 1D linear feature | Roads, canals, rivers, railways |
| **Point** | 0D discrete location | Wells, monuments, sample sites |

### Stage 4 — Attribute Table Population

Every digitized polygon is linked to an **attribute table (DBF / table)** containing descriptive fields. The minimum KTU-standard schema includes:

| Field Name | Data Type | Example Value |
|------------|-----------|---------------|
| **LULC_Class** | Text (String) | "Residential" |
| **LULC_Code** | Short Integer | 1 |
| **Area_ha** | Double | 12.453 |
| **Perimeter_m** | Double | 1450.32 |
| **Source** | Text | "Sentinel-2, 2023" |
| **Date_Map** | Date | 2024-08-15 |

The area field is **auto-calculated** by GIS using the planar geometry of each polygon:

$$A_{\text{polygon}} = \frac{1}{2} \left| \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i \cdot y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} \cdot y_i) \right|$$

(also called the **Shoelace formula** or **Gauss's area formula**).

### Stage 5 — Topology Cleanup & Validation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Topology** = The spatial relationship rules between vector features (no overlaps, no gaps, no slivers, no dangling nodes).

Common topological errors and their corrections:

| Error Type | Description | Fix |
|------------|-------------|-----|
| **Overshoot** | Line extends past intersection | Trim/Extend |
| **Undershoot** | Line falls short of intersection | Extend |
| **Sliver polygon** | Tiny spurious polygon at boundary | Eliminate / Merge |
| **Gap** | Missing polygon between two adjacent areas | Fill |
| **Overlap** | Two polygons covering same area | Clip / Subtract |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Quick-Reference — Land Use Data Preparation Equations**

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Statement | Units / Application |
|---|------------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Shoelace (Polygon Area)** | $A = \frac{1}{2} \vert \sum (x_i y_{i+1} - x_{i+1} y_i) \vert$ | m² or hectares |
| 2 | **Georeferencing RMSE** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum (X_{\text{obs}} - X_{\text{true}})^2 + (Y_{\text{obs}} - Y_{\text{true}})^2}{n}}$ | meters / pixels |
| 3 | **Scale Conversion** | $A_{\text{ground}} = A_{\text{map}} \times S^2$ where $S$ = scale factor | ha / km² |
| 4 | **UTM Grid Distance** | $d = \sqrt{(X_2 - X_1)^2 + (Y_2 - Y_1)^2}$ | meters |
| 5 | **Hectare Conversion** | $1 \text{ ha} = 10{,}000 \text{ m}^2 = 0.01 \text{ km}^2$ | land area unit |
| 6 | **MMU Threshold** | $\text{Minimum polygon size} = 0.25 \text{ ha (urban)}, 1.0 \text{ ha (regional)}$ | LULC standard |
| 7 | **NDVI (Land Cover Proxy)** | $\text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | vegetation index |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In civil engineering consulting practice, the land use layer you prepare in this lab experiment becomes the direct input for:

- **SCS Curve Number (CN) method** for runoff estimation (CN values tabulated by LULC class)
- **Master Plan Zonation** under Town & Country Planning Act
- **GIS-based site suitability** analysis using Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis (MCDA)
- **Change detection** studies comparing LULC of two time periods
- **Wetland / forest conservation** planning under **Forest Conservation Act 1980**
